topics.title: System of Units and Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System of Units and Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "System of Units"?

A **System of Units** is a structured, internationally agreed-upon set of fundamental reference standards (called **base units**) from which all other physical quantities (called **derived units**) can be mathematically expressed through multiplication, division, or combination. In the context of **Bioprocess Calculations**, every reaction stoichiometry, every fermenter volume, every nutrient concentration, and every heat-transfer term must be reduced to a common numerical language before it can be plugged into a mass-balance, energy-balance, or rate equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1):**
> The student is expected to master the **International System of Units (SI)**, recognize the seven **base quantities**, derive the relevant **derived units** (especially those used in chemical and biological engineering), and execute flawless **unit conversion** using **dimensional analysis** and the **F-factor / unity bracket method**.

## 1.2 Historical Context — Why so Many Systems?

Before the modern SI system became universal in **1960** (adopted by the General Conference on Weights and Measures — CGPM), engineers and scientists used regionally dominant systems:

| System | Region of Origin | Base Length | Base Mass | Base Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CGS** (Centimetre-Gram-Second) | France (late 1700s) | centimetre (cm) | gram (g) | second (s) |
| **FPS** (Foot-Pound-Second) | British Empire | foot (ft) | pound (lb) | second (s) |
| **MKS** (Metre-Kilogram-Second) | France (post-Revolution) | metre (m) | kilogram (kg) | second (s) |
| **SI** (Système International) | International (1960) | metre (m) | kilogram (kg) | second (s) |

## 1.3 The Seven SI Base Quantities

The **SI system** is built on **seven** independent, dimensionally distinct base quantities. No base quantity can be derived from any combination of the others.

$$\text{Number of SI Base Quantities} = 7$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven SI Base Units (with their official SI symbols and definitions):**
> 1. **Length** — `metre (m)` — distance travelled by light in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second.
> 2. **Mass** — `kilogram (kg)` — defined via Planck's constant $h = 6.62607015 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{kg·m}^2\text{/s}$.
> 3. **Time** — `second (s)` — 9,192,631,770 cycles of radiation from the $^{133}\text{Cs}$ atom.
> 4. **Electric Current** — `ampere (A)` — defined via elementary charge $e$.
> 5. **Temperature** — `kelvin (K)` — defined via Boltzmann's constant $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$.
> 6. **Amount of Substance** — `mole (mol)` — exactly $6.02214076 \times 10^{23}$ elementary entities (Avogadro's number, $N_A$).
> 7. **Luminous Intensity** — `candela (cd)` — defined via luminous efficacy of monochromatic radiation of frequency $540 \times 10^{12}$ Hz.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Language"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (for a B.Tech student):**
> Imagine you walk into a kitchen in France, India, and the USA, and the recipes are written in three completely different languages with three different units of mass. You have **3 cups of flour (USA)**, **300 g of flour (France)**, and **1.5 *pav* of flour (India)**. To follow any recipe, you must first **convert** everything to **one common language** — the SI system is that universal recipe language for engineers.
>
> In a **bioreactor**, a microbial growth rate might be given as `0.5 h⁻¹`, the feed concentration as `40 g/L`, the tank volume as `5000 L`, and the impeller speed as `150 rpm`. To make these quantities "talk" to each other in a single mass-balance equation, every term **must be expressed in the same SI-derived units** (e.g., kg, m³, s). That is the practical job of "Unit Conversion".

## 1.5 Derived Units vs. Base Units

A **derived unit** is a unit formed by combining two or more base units through multiplication or division. The table below lists the derived units that recur in **Bioprocess Calculations**:

| Derived Quantity | SI Derived Unit | Symbol | Expression in SI Base Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Force | newton | N | $\text{kg·m·s}^{-2}$ |
| Energy / Work / Heat | joule | J | $\text{kg·m}^2\text{·s}^{-2}$ |
| Power | watt | W | $\text{kg·m}^2\text{·s}^{-3}$ |
| Pressure | pascal | Pa | $\text{kg·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-2}$ |
| Frequency | hertz | Hz | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| Volume | cubic metre | m³ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| Density | kilogram per cubic metre | kg/m³ | $\text{kg·m}^{-3}$ |
| Concentration | mole per cubic metre | mol/m³ | $\text{mol·m}^{-3}$ |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | pascal-second | Pa·s | $\text{kg·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$ |
| Surface tension | newton per metre | N/m | $\text{kg·s}^{-2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Dimensional Homogeneity

The single most important rule in bioprocess mathematics is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Dimensional Homogeneity:**
> A physical equation is valid **only if every term on both sides of the equals sign has the same fundamental dimensions** (e.g., every term is in "kg", or every term is in "moles/time", etc.). Conversion is the mechanical process of *enforcing* this principle.

In **Bioprocess Calculations**, almost every mass balance, energy balance, and rate equation is written in the form:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} \pm \text{Generation}$$

and the units of every term **must be identical** (typically $\text{kg·h}^{-1}$ or $\text{mol·s}^{-1}$).

## 2.2 The "Unity Bracket" / F-Factor Method for Conversions

A conversion factor (often called an **F-factor**) is a ratio that equals **exactly 1**, used to multiply a quantity and silently change its units without changing its numerical magnitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal F-Factor Logic:**
> If $A$ has units $\text{X}$ and you want $A$ in units $\text{Y}$, find any known equality such as $\text{X} = k \cdot \text{Y}$. Then:
>
> $$A\ [\text{X}] = A\ [\text{X}] \times \left(\frac{1\ \text{Y}}{k\ \text{X}}\right) = \frac{A}{k}\ [\text{Y}]$$
>
> The bracketed conversion ratio has a *value of 1* and is therefore called a "unity bracket" or F-factor.

## 2.3 Bioprocess-Relevant Conversion Categories

Bioprocess engineering relies heavily on six conversion families. The KTU examiner will test each of these.

### 2.3.1 Mass Conversions

$$1\ \text{kg} = 1000\ \text{g} = 2.20462\ \text{lb} = 35.274\ \text{oz}$$

### 2.3.2 Volume Conversions

$$1\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{L} = 10^6\ \text{mL} = 264.172\ \text{gal (US)} = 35.3147\ \text{ft}^3$$

### 2.3.3 Temperature Conversions

The temperature conversion is the only category that is **not a linear multiplier** (it involves an offset).

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$$

$$T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \frac{9}{5}\ T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32$$

$$T(\text{K}) = \frac{5}{9}\ \big[T(^{\circ}\text{F}) - 32\big] + 273.15$$

### 2.3.4 Pressure Conversions (critical for sterilizer / fermenter design)

$$1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 1.01325\ \text{bar} = 760\ \text{mmHg (torr)} = 14.696\ \text{psi} = 1.01325 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$$

### 2.3.5 Energy Conversions (critical for agitator power, sterilization, heat balance)

$$1\ \text{kJ} = 1000\ \text{J} = 0.23901\ \text{cal} = 0.94782\ \text{BTU} = 2.7778 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kWh}$$

### 2.3.6 Amount-of-Substance Conversions (moles)

$$1\ \text{kmol} = 1000\ \text{mol} = 2.20462\ \text{lb-mol}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

The following table is the single most important reference for Module 1 questions. Memorize it before the exam.

| # | Quantity | From → To | Exact Conversion Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mass | lb → kg | $\text{kg} = 0.4536 \times \text{lb}$ |
| 2 | Mass | ton → kg | $\text{kg} = 1000 \times \text{metric ton}$ |
| 3 | Volume | gal (US) → L | $\text{L} = 3.7854 \times \text{gal}$ |
| 4 | Volume | ft³ → m³ | $\text{m}^3 = 0.02832 \times \text{ft}^3$ |
| 5 | Temperature | °C → K | $\text{K} = °\text{C} + 273.15$ |
| 6 | Temperature | °F → °C | $°\text{C} = \frac{5}{9}(°\text{F} - 32)$ |
| 7 | Temperature | °R → K | $\text{K} = °\text{R} \times \frac{5}{9}$ |
| 8 | Pressure | atm → Pa | $\text{Pa} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \times \text{atm}$ |
| 9 | Pressure | psi → Pa | $\text{Pa} = 6894.76 \times \text{psi}$ |
| 10 | Pressure | bar → Pa | $\text{Pa} = 10^5 \times \text{bar}$ |
| 11 | Pressure | mmHg → Pa | $\text{Pa} = 133.322 \times \text{mmHg}$ |
| 12 | Energy | cal → J | $\text{J} = 4.184 \times \text{cal}$ |
| 13 | Energy | BTU → J | $\text{J} = 1055.06 \times \text{BTU}$ |
| 14 | Energy | kWh → J | $\text{J} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \times \text{kWh}$ |
| 15 | Energy | eV → J | $\text{J} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times \text{eV}$ |
| 16 | Force | dyne → N | $\text{N} = 10^{-5} \times \text{dyne}$ |
| 17 | Force | lbf → N | $\text{N} = 4.4482 \times \text{lbf}$ |
| 18 | Density | g/cm³ → kg/m³ | $\text{kg/m}^3 = 1000 \times \text{g/cm}^3$ |
| 19 | Viscosity | cP → Pa·s | $\text{Pa·s} = 10^{-3} \times \text{cP}$ |
| 20 | Concentration | g/L → kg/m³ | $\text{kg/m}^3 = 1.0 \times \text{g/L}$ |

## 2.5 Dimensional Analysis — the Bridge Between Equations and Reality

**Dimensional analysis** uses the symbols in square brackets [·] to denote the "dimension" or unit of a quantity.

$$\big[F\big] = \text{N} = \frac{\text{kg·m}}{\text{s}^2}$$

Any equation that mixes incompatible units (e.g., adding a mass in *pounds* to a mass in *kilograms*) violates dimensional homogeneity and is **physically meaningless**, even though a calculator will still spit out a number.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Buckingham π Theorem (for reference only — KTU 2024 Module 1 may touch on this):**
> If a physical problem involves $n$ variables and $k$ fundamental dimensions, the relationship can be reduced to $(n - k)$ **dimensionless groups** (called $\pi$-groups). This is the foundation of scale-up in bioprocess engineering (e.g., Reynolds number, Power number).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Worked-Example 1 — Multi-Stage Mass/Volume/Concentration Conversion

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A fermentation broth has a glucose concentration of **$C = 45\ \text{g/L}$**. The total broth volume in the reactor is **$V = 2.5\ \text{m}^3$**. Calculate the total mass of glucose in:
> (a) kilograms (kg)
> (b) pounds-mass (lb)
> (c) gram-moles (mol), given the molecular weight of glucose $M_{\text{glucose}} = 180.156\ \text{g/mol}$.

### Part (a) — Mass in kg

**Step 1 — Identify given data and the desired output.**
The data are: $C = 45\ \text{g/L}$ and $V = 2.5\ \text{m}^3$.
Output required: $m$ in $\text{kg}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental mass–concentration–volume relation.**

$$m = C \times V$$

**Step 3 — Insert the numbers, but match units before multiplying.**

We need both $C$ and $V$ in *compatible* SI units. Let's express everything in $\text{kg}$ and $\text{m}^3$.

Convert $C$ from $\text{g/L}$ to $\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$C = 45\ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{L}} \times \underbrace{\left(\frac{1\ \text{kg}}{1000\ \text{g}}\right)}_{\text{Factor 1}} \times \underbrace{\left(\frac{1000\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{m}^3}\right)}_{\text{Factor 2}} = 45\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$m = 45\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 2.5\ \text{m}^3$$

$$m = 112.5\ \text{kg}$$

### Part (b) — Mass in lb

**Step 5 — Apply unity-bracket F-factor conversion from kg to lb.**

$$m = 112.5\ \text{kg} \times \underbrace{\left(\frac{2.20462\ \text{lb}}{1\ \text{kg}}\right)}_{\text{Factor 3}}$$

$$m \approx 248.02\ \text{lb}$$

### Part (c) — Amount of substance in mol

**Step 6 — Convert mass to moles using molecular weight.**

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

First, the mass must be in **grams** (to match $M$ in g/mol).

$$m = 112.5\ \text{kg} \times \left(\frac{1000\ \text{g}}{1\ \text{kg}}\right) = 112\,500\ \text{g}$$

**Step 7 — Final division.**

$$n = \frac{112\,500\ \text{g}}{180.156\ \text{g/mol}}$$

$$n \approx 624.4\ \text{mol}$$

## 3.2 Master Worked-Example 2 — Temperature Conversion with Offset

> **Problem Statement:**
> An autoclave is operating at **$250\ °\text{F}$**. Steam tables give the saturation pressure in **kPa**. Convert the temperature to **K** for the steam-table lookup.

**Step 1 — Convert °F to °C using the offset formula.**

$$T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9}\ \big(T(^{\circ}\text{F}) - 32\big)$$

$$T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9}\ (250 - 32)$$

$$T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \times 218 = 121.11\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Convert °C to K using the offset formula.**

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$$

$$T(\text{K}) = 121.11 + 273.15$$

$$T(\text{K}) = 394.26\ \text{K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU 2024):** Students often *multiply* by 5/9 and *forget* to subtract 32. Temperature conversions are the **only** common conversions that involve an *additive* offset, not a pure multiplier.

## 3.3 Master Worked-Example 3 — Pressure Conversion in Fermenter Headspace

> **Problem Statement:**
> The dissolved-oxygen probe reads a partial pressure of **$p_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21\ \text{atm}$**. The aeration system delivers air at **$3.0\ \text{bar}$**. Convert both to **Pa** and verify the gas-mixture law.

**Step 1 — Convert $0.21\ \text{atm}$ to Pa.**

$$p_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21\ \text{atm} \times \left(\frac{1.01325 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}}{1\ \text{atm}}\right)$$

$$p_{\text{O}_2} = 2.128 \times 10^4\ \text{Pa} \approx 21.28\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $3.0\ \text{bar}$ to Pa.**

$$p_{\text{air}} = 3.0\ \text{bar} \times \left(\frac{10^5\ \text{Pa}}{1\ \text{bar}}\right) = 3.0 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 300\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Verify by ratio (sanity check).**

$$\frac{p_{\text{O}_2}}{p_{\text{air}}} = \frac{21.28}{300} \approx 0.0709 \neq 0.21$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $0.21\ \text{atm}$ value refers to the **partial pressure of O₂ in the air mixture** (i.e., $21\%$ of $1\ \text{atm}$), whereas $3.0\ \text{bar}$ is the *total delivery pressure* of the air stream. They are not directly comparable until the air is also normalized to *its own* partial pressure of O₂ ($3.0 \times 0.21 = 0.63\ \text{bar} = 63\ \text{kPa}$).

## 3.4 Master Worked-Example 4 — Energy Conversion in Sterilization

> **Problem Statement:**
> The sterilization of a fermentation medium requires **$Q = 1.5 \times 10^6\ \text{BTU}$** of energy. Express this in:
> (a) joules (J)
> (b) kilojoules (kJ)
> (c) kilowatt-hours (kWh)

### Part (a) — Convert BTU to J

$$Q = 1.5 \times 10^6\ \text{BTU} \times \left(\frac{1055.06\ \text{J}}{1\ \text{BTU}}\right)$$

$$Q \approx 1.5826 \times 10^9\ \text{J}$$

### Part (b) — Convert J to kJ

$$Q = 1.5826 \times 10^9\ \text{J} \times \left(\frac{1\ \text{kJ}}{1000\ \text{J}}\right)$$

$$Q = 1.5826 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ}$$

### Part (c) — Convert J to kWh

$$Q = 1.5826 \times 10^9\ \text{J} \times \left(\frac{1\ \text{kWh}}{3.6 \times 10^6\ \text{J}}\right)$$

$$Q \approx 439.6\ \text{kWh}$$

## 3.5 Dimensional Homogeneity Check — A Mini-Analytical Matrix

> **Problem Statement:**
> Verify whether the following proposed bioprocess equation is dimensionally homogeneous, where $r$ is the volumetric reaction rate ($\text{mol·L}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$), $k$ is the rate constant, and $C$ is the substrate concentration ($\text{mol/L}$):
>
> $$r = k \cdot C^2$$
>
> For this equation to be valid, what must be the units of $k$?

**Step 1 — Write the dimension equation.**

$$\big[r\big] = \big[k\big] \cdot \big[C\big]^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known dimensions.**

$$\frac{\text{mol}}{\text{L·h}} = \big[k\big] \cdot \left(\frac{\text{mol}}{\text{L}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the dimensions of $k$.**

$$\big[k\big] = \frac{\text{mol/L/h}}{(\text{mol/L})^2}$$

$$\big[k\big] = \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{L·h}} \times \frac{\text{L}^2}{\text{mol}^2}$$

$$\big[k\big] = \frac{\text{L}}{\text{mol·h}}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Yes, the equation is dimensionally homogeneous provided $k$ carries the units $\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$. This is the classic form of a **second-order reaction rate constant** in bioprocess kinetics.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Conversion from One System to Another (Consolidated Table)

The following consolidated table is the **single most useful study aid** for Module 1 of PCBBT205. It is built directly from the SI base-unit framework.

| Physical Quantity | SI Unit (Base/Derived) | CGS Equivalent | FPS Equivalent | Conversion F-Factor (×) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length | m (base) | 100 cm | 3.28084 ft | 1 m = 100 cm = 3.28084 ft |
| Mass | kg (base) | 1000 g | 2.20462 lb | 1 kg = 1000 g = 2.20462 lb |
| Time | s (base) | 1 s | 1 s | identical |
| Force | N (derived) | $10^5$ dyne | 0.22481 lbf | 1 N = $10^5$ dyne = 0.22481 lbf |
| Energy | J (derived) | $10^7$ erg | $9.4782 \times 10^{-4}$ BTU | 1 J = $10^7$ erg |
| Power | W (derived) | $10^7$ erg/s | 0.73756 ft·lbf/s | 1 W = 1 J/s |
| Pressure | Pa (derived) | 10 dyne/cm² | $1.4504 \times 10^{-4}$ psi | 1 Pa = 10 dyne/cm² |
| Volume | m³ (derived) | $10^6$ cm³ (cc) | 35.3147 ft³ | 1 m³ = $10^6$ cc |
| Density | kg/m³ (derived) | 0.001 g/cm³ | 0.06243 lb/ft³ | 1 kg/m³ = 0.001 g/cc |
| Viscosity | Pa·s (derived) | 10 poise | 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s | 1 Pa·s = 10 P (poise) |
| Concentration | mol/m³ (derived) | $10^{-3}$ mol/L (M) | — | 1 mol/m³ = $10^{-3}$ M |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Architecture of the SI System

This diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy: from the seven **base quantities** at the root, through **derived units**, to the specific **bioprocess engineering quantities** that you will encounter in every module of PCBBT205.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SI SYSTEM OF UNITS]:::root --> B[7 BASE QUANTITIES]:::base
    A --> C[DERIVED UNITS]:::derived
    A --> D[SUPPLEMENTARY]:::supp

    B --> B1[Length m]:::base
    B --> B2[Mass kg]:::base
    B --> B3[Time s]:::base
    B --> B4[Electric Current A]:::base
    B --> B5[Temperature K]:::base
    B --> B6[Amount of Substance mol]:::base
    B --> B7[Luminous Intensity cd]:::base

    C --> C1[Force N]:::derived
    C --> C2[Energy J]:::derived
    C --> C3[Power W]:::derived
    C --> C4[Pressure Pa]:::derived
    C --> C5[Volume m3]:::derived
    C --> C6[Density kg per m3]:::derived
    C --> C7[Viscosity Pa s]:::derived
    C --> C8[Concentration mol per m3]:::derived
    C --> C9[Surface Tension N per m]:::derived

    C1 --> E1[Sterilizer Design Agitator Torque]:::app
    C2 --> E2[Heat Balance Steam Sterilization]:::app
    C3 --> E3[Power Input Aeration Agitation]:::app
    C4 --> E4[Autoclave Headspace Fermenter]:::app
    C5 --> E5[Reactor Volume Sizing]:::app
    C6 --> E6[Broth Density Settling]:::app
    C7 --> E7[Reynolds Number Mixing]:::app
    C8 --> E8[Substrate Kinetics Monod Model]:::app
    C9 --> E9[Foam Control Antifoam Addition]:::app

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef base fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef derived fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef supp fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The "Unity Bracket" / F-Factor Conversion Flowchart

This flow-chart shows the **operational logic** that an engineer follows when converting a quantity from one set of units to another using the F-factor method. It is the most important procedural diagram in this module.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Given Quantity and Unit]:::step --> S2[Step 2 Identify Target Unit Required]:::step
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Find the Equality Between Source and Target Units]:::step
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Construct the Unity Bracket Ratio Value must equal 1]:::step
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Place Source Unit in Denominator and Target Unit in Numerator]:::step
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Multiply the Original Quantity by the Unity Bracket]:::step
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Cancel Units and Verify Dimensional Homogeneity]:::step
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Carry out Numerical Calculation and Apply Significant Figures]:::step
    S8 --> S9[Final Answer in Target Unit]:::done

    classDef step fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Bioprocess-Engineering Quantities Mapped to SI Derived Units

This diagram illustrates how every bioprocess quantity you will face in Modules 2–5 of PCBBT205 is a *derived unit* of the seven SI base quantities.

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Mass kg]:::base --> S1[Volumetric Flow Rate m3 per s]:::bio
    L[Length m]:::base --> S1
    T[Time s]:::base --> S1
    M --> S2[Mass Transfer Coefficient m per s]:::bio
    L --> S2
    T --> S2
    M --> S3[Power per Unit Volume W per m3]:::bio
    L --> S3
    T --> S3
    M --> S4[Impeller Tip Speed m per s]:::bio
    L --> S4
    T --> S4
    M --> S5[Heat Transfer Coefficient W per m2 per K]:::bio
    L --> S5
    T --> S5
    K[Temperature K]:::base --> S5
    N[Amount mol]:::base --> S6[Reaction Rate mol per m3 per s]:::bio
    L --> S6
    T --> S6
    N --> S7[Yield Coefficient g per g or mol per mol]:::bio
    M --> S7

    classDef base fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bio fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Temperature Conversion Cross-Reference Map

Because temperature is the only "offset" conversion, the following map shows the **four temperature scales** and the conversion pathways that connect them.

```mermaid
graph LR
    C[Celsius C]:::temp -->|Add 273.15| K[Kelvin K]:::temp
    K -->|Subtract 273.15| C
    F[Fahrenheit F]:::temp -->|Subtract 32 then x 5 by 9| C
    C -->|x 9 by 5 then Add 32| F
    F -->|Subtract 32 x 5 by 9 then Add 273.15| K
    K -->|Subtract 273.15 x 9 by 5 then Add 32| F
    R[Rankine R]:::temp -->|x 5 by 9| K
    K -->|x 9 by 5| R

    classDef temp fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Define the SI system of units. List the seven base quantities with their SI units and symbols.**

**Model Answer:**

The **International System of Units (SI)** is the modern, internationally accepted metric system established by the *General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM)* in 1960. It is built on **seven** independent, dimensionally distinct **base quantities**, from which all other (derived) units are constructed.

The seven SI base quantities are:

1. **Length** — `metre (m)`
2. **Mass** — `kilogram (kg)`
3. **Time** — `second (s)`
4. **Electric current** — `ampere (A)`
5. **Thermodynamic temperature** — `kelvin (K)`
6. **Amount of substance** — `mole (mol)`
7. **Luminous intensity** — `candela (cd)`

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Marking Key:** 1 mark for the SI definition + 1 mark for the list + 1 mark for correct symbols.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**What is dimensional homogeneity? Why is it essential in bioprocess engineering equations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Dimensional homogeneity** is the principle that *every term on both sides of a valid physical equation must have the same fundamental dimensions* (i.e., the same units when expressed in the SI base).

It is essential in bioprocess engineering because:

- It is the **first correctness check** on any newly proposed rate equation (e.g., Monod, Michaelis–Menten, logistic growth).
- It **forces all input data to be converted to a common unit system** (usually SI) before being plugged into a mass or energy balance.
- It **prevents physically meaningless operations** such as adding a mass in pounds to a mass in kilograms.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks, **or** as a single 14-mark question. Module 1 of PCBBT205 typically has an internal choice; both options are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relation between the SI unit of pressure (pascal) and the CGS unit of pressure (dyne/cm²). Show all intermediate steps.

**(b)** A fermentation medium has a glucose concentration of **$50\ \text{g/L}$** and a total working volume of **$3.0\ \text{m}^3$**. Calculate the mass of glucose present in (i) **kg**, (ii) **pounds (lb)**, and (iii) **kg-moles**. Molecular weight of glucose = **$180.156\ \text{g/mol}$**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Start with the SI definition.**

$$\big[p\big] = \text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Express newton in SI base units.**

$$1\ \text{N} = 1\ \text{kg·m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Express dyne in SI base units (using $1\ \text{N} = 10^5\ \text{dyne}$).**

$$1\ \text{dyne} = 10^{-5}\ \text{N} = 10^{-5}\ \text{kg·m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Express $1\ \text{m}^2$ in $\text{cm}^2$.**

$$1\ \text{m}^2 = (100\ \text{cm})^2 = 10^4\ \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Combine using the F-factor method.**

$$1\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = 1\ \frac{10^5\ \text{dyne}}{10^4\ \text{cm}^2} = 10\ \frac{\text{dyne}}{\text{cm}^2}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore 1\ \text{Pa} = 10\ \text{dyne/cm}^2}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**

$$C = 50\ \text{g/L}, \quad V = 3.0\ \text{m}^3, \quad M_{\text{glucose}} = 180.156\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass (i) in kg.**

Convert concentration to $\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$C = 50\ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{L}} \times \frac{1\ \text{kg}}{1000\ \text{g}} \times \frac{1000\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{m}^3} = 50\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$$

$$m = C \cdot V = 50\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 3.0\ \text{m}^3 = 150\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to pounds (ii).**

$$m = 150\ \text{kg} \times 2.20462\ \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{kg}}$$

$$m \approx 330.69\ \text{lb}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg-moles (iii).**

First, total grams:

$$m = 150\ \text{kg} \times 1000\ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{kg}} = 1.5 \times 10^5\ \text{g}$$

Then moles:

$$n = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5\ \text{g}}{180.156\ \text{g/mol}} \approx 832.6\ \text{mol} = 0.8326\ \text{kmol}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Mass} = 150\ \text{kg}\ ;\ 330.69\ \text{lb}\ ;\ 0.8326\ \text{kmol}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** An industrial fermenter is sterilized at **$121\ °\text{C}$** for 20 minutes. Convert this temperature to **(i)** kelvin, **(ii)** °F, and **(iii)** °R (Rankine). State clearly where an "offset" is required and why.

**(b)** A pump delivers **$15\ \text{gal/min}$** of nutrient feed. The pressure at the pump discharge is **$40\ \text{psi}$**. Convert both quantities to SI units (**m³/s** and **Pa**, respectively) and explain the role of dimensional homogeneity in this conversion.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert $121\ °\text{C}$ to K (offset of $+273.15$).**

$$T = 121 + 273.15 = 394.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $121\ °\text{C}$ to °F (offset of $+32$ and slope of $9/5$).**

$$T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \frac{9}{5} \times 121 + 32 = 217.8 + 32 = 249.8\ ^{\circ}\text{F}$$

**Step 3 — Convert K to °R (Rankine).**

$$T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = T(\text{K}) \times \frac{9}{5} = 394.15 \times 1.8$$

$$T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = 709.47\ ^{\circ}\text{R}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on offsets.**
Temperature is an *intensive* property measured from a *reference datum* (e.g., absolute zero for K, freezing point of water for °C). A pure multiplicative F-factor cannot capture the shift in zero-point, hence an **additive offset** (e.g., $+273.15$ or $-32$) is mandatory. Mass, length, time, and force are *extensive* and require only multiplicative F-factors.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert $15\ \text{gal/min}$ to $\text{m}^3\text{/s}$.**

$$15\ \frac{\text{gal}}{\text{min}} \times \frac{3.7854\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{gal}} \times \frac{1\ \text{m}^3}{1000\ \text{L}} \times \frac{1\ \text{min}}{60\ \text{s}}$$

$$Q = 15 \times 3.7854 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{1}{60}$$

$$Q = 9.4635 \times 10^{-4}\ \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $40\ \text{psi}$ to Pa.**

$$40\ \text{psi} \times 6894.76\ \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{psi}} = 2.758 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 275.8\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Role of dimensional homogeneity.**
The volumetric flow rate is in $\text{m}^3\text{/s}$ and the pressure is in $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2 = \text{kg/(m·s}^2)$. If the next step in the calculation is to compute the *pump power* $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$, then:

$$P = (\text{Pa}) \cdot (\text{m}^3\text{/s}) = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m·s}^2} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} = \frac{\text{kg·m}^2}{\text{s}^3} = \text{W}$$

The output unit (W) emerges *automatically* from the dimensional algebra — a powerful proof of dimensional homogeneity.

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the offset in temperature conversion.** Multiplying °C by 9/5 without first subtracting 32 costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Inverting the F-factor.** Writing $\left(\frac{1\ \text{kg}}{2.20462\ \text{lb}}\right)$ instead of $\left(\frac{2.20462\ \text{lb}}{1\ \text{kg}}\right)$ will invert your answer and lose full marks.
> 3. **Mixing g/L with m³.** Students frequently compute $m = 50\ \text{g/L} \times 3.0\ \text{m}^3$ directly, producing the absurd unit $\text{g·m}^3\text{/L}$. Always normalize *both* factors to compatible units first.
> 4. **Wrong molecular weight for moles.** A common confusion is to use $M$ in kg/mol when $m$ is in g. The KTU key is strict: 0.5 mark deducted per unit mismatch.
> 5. **Dropping units in final answer.** A number alone is incomplete; the unit *is* part of the answer. Always write, e.g., "150 kg", not just "150".
> 6. **Using `atm` for partial pressure without noting "absolute" vs. "gauge".** In fermenter headspace calculations, "30 psig" (gauge) is **not** the same as "30 psia" (absolute). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Memorize Before the Exam):**
>
> - **SI = 7 base units**: `m, kg, s, A, K, mol, cd` — these are the *root* of every other unit.
> - **Derived units** are *products* or *quotients* of base units. The most-used in bioprocess: `N, J, W, Pa, Hz, m³, kg/m³, mol/m³, Pa·s, N/m`.
> - **All conversions** are products of *unity brackets* (F-factors), where the bracket is a ratio **numerically equal to 1**.
> - **Temperature** is the *only* common conversion requiring an **additive offset** (because °C, °F, and °R have different zero-points).
> - **Dimensional homogeneity** is the *first* check on any new equation; if the units don't match, the equation is wrong.
> - **Pressure conversions** to memorize: `1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa`.
> - **Energy conversions** to memorize: `1 cal = 4.184 J`, `1 BTU = 1055.06 J`, `1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J`, `1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J`.
> - **Mass conversions** to memorize: `1 kg = 1000 g = 2.20462 lb`, `1 lb = 453.6 g`.
> - **Volume conversions** to memorize: `1 m³ = 1000 L = 264.172 gal (US) = 35.3147 ft³`, `1 gal (US) = 3.7854 L`.
> - **Mole concept**: $n = m/M$ — keep $m$ in **g** when $M$ is in **g/mol**; keep $m$ in **kg** when $M$ is in **kg/kmol**.
> - **Dimensional analysis** uses square brackets [·] to denote the unit of a quantity; the *Buckingham π-theorem* reduces an $n$-variable problem to $(n - k)$ dimensionless groups.
> - **Always write the unit** alongside every numerical answer — a "naked number" is an incomplete answer in KTU valuation.
> - **Significant figures** must be preserved through the *least-precise* input; do not invent decimal places during conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Composition: Methods of Expressing Compositions of Mixtures and Solutions (Mole Percent, Mass Percent, Volume Percent, Molarity, Molality, Normality)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemical Composition: Methods of Expressing Compositions of Mixtures and Solutions (Mole Percent, Mass Percent, Volume Percent, Molarity, Molality, Normality)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Need for Expressing Composition

In bioprocess engineering, we rarely deal with **pure substances**. A fermentation broth contains water, sugars, amino acids, dissolved oxygen, salts, cells, proteins, and metabolic by-products. To design a bioreactor, scale-up a fermentation, or perform mass balances, we MUST have a standardized, unambiguous way of reporting "how much of what is present."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1):** Students must master the six primary methods of expressing chemical composition because the *same* mixture described in two different ways leads to entirely different numerical values. Choosing the wrong concentration unit is the #1 source of calculation errors in bioprocess design.

## 1.2 The Six Methods — Formal Definitions

### A. Mole Percent (mol %) — $x_i$

The **mole percent** of component $i$ in a mixture is the ratio of the number of moles of $i$ to the **total number of moles of ALL components**, multiplied by 100.

$$x_i \,(\%) = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j} \times 100$$

where $n_i$ = moles of component $i$ and the denominator is the sum of moles of every component in the mixture.

### B. Mass Percent (wt %) — $w_i$

The **mass percent** of component $i$ is the ratio of the mass of $i$ to the **total mass of the mixture**, multiplied by 100.

$$w_i \,(\%) = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} m_j} \times 100$$

### C. Volume Percent (vol %) — $\phi_i$

The **volume percent** of component $i$ is the ratio of the volume of pure $i$ to the **total volume of the mixture**, multiplied by 100. Valid strictly for liquids mixing without significant volume contraction.

$$\phi_i \,(\%) = \frac{V_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} V_j} \times 100$$

### D. Molarity (M) — $C_i$

The **molarity** of a solution is the number of moles of solute dissolved in **one litre of solution** (not solvent).

$$C_i = \frac{n_{\text{solute},\,i}}{V_{\text{solution}}} \quad \left[\text{units: mol/L or mol/dm}^3\right]$$

### E. Molality (m) — $b_i$

The **molality** of a solution is the number of moles of solute dissolved in **one kilogram of solvent** (not solution).

$$b_i = \frac{n_{\text{solute},\,i}}{m_{\text{solvent}}} \quad \left[\text{units: mol/kg}\right]$$

### F. Normality (N) — $N_i$

The **normality** of a solution is the number of **gram-equivalents** of solute dissolved in one litre of solution.

$$N_i = \frac{\text{mass of solute} / \text{equivalent weight}}{V_{\text{solution}}} = \frac{n \times n_{\text{factor}}}{V_{\text{solution}}} \quad \left[\text{units: eq/L}\right]$$

where $n_{\text{factor}}$ = valence factor (number of replaceable $H^+$, electrons transferred, or charge).

## 1.3 The Six Methods at a Glance

| # | Method | Symbol | What it relates | Key Denominator |
|---|--------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Mole Percent | $x_i$ | Moles of $i$ | Total moles of mixture |
| 2 | Mass Percent | $w_i$ | Mass of $i$ | Total mass of mixture |
| 3 | Volume Percent | $\phi_i$ | Volume of $i$ | Total volume of mixture |
| 4 | Molarity | $C_i$ or $M$ | Moles of solute | **Volume of solution (L)** |
| 5 | Molality | $b_i$ or $m$ | Moles of solute | **Mass of solvent (kg)** |
| 6 | Normality | $N_i$ | Equivalents of solute | Volume of solution (L) |

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Coffee Shop" Analogy**
> Imagine you ordered a cup of mixed coffee:
> - **Mass percent** = "30 g of sugar in a 250 g cup" → compares with the *whole cup on the weighing scale*.
> - **Mole percent** = "Out of every 100 molecules, 12 are caffeine" → a *molecular headcount*; matters when reactions occur on a molecule-by-molecule basis.
> - **Molarity** = "The cup contains 0.5 mol of sugar in 0.25 L of coffee" → a *volumetric* statement, ideal for titrations in the lab.
> - **Molality** = "The cup contains 0.5 mol of sugar per 0.22 kg of water" → a *gravimetric* statement; the only unit that doesn't change with temperature! (Critical in colligative property calculations.)
> - **Normality** = "1 N acid means 1 mole of $H^+$ per litre" → emphasizes *reacting capacity* per litre.
> - **Volume percent** = "20 mL of vanilla syrup in 100 mL of drink" → the bartender's unit.

## 1.5 Why Bioprocess Engineers Care

In a fermentation tank we use **mass percent** for feedstock billing, **molarity** for enzymatic assays, **molality** for thermodynamic property predictions (boiling-point elevation in sterilization), and **normality** for acid/base neutralization during pH control. The **mole fraction** is the gateway to **Raoult's Law** and **vapour–liquid equilibria**, which dictate ethanol removal in distillation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dissecting Each Method — The "Why" Behind the Denominator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Denominator Principle:** The choice of denominator decides the *physical meaning* of the concentration. Always read the denominator first!

### 2.1.1 Mole Percent — $x_i$

**Why total moles?** Because stoichiometry (chemical reactions) occurs on a *mole-to-mole* basis. A reaction cannot distinguish mass; it only knows "molecules bumping into each other."

- Mole fractions obey: $\sum x_i = 1$ (or 100 %).
- **Unitless** (a pure ratio × 100).
- Most useful in **gas-phase reactions** and **thermodynamics** (chemical potential, partial pressure $p_i = x_i P$).

### 2.1.2 Mass Percent — $w_i$

**Why total mass?** Because balances in industrial accounting (kg in vs. kg out) are made on a *mass* basis; mass is conserved in any process, even if volume or moles change due to reactions.

- Mass fractions obey: $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Unitless**, but engineers sometimes write wt %.
- Dominant in **feed formulation**, **drying calculations**, and **ash content** analysis.

### 2.1.3 Volume Percent — $\phi_i$

**Why pure-component volumes?** For ideal liquid mixtures (ethanol-water, benzene-toluene), the pure-component volumes are additive, so a percentage of *bulk* volume reflects the actual filling of a tank.

- $\sum \phi_i = 100\%$ only if volumes are strictly additive (rare for aqueous electrolyte solutions).
- Used in **beverage industry**, **alcohol content (ABV)**, and **perfumes**.

### 2.1.4 Molarity — $C_i$

**Why volume of solution?** Because laboratory glassware (volumetric flasks, pipettes, burettes) measures *volume* of solution, not solvent. Molarity is convenient at the bench.

- **Disadvantage:** Molarity changes with temperature (solution expands/contracts).
- **Disadvantage:** Molarity changes when components react (e.g., dilution by product water).
- 1 M = 1 mol/L = 1 mol/dm³ = **1000 mol/m³**.

### 2.1.5 Molality — $b_i$

**Why mass of solvent?** Because mass is *temperature-independent*. The solvent's mass doesn't change when the solution is heated or cooled. This makes molality the gold standard for colligative properties (boiling-point elevation, freezing-point depression, osmotic pressure).

- **1 molal** = 1 mol solute / 1 kg solvent.
- Used in **physical chemistry**, **cryoscopy**, **ebullioscopy**.

### 2.1.6 Normality — $N_i$

**Why equivalents?** Because a single molecule of $H_2SO_4$ can neutralize **2** molecules of NaOH. Normality captures the *effective reacting capacity* per litre, making titration calculations a one-step affair: $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$.

- $N = M \times n_{\text{factor}}$
- Valence factors:
  - Acids: number of replaceable $H^+$ (HCl → 1, $H_2SO_4$ → 2, $H_3PO_4$ → 3)
  - Bases: number of replaceable $OH^-$ (NaOH → 1, $Ca(OH)_2$ → 2)
  - Salts: total positive (or negative) charge
  - Redox: number of electrons transferred

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it solves 90 % of Module-1 numerical questions.**

| # | Method | Master Formula | SI / Practical Unit | Denominator Dependence |
|---|--------|----------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Mole Fraction | $x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | Total moles |
| 2 | Mass Fraction | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum m_j}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | Total mass |
| 3 | Volume Fraction | $\phi_i = \dfrac{V_i}{\sum V_j}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | Total volume |
| 4 | Molarity | $C_i = \dfrac{n_i}{V_{\text{soln}}}$ | mol/L (M) | **Volume of solution** |
| 5 | Molality | $b_i = \dfrac{n_i}{m_{\text{solvent, kg}}}$ | mol/kg (m) | **Mass of solvent** |
| 6 | Normality | $N_i = \dfrac{m_i / E_i}{V_{\text{soln, L}}}$ | eq/L (N) | Volume of solution |

### 2.2.1 Inter-Conversion Master Formulas

| Conversion | Formula | Remarks |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Molarity → Molality | $b = \dfrac{1000 \, C}{\rho \, (1000) - C \, M_2}$ | $\rho$ in g/mL, $M_2$ = molar mass solute |
| Molality → Molarity | $C = \dfrac{1000 \, b \, \rho}{1000 + b \, M_2}$ | $\rho$ = solution density (g/mL) |
| Molarity → Normality | $N = M \times n_{\text{factor}}$ | Only if same species |
| Mass % ↔ Molarity | $C = \dfrac{10 \, \rho \, w}{M_2}$ | $w$ in %, $\rho$ in g/mL |

where $M_2$ is the molar mass of the solute, $\rho$ is the density of the solution (g/mL), and $w$ is the mass percent (as a number, e.g., 10 not 0.10).

## 2.3 The Mole–Mass–Volume Bridge (Engineering Utility)

In bioprocess calculations we frequently traverse between mass and moles:

$$n = \frac{m}{M} \qquad \text{and} \qquad m = n \times M \qquad \text{and} \qquad V = n \times V_m$$

where $M$ is molar mass (g/mol) and $V_m$ is molar volume (L/mol). At STP, $V_m = 22.414$ L/mol for an ideal gas; for liquids, $V_m = M / \rho$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications**
> - **Mole fraction** → drives **Raoult's Law** $p_i = x_i p_i^{\text{sat}}$, the foundation of distillation column design (e.g., ethanol recovery from fermentation broth).
> - **Mass percent** → drives **drying/evaporation** mass balances; essential in **spray-drier** design for enzyme powders.
> - **Molarity** → used in **Michaelis–Menten kinetics** $v = V_{\max} [S] / (K_m + [S])$ where $[S]$ is in mol/L.
> - **Molality** → used in **freezing-point depression** to characterize cold denaturation of proteins.
> - **Normality** → used in **pH-control loops** (titrant dosing of NaOH / $H_3PO_4$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Mass Percent to Mole Fraction (and back)

This is the **most-asked conversion** in KTU Module 1 numericals. We must derive it rigorously.

### Given:
- A solution of $N$ components.
- Mass percent of component $i$: $w_i$ (% by mass).
- Molar mass of component $i$: $M_i$ (g/mol).
- Choose a **basis**: $100$ g of solution (so each $w_i$ becomes grams directly).

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Pick the basis.**
Assume a basis of $100$ g of solution. Then $m_i = w_i$ grams for every component $i$.

**Step 2 — Convert each mass to moles.**
Using $n_i = m_i / M_i$:

$$n_i = \frac{w_i}{M_i}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the moles to get the total.**
Total moles in the solution:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{w_j}{M_j}$$

**Step 4 — Form the mole fraction.**
Mole fraction of $i$:

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum_j n_j} = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j / M_j}$$

After multiplying numerator and denominator by 100 (or by 1 — it cancels):

$$\boxed{\,x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j / M_j}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Invert: Mole fraction to mass percent.**

$$\boxed{\,w_i = \frac{x_i \, M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} x_j \, M_j} \times 100\,}$$

This last formula gives $w_i$ in **%** (because the denominator equals the *mean molar mass* of the mixture $\bar{M}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Converting Molarity to Molality

### Given:
- Aqueous solution of a single non-dissociating solute.
- Molarity $C$ (mol/L) and density of solution $\rho$ (g/mL).
- Molar mass of solute $M_2$ (g/mol).
- Molar mass of water $M_1 = 18.015$ g/mol.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Choose a basis: 1 L of solution.**
- Volume of solution = $1000$ mL.
- Mass of solution = $1000 \, \rho$ g.
- Moles of solute = $C \times 1$ L = $C$ mol.
- Mass of solute = $C \, M_2$ g.

**Step 2 — Compute the mass of solvent.**
Mass of solvent = mass of solution − mass of solute:

$$m_{\text{solvent}} = 1000 \rho - C \, M_2 \quad \text{[grams]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert solvent mass to kg.**

$$m_{\text{solvent}} = \frac{1000 \rho - C \, M_2}{1000} \quad \text{[kg]}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the molality definition.**

$$b = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solvent, kg}}} = \frac{C}{(1000 \rho - C \, M_2)/1000}$$

Simplify:

$$\boxed{\,b = \frac{1000 \, C}{1000 \, \rho - C \, M_2}\quad \left[\text{mol/kg solvent}\right]\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Molarity ↔ Molality Equation is the most-frequently-marked derivation in KTU Module 1 — memorize it.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A fermentation medium contains 8 wt % glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$), 2 wt % yeast extract, and 90 wt % water. The density of the medium is 1.03 g/mL. Calculate (i) mole fraction of glucose, (ii) molarity of glucose, (iii) molality of glucose.

**Data:**
- $w_{\text{glucose}} = 8$, $w_{\text{extract}} = 2$, $w_{\text{water}} = 90$ (g per 100 g solution)
- Molar masses: $M_{\text{glucose}} = 180$ g/mol, $M_{\text{extract}} \approx 500$ g/mol (typical), $M_{\text{water}} = 18$ g/mol
- $\rho = 1.03$ g/mL

### (i) Mole fraction of glucose

**Step 1 — Convert masses to moles on a 100 g basis.**

$$n_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{8}{180} = 0.04444 \; \text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{extract}} = \frac{2}{500} = 0.00400 \; \text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{water}} = \frac{90}{18} = 5.0000 \; \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Sum to get total moles.**

$$\sum n_j = 0.04444 + 0.00400 + 5.0000 = 5.04844 \; \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the mole fraction formula.**

$$x_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{0.04444}{5.04844} = 0.008802 \approx 0.00880$$

$$x_{\text{glucose}} \approx 0.88 \;\text{mol \%}$$

### (ii) Molarity of glucose

**Step 1 — Mass of glucose in 1 L of solution.**
1 L of solution = $1000 \times 1.03 = 1030$ g of medium.

$$m_{\text{glucose}} = 0.08 \times 1030 = 82.4 \; \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of glucose in 1 L.**

$$n_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{82.4}{180} = 0.4578 \; \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Molarity.**

$$C_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{0.4578 \; \text{mol}}{1 \; \text{L}} = 0.458 \; \text{M}$$

### (iii) Molality of glucose

**Step 1 — Mass of water in 1 L of solution.**

$$m_{\text{water}} = 0.90 \times 1030 = 927 \; \text{g} = 0.927 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply molality definition.**

$$b_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{0.4578 \; \text{mol}}{0.927 \; \text{kg}} = 0.494 \; \text{m}$$

### Verification using the derived formula:

$$b = \frac{1000 \times 0.4578}{1000 \times 1.03 - 0.4578 \times 180} = \frac{457.8}{1030 - 82.4} = \frac{457.8}{947.6} = 0.483 \; \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight discrepancy ($0.494$ vs $0.483$) arises from rounding in the manual approach. The formula route is *exact* and preferred in KTU board solutions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Normality in Acid–Base Neutralization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** How many mL of 0.20 N NaOH are required to neutralize 25 mL of 0.15 N $H_2SO_4$ in a fermentation broth during pH control?

**Solution:**

Using the **equivalence principle** $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$:

$$0.20 \times V_1 = 0.15 \times 25$$

$$V_1 = \frac{0.15 \times 25}{0.20} = \frac{3.75}{0.20} = 18.75 \; \text{mL}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{NaOH}} = 18.75 \; \text{mL}}$$

---

## 3.5 Quick Sanity Checks for Exams

| If the answer is… | Check that… |
|--------------------|--------------|
| Mole fraction ≈ 0.5 for *one* component in a *two-component* mixture | …it is consistent with a 1:1 molar ratio. |
| Molality > Molarity | …the solution is *denser than water* (ρ > 1 g/mL) **OR** the solute has a *low molar mass*. |
| Normality = Molarity | …the valence factor = 1 (e.g., HCl, NaOH). |
| Normality = 2 × Molarity | …the valence factor = 2 (e.g., $H_2SO_4$ as an acid). |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Concentration Units
