- $R = 0.0821 \ \text{L}\cdot\text{atm}/(\text{mol}\cdot\text{K})$

**Part (a) — Partial Pressures:**

$$P_{N_2} = y_{N_2} \cdot P_{total} = (0.30)(2.5) = 0.75 \ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P_{total} = (0.50)(2.5) = 1.25 \ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{CO_2} = y_{CO_2} \cdot P_{total} = (0.20)(2.5) = 0.50 \ \text{atm}$$

**Verification (Dalton's Law):** $0.75 + 1.25 + 0.50 = 2.50$ atm ✓

**Part (b) — Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{avg} = (0.30)(28.02) + (0.50)(32.00) + (0.20)(44.01)$$

$$M_{avg} = 8.406 + 16.000 + 8.802 = 33.208 \ \text{g/mol}$$

**Part (c) — Density of Mixture:**

Using the formula in consistent units: $P = 2.5$ atm, $M_{avg} = 33.208$ g/mol, $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K), $T = 310.15$ K:

$$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T} = \frac{(2.5)(33.208)}{(0.0821)(310.15)}$$

$$\rho = \frac{83.020}{25.466} = 3.260 \ \text{g/L}$$

Converting to SI: $\rho = 3.260 \ \text{kg/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Ideal Gas Mixture Relationships

The following block-level flow diagram maps the interactions between the Ideal Gas Law, Dalton's Law, Amagat's Law, and the derived quantities (mole fraction, average molecular weight, density) for an ideal gas mixture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Mixture Container] --> B[Define System: Volume V, Temperature T, Total Pressure P]
    B --> C[Identify Component Moles: n1, n2, ..., nn]
    C --> D[Compute Total Moles: ntotal = sum of ni]
    D --> E[Compute Mole Fractions: yi = ni / ntotal]
    E --> F{Daltons Law Pathway}
    E --> G{Amagats Law Pathway}
    F --> F1[Partial Pressure: Pi = yi x Ptotal]
    F1 --> F2[Total Pressure: Ptotal = sum of Pi]
    G --> G1[Partial Volume: Vi = yi x Vtotal]
    G1 --> G2[Total Volume: Vtotal = sum of Vi]
    F2 --> H[Apply Ideal Gas Law: PV = nRT]
    G2 --> H
    E --> I[Compute Average Molecular Weight: Mavg = sum of yi x Mi]
    I --> J[Compute Density: rho = P x Mavg / RT]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Final Engineering Outputs: OUR, CER, Aeration Rate, Biogas SG]
    F2 --> K
    I --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Gas Constant Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Units of P, V, T, n] --> B{Pressure Unit}
    B -->|atm| C1[Use R = 0.0821 L atm / mol K]
    B -->|kPa or Pa| C2[Use R = 8.314 J / mol K or m3 Pa / mol K]
    B -->|psia| C3[Use R = 10.73 psia ft3 / lbmol R]
    B -->|mmHg or torr| C4[Use R = 62.36 L mmHg / mol K]
    C1 --> D[Apply PV = nRT Consistently]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Solve for Unknown]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix: Real Gas vs. Ideal Gas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Gas and Conditions] --> B{Is Pressure High or Temperature Low?}
    B -->|No - Moderate T and P| C[Treat as Ideal Gas]
    B -->|Yes - High P or Low T| D{Use Compressibility Chart or EOS}
    C --> E[Apply PV = nRT]
    C --> F[Apply Daltons and Amagats Laws]
    D --> G[Use Z Factor: PV = ZnRT]
    D --> H[Use Van der Waals or Redlich Kwong EOS]
    E --> I[Solution]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that *the total pressure exerted by an ideal gas mixture is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual component gases, where each partial pressure is the pressure that the component would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume of the mixture at the same temperature.*

Mathematically: $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = P_1 + P_2 + \cdots + P_n$

The partial pressure of any component $i$ is related to its mole fraction by: $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$

**Limitations:** Applicable only to ideal gas mixtures; deviations occur for real gases at high pressures or low temperatures.

### Question 2: A gas mixture contains 40% H₂ and 60% N₂ by volume. Calculate the average molecular weight of the mixture. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Given: $y_{H_2} = 0.40$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol; $y_{N_2} = 0.60$, $M_{N_2} = 28.02$ g/mol.

Applying the formula: $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$

$$M_{avg} = (0.40)(2.016) + (0.60)(28.02)$$

$$M_{avg} = 0.8064 + 16.812 = 17.618 \ \text{g/mol}$$

**Average molecular weight = 17.62 g/mol**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

A flue gas from a boiler has the following composition by volume: CO₂ = 12%, O₂ = 8%, N₂ = 75%, H₂O = 5%. The gas leaves the boiler at a total pressure of 1 atm and temperature of 200 °C.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the partial pressure of each component, (ii) the average molecular weight of the flue gas mixture.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the density of the flue gas mixture in kg/m³, (ii) the specific gravity of the flue gas relative to air at the same conditions.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Given Data:**
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$ g/mol
- $y_{O_2} = 0.08$, $M_{O_2} = 32.00$ g/mol
- $y_{N_2} = 0.75$, $M_{N_2} = 28.02$ g/mol
- $y_{H_2O} = 0.05$, $M_{H_2O} = 18.02$ g/mol
- $P_{total} = 1$ atm, $T = 200 + 273.15 = 473.15$ K
- $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol

**Part (a) — Partial Pressures and Average Molecular Weight:**

**(i) Partial Pressures (Dalton's Law):**

$$P_{CO_2} = (0.12)(1) = 0.12 \ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{O_2} = (0.08)(1) = 0.08 \ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{N_2} = (0.75)(1) = 0.75 \ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{H_2O} = (0.05)(1) = 0.05 \ \text{atm}$$

**Check:** $0.12 + 0.08 + 0.75 + 0.05 = 1.00$ atm ✓

**(ii) Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{avg} = (0.12)(44.01) + (0.08)(32.00) + (0.75)(28.02) + (0.05)(18.02)$$

$$M_{avg} = 5.2812 + 2.5600 + 21.0150 + 0.9010 = 29.757 \ \text{g/mol}$$

**Part (b) — Density and Specific Gravity:**

**(i) Density using $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K):**

Converting to kg/m³: we use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) = 8.314 Pa·m³/(mol·K) and $P = 101325$ Pa, $M_{avg} = 0.029757$ kg/mol.

$$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T} = \frac{(101325)(0.029757)}{(8.314)(473.15)}$$

$$\rho = \frac{3014.8}{3934.2} = 0.7664 \ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Specific Gravity Relative to Air:**

$$SG = \frac{M_{avg}}{M_{air}} = \frac{29.757}{28.97} = 1.027$$

**Interpretation:** The flue gas is slightly denser (heavier) than air. $SG > 1$ means it will tend to settle in confined spaces — a critical safety consideration in boiler house design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

A bioreactor is supplied with sterile air at a flow rate of 1000 L/min measured at 30 °C and 1.2 atm. The air composition is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the molar flow rate of air in kmol/min, (ii) the molar flow rate of O₂ and N₂ separately, (iii) the mass flow rate of air in kg/min.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the off-gas from the bioreactor contains 18% O₂ and 82% (N₂ + CO₂) by volume on a dry basis, calculate (i) the moles of O₂ consumed per minute, (ii) the moles of CO₂ produced per minute (assuming N₂ is inert), and (iii) the Respiratory Quotient (RQ = CO₂ produced / O₂ consumed).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Given Data:**
- Volumetric flow rate $Q = 1000$ L/min
- $T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K
- $P = 1.2$ atm
- $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K)
- Inlet: $y_{O_2} = 0.21$, $y_{N_2} = 0.79$
- Outlet (dry): $y_{O_2} = 0.18$, $y_{N_2 + CO_2} = 0.82$

**Part (a) — Molar Flow Rate and Composition:**

**(i) Molar Flow Rate of Air (Ideal Gas Law):**

Using $PV = nRT \Rightarrow n = PV/(RT)$:

$$n_{air} = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T} = \frac{(1.2)(1000)}{(0.0821)(303.15)}$$

$$n_{air} = \frac{1200}{24.886} = 48.221 \ \text{mol/min} = 0.04822 \ \text{kmol/min}$$

**(ii) Molar Flow Rates of O₂ and N₂:**

$$n_{O_2,in} = (0.21)(48.221) = 10.126 \ \text{mol/min}$$

$$n_{N_2,in} = (0.79)(48.221) = 38.094 \ \text{mol/min}$$

**(iii) Mass Flow Rate of Air:**

Average molecular weight of inlet air:
$$M_{avg} = (0.21)(32.00) + (0.79)(28.02) = 6.72 + 22.136 = 28.856 \ \text{g/mol}$$

Mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m} = n_{air} \cdot M_{avg} = (48.221)(28.856) = 1391.5 \ \text{g/min} = 1.392 \ \text{kg/min}$$

**Part (b) — Off-Gas Analysis (N₂ Balance and RQ):**

**Key Insight:** Since N₂ is inert, the molar flow rate of N₂ in the off-gas equals that in the inlet. This is the basis of oxygen uptake rate (OUR) calculation in bioprocesses.

**(i) Moles of O₂ Consumed per Minute:**

In off-gas, $y_{N_2,out} = 0.79$ (unchanged because N₂ is conserved and the basis is dry gas where $y_{O_2,out} = 0.18$, $y_{N_2+CO_2} = 0.82$, and N₂ is still 79% of the original basis if we assume only O₂ and CO₂ change — but for a proper dry-basis balance):

For dry off-gas basis: $y_{N_2} = 0.82 - y_{CO_2}$. By N₂ balance:
$$n_{N_2,out} = n_{N_2,in} = 38.094 \ \text{mol/min}$$

Let the total molar flow rate of dry off-gas be $n_{out,dry}$. Then $y_{N_2,out} = 38.094 / n_{out,dry}$.

If $y_{O_2,out} = 0.18$ and the rest is $N_2 + CO_2 = 0.82$, then $y_{N_2,out} + y_{CO_2,out} = 0.82$. The N₂ fraction in the dry off-gas is not necessarily 0.79 — we need the N₂ balance:

We assume that all O₂ consumed appears as CO₂ (carbon balance is separate). For this simplified problem, $y_{N_2,out}$ in the dry off-gas is found from $n_{N_2,out}/n_{out,dry}$.

Given the problem says the off-gas is 18% O₂ and 82% (N₂ + CO₂), the simplest assumption is that the split between N₂ and CO₂ within that 82% is determined by the N₂ balance. If we assume $y_{CO_2,out} = $ unknown, then $y_{N_2,out} = 0.82 - y_{CO_2,out}$.

Without additional carbon-balance information, we can simplify: assume $y_{N_2,out} = 0.79$ (unchanged mole fraction basis if no dilution), so $y_{CO_2,out} = 0.82 - 0.79 = 0.03$. Then:

$$n_{out,dry} = \frac{n_{N_2}}{y_{N_2}} = \frac{38.094}{0.79} = 48.220 \ \text{mol/min}$$

$$n_{O_2,out} = (0.18)(48.220) = 8.680 \ \text{mol/min}$$

**O₂ consumed per minute:**
$$n_{O_2,consumed} = n_{O_2,in} - n_{O_2,out} = 10.126 - 8.680 = 1.446 \ \text{mol/min}$$

**(ii) Moles of CO₂ Produced per Minute:**

$$n_{CO_2,out} = (0.03)(48.220) = 1.447 \ \text{mol/min}$$

(Verification: O₂ balance $1.446$ ≈ CO₂ produced $1.447$ — they are nearly equal in this problem.)

**(iii) Respiratory Quotient:**

$$RQ = \frac{n_{CO_2,produced}}{n_{O_2,consumed}} = \frac{1.447}{1.446} = 1.00$$

**Interpretation:** $RQ \approx 1.0$ indicates carbohydrate-based metabolism (e.g., glucose oxidation), which is common in many industrial fermentations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Errors (Most Common -2 to -3 marks):** Students often mix units (using R = 0.0821 with temperature in °C, or R = 8.314 with volume in liters). Always check P, V, T, n units BEFORE picking R.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert °C to K:** Temperature in the Ideal Gas Law MUST always be in Kelvin. Adding 273 (not 273.15) loses 0.5 marks; using °C directly loses full marks.
> 3. **Confusing Partial Pressure and Partial Volume:** In ideal gases both work with $y_i$, but in real gases or liquid solutions they DIFFER.
> 4. **Not Verifying Mole Fractions Sum to 1:** Always check $\sum y_i = 1$. Examiners often give deliberately tricky data to test this.
> 5. **Sign Error in O₂ Consumption:** Forgetting that O₂ consumed = O₂ in − O₂ out (it's NOT a sum).
> 6. **Confusing Dry and Wet Basis in Off-Gas Analysis:** Always clarify whether the given mole fractions are on a dry basis (water removed) or wet basis.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Ideal Gas Mixtures and Gas Constant:**

- ✅ **Ideal Gas Law:** $PV = nRT$ — the foundation of all gas calculations.
- ✅ **Universal Gas Constant:** Always carry the units: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K).
- ✅ **Dalton's Law:** $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ — partial pressures are additive.
- ✅ **Amagat's Law:** $V_{total} = \sum V_i$ — partial volumes are additive.
- ✅ **Mole Fraction:** $y_i = n_i / n_{total} = P_i / P_{total} = V_i / V_{total}$ — the master intensive variable.
- ✅ **Partial Pressure:** $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ — most-used relation in KTU problems.
- ✅ **Partial Volume:** $V_i = y_i \cdot V_{total}$ — useful for Amagat's Law problems.
- ✅ **Average Molecular Weight:** $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ — weighted average of pure-component molecular weights.
- ✅ **Density of Gas:** $\rho = PM_{avg} / (RT)$ — used in mass balance and flow calculations.
- ✅ **Specific Gravity:** $SG = M_{avg} / M_{air}$, with $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol.
- ✅ **Mole Fractions Sum to Unity:** $\sum y_i = 1$ — always verify.
- ✅ **STP vs NTP:** Know the difference: STP = 273.15 K, 1 atm; NTP = 293.15 K, 1 atm.
- ✅ **Real Gas Limitation:** Ideal gas law fails at high P, low T; check with compressibility factor $Z = PV/(nRT)$.
- ✅ **Bioprocess Application:** Air supply to fermenters, off-gas analysis, OUR, CER, RQ calculations.
- ✅ **Common Conversion Factors:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psia.
- ✅ **N₂ as Inert Tracer:** In off-gas analysis, N₂ flow is conserved; this is the basis for OUR/CER calculation.
- ✅ **N₂ Inlet = N₂ Outlet:** This is the key mass balance used in bioprocess gas analysis.

**Key Formula Triad to Memorize:**
$$\boxed{P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total} \quad ; \quad M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i \quad ; \quad \rho = \frac{P M_{avg}}{RT}}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition of Gases on Dry and Wet Basis, Average Molecular Weight, and Gas Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Composition of Gases on Dry and Wet Basis, Average Molecular Weight, and Gas Density ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Bioprocess Calculations**, the term **"Composition of Gases"** refers to the quantitative description of the fractional amounts of individual gaseous components (e.g., O₂, CO₂, N₂, H₂O vapor) present in a gaseous mixture. This composition can be expressed in two fundamentally distinct reference frameworks: **a) Composition on a Dry Basis (yᵢᵈʳʸ):** The mole fraction of component *i* in the gas mixture when **water vapor is completely excluded** from both the numerator and the denominator of the fractional composition. The dry basis reports what the gas would look like if all moisture were removed. Mathematically: $$y_i^{dry} = \frac{\text{moles of component } i}{\text{total moles of all non-water components}}$$ By definition: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{dry} = 1.0$$ **b) Composition on a Wet Basis (yᵢʷᵉᵗ):** The mole fraction of component *i* in the gas mixture when **water vapor is included** as a regular component. This represents the actual, real-world gas mixture as it exists in a fermenter off-gas, humid air line, or exhaust stream. $$y_i^{wet} = \frac{\text{moles of component } i}{\text{total moles of all components (including } H_2O\text{)}}$$ By definition: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{wet} + y_{H_2O}^{wet} = 1.0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In bioprocess engineering, gases leaving a fermenter are typically saturated (or near-saturated) with water vapor. The off-gas analyzer in a bioreactor **measures only the dry composition**, but the gas in the headspace is *wet*. The student must therefore master the interconversion between these two bases to perform mass balances correctly. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are at a fruit juice stall with two different ways the vendor could describe his drink: - **Dry Basis Analogy:** "My juice is 30% mango, 30% orange, 40% apple" — but he is **ignoring the ice/water** in the glass. This is *dry* reporting: components of interest are normalized among themselves. - **Wet Basis Analogy:** "My juice is 25% mango, 25% orange, 35% apple, and 15% water" — this is the **whole truth**, including the water. The fractions here are smaller because water takes up some of the share. In a bioreactor, the **inlet air (sparger) is humidified** (wet basis), but the **mass spectrometer gas analyzer cannot "see" water vapor** and so it gives a dry basis reading. Engineers must convert one to the other to close the mass balance. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** All gas composition in KTU calculations is expressed in **mole fractions (dimensionless, sum to 1)** unless explicitly stated as mass fractions. For ideal gases, mole fraction equals volume fraction equals partial pressure fraction (Dalton's Law). ### 1.3 Definition of Related Core Quantities **Average Molecular Weight (Mₐᵥ𝓰):** The weighted mean of the molecular weights of all components in a gas mixture, weighted by their mole fractions. $$\boxed{M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot M_i}$$ **Gas Density (ρ):** The mass of the gas per unit volume, which depends on pressure, temperature, and composition through the **Ideal Gas Law**. $$\boxed{\rho_{gas} = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}}$$ where: - $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa or atm) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $M_{avg}$ = average molecular weight (kg/kmol or g/mol) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Two Bases Exist? In bioprocess operations, water is a **ubiquitous component** that behaves differently from other gases: - Water condenses, evaporates, and shifts phase with temperature changes. - Gas analyzers (IR, paramagnetic, MS) often **fail to detect water vapor** reliably. - Fermenter headspace gases are inherently **humid** (often 100% relative humidity). - Drying, humidification, and sterilization operations involve water as a key mass transfer agent. Because of this special role of water, the engineer must distinguish whether water is counted as a "component of interest" or as a "carrier background." ### 2.2 Logical Steps for Interconversion (Dry ↔ Wet) **Step 1 — Identify the mole fraction of water vapor in the wet basis.** If $y_{H_2O}$ is the mole fraction of water in the wet mixture: $$y_{H_2O}^{wet} = \frac{n_{H_2O}}{n_{total}}$$ **Step 2 — Recognize the dry basis as the wet basis with water removed.** When water is stripped out, the remaining moles are $(1 - y_{H_2O})$ of the original total. Therefore, any component $i$ in the dry basis is the ratio of its moles to the *non-water* moles: $$y_i^{dry} = \frac{y_i^{wet}}{1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet}}$$ **Step 3 — Reverse the conversion (dry → wet).** If dry-basis compositions and water mole fraction are known: $$y_i^{wet} = y_i^{dry} \cdot \left(1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Verify the sum.** $$\sum_{i \neq H_2O} y_i^{dry} = 1.0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i \text{ (all)}} y_i^{wet} = 1.0$$ ### 2.3 Why Use Average Molecular Weight? A gas mixture has a *single effective* molecular weight that represents the entire mixture as if it were a single component. This is essential for: - **Ideal gas law calculations** (PV = nRT, ρ = PM/RT) - **Mass flow rate computations** from volumetric flow measurements - **Stokes' law / settling velocity** in gas-solid systems - **Material balance closure** on a molar or mass basis ### 2.4 Gas Density — Physical Insight The density of a gas depends on three independent factors: 1. **Pressure (P)** — higher P → molecules packed tighter → higher ρ 2. **Temperature (T)** — higher T → molecules move faster → spread out → lower ρ 3. **Average molecular weight (Mₐᵥ𝓰)** — heavier molecules → more mass per mole → higher ρ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Gas density is used to size **blowers, compressors, gas filters, and vent lines** in a bioprocess plant. It also determines the **buoyancy** of gas bubbles in fermenter broth, which affects kLa (volumetric mass transfer coefficient). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions | |---|---------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | $y_i^{dry} = \dfrac{y_i^{wet}}{1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet}}$ | Wet → Dry conversion | Dimensionless | | 2 | $y_i^{wet} = y_i^{dry} \cdot (1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet})$ | Dry → Wet conversion | Dimensionless | | 3 | $M_{avg} = \sum_i y_i \cdot M_i$ | Average molecular weight (mole-fraction weighted) | kg/kmol or g/mol | | 4 | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}}$ | Average MW from mass fractions | kg/kmol | | 5 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | Ideal gas law | P in Pa, V in m³, n in mol | | 6 | $\rho_{gas} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}$ | Gas density (ideal gas) | kg/m³ | | 7 | $C_i = \dfrac{y_i \cdot P}{R \cdot T}$ | Molar concentration of component $i$ | mol/m³ or kmol/m³ | | 8 | $\dot{m} = \rho_{gas} \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s | | 9 | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{P \cdot \dot{V}}{R \cdot T}$ | Molar flow rate | kmol/s | | 10 | $\dot{m}_i = \dot{n} \cdot y_i \cdot M_i$ | Mass flow rate of component $i$ | kg/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students use $R = 0.0821$ and forget to convert pressure to **atm** or temperature to **K**. Always verify units before substituting. The most exam-safe value is **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** with P in Pa and V in m³. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wet-to-Dry Basis Conversion Formula **Starting Point:** Consider a gas mixture of total moles $n_T$, with $n_i$ moles of component $i$ and $n_w$ moles of water vapor. **Step 1:** Express the wet-basis mole fraction: $$y_i^{wet} = \frac{n_i}{n_T} \quad \text{and} \quad y_w^{wet} = \frac{n_w}{n_T}$$ **Step 2:** The total dry (non-water) moles after removing water: $$n_{dry} = n_T - n_w = n_T \cdot (1 - y_w^{wet})$$ **Step 3:** The dry-basis mole fraction is defined on the dry moles: $$y_i^{dry} = \frac{n_i}{n_{dry}} = \frac{n_i}{n_T \cdot (1 - y_w^{wet})} = \frac{y_i^{wet}}{1 - y_w^{wet}}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad y_i^{dry} = \frac{y_i^{wet}}{1 - y_w^{wet}}}$$ **Step 4 — Algebraic Reverse Derivation (Dry → Wet):** Multiply both sides of the above by $(1 - y_w^{wet})$: $$y_i^{dry} \cdot (1 - y_w^{wet}) = y_i^{wet}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad y_i^{wet} = y_i^{dry} \cdot (1 - y_w^{wet})}$$ **Step 5 — Summation Check:** Summing over all non-water components: $$\sum_{i \neq w} y_i^{dry} = \sum_{i \neq w} \frac{y_i^{wet}}{1 - y_w^{wet}} = \frac{1}{1 - y_w^{wet}} \cdot \sum_{i \neq w} y_i^{wet} = \frac{1 - y_w^{wet}}{1 - y_w^{wet}} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Molecular Weight from Mole Fractions **Starting Point:** Let $n_i$ be the moles of component $i$ in a mixture of total moles $n_T = \sum_i n_i$. The total mass of the mixture is $m_T = \sum_i n_i \cdot M_i$. **Step 1:** Definition of average molecular weight (mass per mole of mixture): $$M_{avg} = \frac{m_T}{n_T} = \frac{\sum_i n_i \cdot M_i}{n_T}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute mole fraction $y_i = n_i / n_T$: $$M_{avg} = \sum_i \frac{n_i}{n_T} \cdot M_i = \sum_i y_i \cdot M_i$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad M_{avg} = \sum_i y_i \cdot M_i}$$ **Step 3 — Alternative Form (Mass Fraction Basis):** If $w_i$ is the mass fraction of component $i$, then $w_i = y_i M_i / M_{avg}$, so: $$w_i = \frac{y_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_j y_j \cdot M_j}$$ Inverting this relationship: $$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gas Density from Ideal Gas Law **Starting Point:** The ideal gas law for $n$ moles occupying volume $V$ at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is: $$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$ **Step 1:** Density is mass per volume: $$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{n \cdot M_{avg}}{V}$$ **Step 2:** Solve the ideal gas law for $n/V$: $$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the density expression: $$\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T} \cdot M_{avg}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad \rho = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}}$$ ### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A bioreactor off-gas analyzer reports the dry-basis composition of the exhaust gas as follows: O₂ = 18.5%, CO₂ = 4.2%, N₂ = 77.3%. The off-gas leaves the fermenter saturated with water at 37 °C and 1.013 bar. Calculate: (a) The wet-basis mole fractions of all components. (b) The average molecular weight on a wet basis. (c) The density of the off-gas. **Given Data:** - $y_{O_2}^{dry} = 0.185$, $y_{CO_2}^{dry} = 0.042$, $y_{N_2}^{dry} = 0.773$ - Saturation condition: $y_{H_2O}$ at 37 °C → from steam tables, $P_{H_2O}^{sat}(37°C) \approx 0.0628$ bar **Solution:** **Part (a) — Wet-basis mole fractions:** First, find the mole fraction of water vapor in the wet gas (using Dalton's Law at saturation): $$y_{H_2O}^{wet} = \frac{P_{H_2O}^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.0628 \text{ bar}}{1.013 \text{ bar}} = 0.0620$$ Apply the conversion formula $y_i^{wet} = y_i^{dry} \cdot (1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet})$: $$y_{O_2}^{wet} = 0.185 \times (1 - 0.0620) = 0.185 \times 0.9380 = 0.1735$$ $$y_{CO_2}^{wet} = 0.042 \times 0.9380 = 0.0394$$ $$y_{N_2}^{wet} = 0.773 \times 0.9380 = 0.7251$$ **Verification of sum:** $$0.1735 + 0.0394 + 0.7251 + 0.0620 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark$$ **Part (b) — Average Molecular Weight (wet basis):** $$M_{avg} = \sum_i y_i \cdot M_i$$ $$M_{avg} = (0.1735)(32) + (0.0394)(44) + (0.7251)(28) + (0.0620)(18)$$ Computing each term: $$= 5.552 + 1.734 + 20.303 + 1.116$$ $$M_{avg} = 28.705 \text{ g/mol} \approx 28.71 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Part (c) — Gas Density:** Using $R = 0.08314 \text{ L·bar/(mol·K)}$, $P = 1.013$ bar, $T = 310$ K: $$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T} = \frac{1.013 \times 28.71}{0.08314 \times 310}$$ $$\rho = \frac{29.083}{25.773} = 1.128 \text{ g/L} = 1.128 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Dry vs. Wet Basis Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Real Fermenter Off-Gas
WET BASIS
contains H2O vapor"] --> nodeB{"Gas Analyzer
Cannot Detect H2O"} nodeB --> nodeC["DRY BASIS
Reported Composition
sum of non-water = 1"] nodeA --> nodeD["Mole fraction of H2O
from saturation P"] nodeC --> nodeE["Conversion Formula
y_wet = y_dry x 1 - y_H2O"] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF["WET BASIS
True Composition
sum of all = 1"] nodeF --> nodeG["M_avg = sum y_i M_i"] nodeG --> nodeH["Density rho = P M_avg / R T"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Architecture: Gas Property Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA1["Input: Dry Composition
y_i_dry and y_H2O"] --> nodeB1["Step 1
Convert to Wet Basis
y_i_wet = y_i_dry x 1 - y_H2O"] nodeB1 --> nodeC1["Step 2
Verify Sum = 1"] nodeC1 --> nodeD1["Step 3
Compute M_avg
weighted sum"] nodeD1 --> nodeE1["Step 4
Apply Ideal Gas Law
rho = P M_avg / RT"] nodeE1 --> nodeF1["Output: rho kg per m3"] nodeD1 --> nodeG1["Step 5
Molar Flow
n_dot = P V_dot / RT"] nodeG1 --> nodeH1["Step 6
Component Mass Flow
m_dot_i = n_dot y_i M_i"] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Basis | Scenario | Correct Basis | Reason | |----------|---------------|--------| | Off-gas analyzer output | **Dry** | Analyzer cannot see water | | Fermenter headspace gas | **Wet** | Water vapor is present | | Inlet air (sparger) above bubble point | **Wet** | Humidified air | | Drying air (after heater) | **Dry** | Water is removed by desiccant | | Combustion stoichiometry | **Dry** | Products measured dry | | Bioreactor inlet to OUR/CER calculation | **Wet** | Actual mass transfer basis | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Composition on dry basis (ii) Composition on wet basis. **Model Answer:** **(i) Dry basis composition** is the mole fraction of a gaseous component computed by excluding water vapor from both numerator and denominator. The dry-basis fractions of all non-water components sum to 1. **Example:** Off-gas analyzer reading of $O_2$ = 19%, $CO_2$ = 3%, $N_2$ = 78% on dry basis. **(ii) Wet basis composition** is the mole fraction computed by including water vapor as a normal component. All fractions (including water) sum to 1. **Example:** Inlet air saturated at 30 °C and 1 atm has $y_{H_2O} = 0.0426$, $y_{O_2} = 0.204$, $y_{N_2} = 0.7534$ on wet basis. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]** A gas mixture contains 60% N₂ and 40% O₂ on a mole basis. Calculate the average molecular weight of the mixture. **Model Answer:** $$M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.60)(28) + (0.40)(32) = 16.8 + 12.8 = 29.6 \text{ g/mol}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **(a)** A flue gas analysis reports the following on dry basis: CO₂ = 12%, O₂ = 6%, N₂ = 82%. If the gas is saturated with water at 60 °C and 1 atm, determine the wet-basis composition and average molecular weight. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the density of air at 25 °C and 1.013 bar, assuming the composition is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ on a dry basis. Take $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/(mol·K). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Wet-basis composition and average MW:** Saturation pressure of water at 60 °C: $P_{H_2O}^{sat} = 0.1992$ bar. $$y_{H_2O}^{wet} = \frac{0.1992}{1.013} = 0.1967$$ Dry-to-wet conversion: $$y_{CO_2}^{wet} = 0.12 \times (1 - 0.1967) = 0.12 \times 0.8033 = 0.0964$$ $$y_{O_2}^{wet} = 0.06 \times 0.8033 = 0.0482$$ $$y_{N_2}^{wet} = 0.82 \times 0.8033 = 0.6587$$ Sum check: $0.0964 + 0.0482 + 0.6587 + 0.1967 = 1.0000$ ✓ Average molecular weight (wet basis): $$M_{avg} = (0.0964)(44) + (0.0482)(32) + (0.6587)(28) + (0.1967)(18)$$ $$= 4.242 + 1.542 + 18.444 + 3.541 = 27.77 \text{ g/mol}$$ **(b) Density of air at 25 °C, 1.013 bar:** Assuming dry air (since not specified as humid), $M_{avg} = (0.21)(32) + (0.79)(28) = 6.72 + 22.12 = 28.84$ g/mol. $$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T} = \frac{1.013 \times 28.84}{0.08314 \times 298.15}$$ $$\rho = \frac{29.215}{24.788} = 1.179 \text{ g/L} = 1.179 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]** **(a)** A gas stream entering a fermenter has wet-basis composition: O₂ = 20.5%, N₂ = 77.0%, CO₂ = 0.3%, H₂O = 2.2%. Convert this to a dry-basis composition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The exit gas from a fermentation reactor at 35 °C and 1.05 bar has a dry-basis composition of: O₂ = 16.0%, CO₂ = 5.5%, N₂ = 78.5%. If the gas is water-saturated at the exit temperature, compute the density of the wet exit gas. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Dry-basis conversion:** $$y_{H_2O}^{wet} = 0.022 \implies (1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet}) = 0.978$$ $$y_{O_2}^{dry} = \frac{0.205}{0.978} = 0.2096$$ $$y_{N_2}^{dry} = \frac{0.770}{0.978} = 0.7873$$ $$y_{CO_2}^{dry} = \frac{0.003}{0.978} = 0.0031$$ Sum check: $0.2096 + 0.7873 + 0.0031 = 1.0000$ ✓ **(b) Density of wet exit gas:** At 35 °C, $P_{H_2O}^{sat} \approx 0.0563$ bar. $$y_{H_2O}^{wet} = \frac{0.0563}{1.05} = 0.0536$$ Wet-basis mole fractions: $$y_{O_2}^{wet} = 0.160 \times (1 - 0.0536) = 0.160 \times 0.9464 = 0.1514$$ $$y_{CO_2}^{wet} = 0.055 \times 0.9464 = 0.0521$$ $$y_{N_2}^{wet} = 0.785 \times 0.9464 = 0.7429$$ Average molecular weight: $$M_{avg} = (0.1514)(32) + (0.0521)(44) + (0.7429)(28) + (0.0536)(18)$$ $$= 4.845 + 2.292 + 20.801 + 0.965 = 28.903 \text{ g/mol}$$ Density (using $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/mol·K, $T = 308$ K): $$\rho = \frac{1.05 \times 28.903}{0.08314 \times 308} = \frac{30.348}{25.607} = 1.185 \text{ g/L} = 1.185 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract water:** When converting wet → dry, students often use the wrong denominator. The correct denominator is $(1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet})$, not $(1 + y_{H_2O}^{wet})$. > 2. **Unit inconsistency in R:** Using $R = 0.0821$ with pressure in Pa or $R = 8.314$ with pressure in atm is the most common cause of numerical errors. **Match R to the units of P and V.** > 3. **Temperature in °C vs K:** KTU answers that use 308 instead of 308.15 lose 0.5 marks; using 35 (°C) directly is a fatal error. > 4. **Ignoring water in M_avg:** Forgetting the water term in the weighted sum produces a wrong density. Always include *all four* components when computing $M_{avg}$ on wet basis. > 5. **Sum ≠ 1 check:** A correct dry-basis sum should equal 1.0; a correct wet-basis sum should also equal 1.0. Students who skip this verification often carry forward errors. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Dry basis** excludes water vapor; **wet basis** includes it. The two are linked by the water mole fraction. - The conversion formula $y_i^{dry} = y_i^{wet} / (1 - y_{H_2O}^{wet})$ is the **single most tested equation** in this topic. - **Average molecular weight** is the mole-fraction-weighted sum of component molecular weights: $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$. - **Gas density** for ideal gases follows $\rho = PM_{avg}/RT$, requiring P in absolute units, T in Kelvin, and R matched to those units. - Off-gas analyzers report **dry** values; fermenter headspace gas is inherently **wet**; hence conversion is a routine bioprocess step. - Saturation water mole fraction: $y_{H_2O} = P_{H_2O}^{sat}(T) / P_{total}$ (Dalton's Law). - **Verify your sum:** dry-basis non-water fractions sum to 1; wet-basis (including water) fractions sum to 1. - Common molecular weights to memorize: $O_2$ = 32, $N_2$ = 28, $CO_2$ = 44, $H_2O$ = 18, Air ≈ 28.97, $CH_4$ = 16. - **KTU 14-mark problems** typically couple (a) a basis-conversion + average MW with (b) a density or mass-flow calculation, requiring consistent unit handling throughout.

topics.title: Humidity: Terms Associated with Humidity and Saturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Humidity: Terms Associated with Humidity and Saturation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Humidity in Bioprocess Engineering?

**Humidity** is the measure of the amount of water vapour present in a gas (most commonly air). In bioprocess engineering, humidity plays a critical role in operations such as **fermentation air sterilization**, **drying of biological products**, **air conditioning of clean rooms**, **evaporative cooling**, and **design of bioreactors and solid-state fermentation systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Humidity is the general term used to describe the water vapour content of a gas mixture. When the carrier gas is air, the system is referred to as a *humid air* system, and the study of its thermodynamic properties is called **Psychrometry**.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — A "Sponge in the Air"

Imagine the air as a giant dry sponge. This sponge has a fixed capacity to soak up water vapour. On a **hot, sunny day**, the sponge expands (warm air holds more water) — so it can soak a lot more water. On a **cold, foggy morning**, the sponge shrinks (cold air holds less water) — it quickly becomes fully soaked, and the excess water condenses out as **dew**.

- **Dry sponge** → Dry air (low humidity)
- **Fully soaked sponge** → Saturated air (Relative Humidity = 100%)
- **Half-soaked sponge** → Air with 50% RH
- **Water dripping from sponge** → Dew point reached (condensation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in Bioprocess Calculations?**
> In aerobic fermentation, **sterile air** must be supplied to microorganisms. The amount of water this air carries affects **mass balance**, **heat balance**, **oxygen partial pressure**, and **downstream drying energy calculations**. Therefore, *every* bioprocess engineer must master humidity calculations.

### 1.3 The System: Gas–Vapour Mixture

A humid gas (typically air) is a binary mixture:
- **Dry gas (air):** $N_2$ + $O_2$ + traces of $CO_2$, $Ar$, etc.
- **Water vapour (the condensable component)**

The **Dalton's Law** governs the total pressure:
$$P_{total} = P_{dry\,air} + P_{water\,vapour} = P_a + P_w$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Atmospheric Reference:** At sea level, **$P_{total}$ = 101.325 kPa = 1 atm = 760 mm Hg**. This is the reference pressure for nearly all humidity calculations in the KTU syllabus unless stated otherwise.
> **Universal Gas Constant:** $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**.

### 1.4 The Core Property — Saturation

A gas is said to be **saturated** with a vapour when it contains the **maximum possible amount** of that vapour at the given temperature and pressure. Any attempt to add more vapour will result in **condensation** (liquid forms).

- **Saturation humidity** ($H_s$ or $H_{sat}$): The maximum humidity a gas can hold at temperature $T$.
- **Saturation pressure / Vapour pressure of water** ($P_w^*$ or $P_s$): The partial pressure of water vapour when the gas is saturated at temperature $T$. It depends **only on temperature**, not on the amount of air present.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Humidity Terminology

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **eight core terms**. Let us build them in a logical hierarchy.

---

#### A. **Absolute Humidity (AH) / Moisture Content ($H$ or $Y$)**

The **mass of water vapour carried per unit mass of bone-dry air**.

$$H = \frac{\text{mass of water vapour}}{\text{mass of dry air}} = \frac{m_w}{m_a}$$

Using ideal gas law at the same $T$ and $P$:

$$H = \frac{18 \cdot P_w}{29 \cdot (P - P_w)} = \frac{18}{29} \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_a}$$

- **18** = Molecular weight of water
- **29** = Equivalent molecular weight of dry air
- $P_w$ = Partial pressure of water vapour
- $P_a = P - P_w$ = Partial pressure of dry air

---

#### B. **Molar (Molal) Absolute Humidity ($H_m$)**

The ratio of **moles of water vapour** to **moles of dry gas**. Useful in stoichiometric and equilibrium calculations.

$$H_m = \frac{n_w}{n_a} = \frac{P_w}{P_a} = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion:** $H = \dfrac{18}{29} \cdot H_m$. This conversion is a *favourite* 3-mark question setter in KTU exams.

---

#### C. **Relative Humidity (RH) / Relative Saturation ($\phi$ or $H_R$)**

The ratio of the **actual partial pressure of water vapour** in the gas to the **saturation pressure of water** at the same temperature.

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_w^*(T)} \times 100\%$$

where $P_w^*(T)$ is read from the steam tables at temperature $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **RH is temperature-dependent.** Even if the absolute amount of water is constant, RH drops as temperature rises (because $P_w^*$ increases with $T$).

---

#### D. **Percentage Humidity ($H_p$ or % Saturation)**

Defined as the ratio of the **partial pressure of water vapour** to the **vapour pressure of water at gas temperature**, taken on a *percentage* basis. For an ideal gas, $H_p$ is **numerically equal to RH** when expressed as a percentage. However, in some textbooks, percentage humidity is defined as:

$$H_p = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100\%$$

where $H_s$ is the **humidity at saturation** at the same temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** For an ideal gas system, $H_p \approx \phi$. For a non-ideal system (high pressure), they differ slightly. KTU 2024 focuses on the **ideal gas** assumption.

---

#### E. **Dew Point ($T_{dp}$ or $T_{dew}$)**

The temperature at which a humid gas becomes **saturated** when cooled at *constant pressure*. Below the dew point, water begins to **condense** as liquid (dew).

- At dew point: $P_w \text{ (at gas temperature)} = P_w^* \text{ (at dew point)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:** Dew point is the most reliable indicator of absolute moisture content. Cooling and condensation equipment (e.g., dehumidifiers, heat exchangers in fermentation plants) are sized based on dew point.

---

#### F. **Humid Heat ($C_s$ or $C_H$)**

The **heat capacity** of 1 kg of **dry air plus the water vapour associated with it**. Units: **kJ/(kg dry air·K)**.

$$C_s = C_{p,\,air} + H \cdot C_{p,\,vapour}$$

Standard values at moderate temperatures:
- $C_{p,\,air} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $C_{p,\,water\,vapour} = 1.884 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

So:
$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H$$

---

#### G. **Humid Volume ($V_H$ or $v_H$)**

The **volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air** plus the water vapour it carries, measured at the gas conditions ($T$, $P$). Units: **m³/kg dry air**.

$$V_H = \frac{22.4}{273} \cdot \left( \frac{T}{P - P_w} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{1}{29} + \frac{H}{18} \right)$$

Or equivalently:
$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H) \cdot \left(\frac{T}{288}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{101.325}{P}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> The first term (0.00283) is the molar volume of dry air at STP/standard conditions, and 0.00456 is the corresponding term for water vapour, scaled by the molecular weight ratio.

---

#### H. **Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($T_{as}$)**

The temperature attained when a gas is brought into contact with its own liquid (water) in an **adiabatic** (no heat loss) chamber, with continuous circulation, until equilibrium is reached. At this point, the gas leaves **saturated** at temperature $T_{as}$.

This is a *thermodynamic* construct and is the basis of the **psychrometric chart**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas cover ~80% of KTU numerical problems on humidity.**

| # | Term | Formula | Units | Key Condition |
|---|------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Absolute Humidity ($H$) | $H = \frac{18 \, P_w}{29 \, (P - P_w)}$ | kg water/kg dry air | $T$, $P$ constant |
| 2 | Molar Humidity ($H_m$) | $H_m = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}$ | kmol water/kmol dry air | Ideal gas |
| 3 | Relative Humidity ($\phi$) | $\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_w^*(T)}$ | dimensionless (%) | Same temperature |
| 4 | Saturation Humidity ($H_s$) | $H_s = \frac{18 \, P_w^*(T)}{29 \, (P - P_w^*(T))}$ | kg water/kg dry air | Saturated gas at $T$ |
| 5 | Percentage Humidity ($H_p$) | $H_p = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100$ | % | Comparison at same $T$ |
| 6 | Dew Point | $P_w \big\vert_{T_{gas}} = P_w^* \big\vert_{T_{dp}}$ | °C or K | $P$ constant |
| 7 | Humid Heat ($C_s$) | $C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H$ | kJ/(kg dry air·K) | Constant $P$ |
| 8 | Humid Volume ($V_H$) | $V_H = \frac{RT}{P_a \cdot M_a} + \frac{RT}{P_a \cdot M_w} \cdot H$ | m³/kg dry air | At gas $T$, $P$ |
| 9 | Gas Density ($\rho$) | $\rho = \frac{P \cdot M_{mix}}{RT}$ | kg/m³ | Ideal gas |
| 10 | Mol. Weight of Humid Air | $M_{mix} = \frac{1}{\frac{y_a}{M_a} + \frac{y_w}{M_w}}$ | kg/kmol | Dalton's Law |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table rows, vertical bars (such as in $P_w \vert T$) are written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Aerobic Fermentation** | Calculating water removal rate in air filters; sizing of inlet air humidification |
| **Spray Dryers** | Determining inlet/outlet humidity to compute evaporation load |
| **Lyophilization (Freeze Drying)** | Designing vacuum chambers based on residual moisture / dew point |
| **HVAC Cleanrooms** | Maintaining humidity for cell culture and aseptic operations |
| **Cooling Towers** | Predicting evaporative losses using wet-bulb and adiabatic saturation |
| **Solid-State Fermentation** | Moisture control of substrate bed via humid air |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Absolute Humidity from Partial Pressures

**Starting point:** For an ideal gas, the **moles** are proportional to **partial pressure** at fixed $T$ and $V$ (from PV = nRT, so n ∝ P).

**Step 1:** Moles of water vapour: $n_w = \dfrac{P_w \, V}{RT}$

**Step 2:** Moles of dry air: $n_a = \dfrac{P_a \, V}{RT} = \dfrac{(P - P_w) \, V}{RT}$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio (V and RT cancel out beautifully):

$$\frac{n_w}{n_a} = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} = H_m$$

**Step 4:** Convert to **mass ratio** by multiplying with molecular weights:

$$H = \frac{n_w \cdot M_w}{n_a \cdot M_a} = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} \cdot \frac{18}{29}$$

$$H = \frac{18 \cdot P_w}{29 \cdot (P - P_w)}$$

This is the **master equation** for absolute humidity in the KTU 2024 syllabus. **Box this in your mind.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Relative Humidity and Absolute Humidity

Given:
- $P_w = \phi \cdot P_w^*$
- $P_a = P - P_w = P - \phi \cdot P_w^*$

Substitute into the $H$ formula:

$$H = \frac{18 \cdot (\phi \cdot P_w^*)}{29 \cdot (P - \phi \cdot P_w^*)}$$

For **saturated gas**, $\phi = 1$:

$$H_s = \frac{18 \cdot P_w^*}{29 \cdot (P - P_w^*)}$$

Therefore, the **percentage humidity** is:

$$H_p = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100 = \frac{\phi \cdot P_w^* \cdot (P - P_w^*)}{(P - \phi \cdot P_w^*) \cdot P_w^*} \times 100$$

$$H_p = \frac{\phi \, (P - P_w^*)}{P - \phi \, P_w^*} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** For low humidity systems (e.g., $P_w^* \ll P$), the approximation $H_p \approx \phi$ holds. The full equation is required when $P_w^*$ is a significant fraction of $P$ (e.g., near boiling point of water).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Humid Heat — Heat Capacity of Moist Air

**Energy balance** for 1 kg of dry air carrying $H$ kg of water vapour:

Total heat capacity (per kg dry air) = heat capacity of dry air + heat capacity of water vapour it carries:

$$C_s = C_{p,\,dry\,air} \cdot (1 \text{ kg}) + C_{p,\,vapour} \cdot (H \text{ kg})$$

$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H \quad \left[ \text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)} \right]$$

**Logical interpretation:** $C_s$ answers the question — *"How much heat must I add to raise 1 kg of dry air + its associated moisture by 1 K?"*

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Humid Volume — From Mole Concept

For 1 kg of dry air:
- Moles of dry air: $n_a = \dfrac{1}{29} = 0.0345$ kmol
- Moles of water vapour carried: $n_w = \dfrac{H}{18}$ kmol

Total moles in the mixture (per kg dry air):
$$n_{total} = \frac{1}{29} + \frac{H}{18}$$

Applying ideal gas law at $T$ and $P_a$ (partial pressure of dry air):

$$V_H = \frac{n_{total} \cdot R \cdot T}{P_a}$$

where $P_a = P - P_w = P \left(1 - \dfrac{P_w}{P}\right)$. After algebraic manipulation and using $R = 0.08206$ m³·atm/(kmol·K):

$$V_H = \frac{RT}{P - P_w} \left( \frac{1}{29} + \frac{H}{18} \right)$$

At **standard conditions** ($T = 288$ K, $P = 1$ atm, $P_w = 0$):

$$V_H^{\circ} = 0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H \quad \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

Correcting to actual $T$ and $P$:

$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H) \cdot \left(\frac{T}{288}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{101.325}{P}\right)$$

---

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

#### **Example 1 — Finding Humidity from Partial Pressure**

> A sample of air at 80 °C and 1 atm has a partial pressure of water vapour equal to 47.4 kPa. Calculate the absolute humidity, molar humidity, and percentage humidity.
> (Vapour pressure of water at 80 °C = 47.4 kPa)

**Given:**
- $T = 80°$C = 353 K
- $P = 1$ atm = 101.325 kPa
- $P_w = 47.4$ kPa
- $P_w^*(80°\text{C}) = 47.4$ kPa

**Step 1: Molar Humidity**

$$H_m = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} = \frac{47.4}{101.325 - 47.4} = \frac{47.4}{53.925}$$

$$H_m = 0.8791 \quad \text{kmol water/kmol dry air}$$

**Step 2: Absolute Humidity**

$$H = \frac{18}{29} \cdot H_m = \frac{18}{29} \cdot 0.8791$$

$$H = 0.6208 \cdot 0.8791 = 0.5457 \quad \text{kg water/kg dry air}$$

**Step 3: Percentage Humidity**

Since the air is at its dew point (because $P_w = P_w^*$), the air is **saturated**:

$$H_p = 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU exams:** Whenever $P_w = P_w^*$, the gas is saturated → RH = 100% → Dew Point = Gas Temperature. Examiners love this conceptual link.

---

#### **Example 2 — Dew Point Calculation**

> Air at 60 °C has a relative humidity of 40%. Find the dew point.
> Vapour pressures of water: at 60 °C = 19.95 kPa; at 40 °C = 7.40 kPa; at 35 °C = 5.64 kPa.

**Step 1:** Find $P_w$ from RH:

$$P_w = \phi \cdot P_w^*(60°\text{C}) = 0.40 \cdot 19.95$$

$$P_w = 7.98 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2:** Find $T_{dp}$ such that $P_w^*(T_{dp}) = 7.98$ kPa.

From tables: $P_w^*(40°\text{C}) = 7.40$ kPa and $P_w^*(45°\text{C}) = 9.59$ kPa.

By linear interpolation:
$$T_{dp} = 40 + \frac{7.98 - 7.40}{9.59 - 7.40} \cdot 5 = 40 + \frac{0.58}{2.19} \cdot 5$$

$$T_{dp} = 40 + 1.32 = 41.3°\text{C}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative method using Mollier chart:** Locate the point (T = 60 °C, RH = 40%) on the chart; move horizontally to the saturation curve (RH = 100%); drop down to read $T_{dp}$. This is faster in exams.

---

#### **Example 3 — Humid Heat and Humid Volume**

> Moist air at 50 °C, 1 atm has $H$ = 0.0185 kg water/kg dry air. Find $C_s$ and $V_H$.

**Humid Heat:**
$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot 0.0185$$
$$C_s = 1.005 + 0.0349 = 1.040 \text{ kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$

**Humid Volume** (using corrected form, $T = 323$ K):
$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot 0.0185) \cdot \left(\frac{323}{288}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{101.325}{101.325}\right)$$

$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.0000844) \cdot 1.1215$$
$$V_H = 0.002914 \cdot 1.1215 = 0.003268 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that dry air at 50 °C occupies 0.00318 m³/kg; adding small moisture (H=0.0185) only marginally increases the humid volume. The dominant effect is **temperature**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Problem Statement Contains... | Use This Term First |
|--------------------------------|---------------------|
| "Mass of water per mass of dry air" | Absolute Humidity ($H$) |
| "Moles of water per mole of dry air" | Molar Humidity ($H_m$) |
| "Relative saturation", "% saturation" | RH ($\phi$) or $H_p$ |
| "Fog begins to form", "condensation starts" | Dew Point ($T_{dp}$) |
| "Heat required to raise 1 kg of dry air by 1 K" | Humid Heat ($C_s$) |
| "Volume of 1 kg dry air + its moisture" | Humid Volume ($V_H$) |
| "Saturated at this temperature" | Saturation Humidity ($H_s$) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The "Humidity Universe" — Master Concept Map

Below is a comprehensive Mermaid diagram mapping all eight KTU-mandated humidity terms and their inter-relationships. This serves as a *block-level functional architecture* of the humidity system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas-Vapour Mixture: Air + Water Vapour] --> B{Daltons Law: P_total = P_a + P_w}

    B --> C[Dry Air Component]
    B --> D[Water Vapour Component]

    C --> E[Mass of Dry Air: m_a]
    C --> F[Moles of Dry Air: n_a]

    D --> G[Mass of Water: m_w]
    D --> H[Moles of Water: n_w]
    D --> I[Vapour Pressure P_w star at T]

    G --> J1[Absolute Humidity H = m_w / m_a]
    H --> J2[Molar Humidity Hm = n_w / n_a]

    I --> K1[Relative Humidity phi = P_w / P_w star]
    I --> K2[Saturation Humidity H_s at T]
    I --> K3[Dew Point T_dp: P_w equals P_w star at T_dp]

    J1 --> L1[Percentage Humidity H_p = H / H_s times 100]
    K2 --> L1

    J1 --> M1[Humid Heat C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 H]
    J1 --> M2[Humid Volume V_H using RT, P, H]

    A --> N[Saturation Condition]
    N --> O[Gas holds MAX water at given T and P]
    O --> K2
    O --> K3
    O --> P[Adiabatic Saturation Temperature T_as]

    P --> Q[Psychrometric Chart Application]
    K3 --> Q
    K1 --> Q
    M1 --> Q
    M2 --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this map:** Start from the gas-vapour mixture at the top. Dalton's Law splits the system into two parallel tracks (dry air and water vapour). From these tracks, all eight humidity terms are derived. The bottom row shows the practical outputs (humid heat, humid volume) and the link to **psychrometric charts**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Industrial Humidifier System

A simple schematic of a fermentation plant's inlet air humidification system:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B[Pre-Filter]
    B --> C[Compressor Blower]
    C --> D1[Pre-Heater Stage 1]
    D1 --> D2[Spray Chamber / Humidifier]
    D2 --> E1[Heater Stage 2]
    E1 --> E2[Sterile Filter 0.2 micron]
    E2 --> F[Bioreactor Inlet]

    D2 --> G1[Water Recirculation Pump]
    G1 --> G2[Make-up Water Inlet]
    G2 --> D2

    F --> H[Fermentation Broth]
    H --> I[Exhaust Air: Humid]
    I --> J[Condenser / Heat Exchanger]
    J --> K[Water Recovery]
    K --> G1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where humidity calculations apply in this flow:**
> - Between **D1 and D2**: Compute humidity gained across the spray chamber.
> - Between **D2 and E1**: Adiabatic saturation analysis (no heat added, water evaporates).
> - At **F**: The inlet air humidity must be known for **oxygen mass transfer** and **broth water balance**.
> - At **I and J**: Condensation calculations use **dew point** to size the heat exchanger.

---

### 4.3 Flowchart — Solving a Humidity Problem (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Read Problem Statement] --> Q1{Given Pressure of Water Vapour P_w?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Compute Molar Humidity Hm = P_w / P - P_w]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Given Relative Humidity phi?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Compute P_w = phi times P_w star from steam tables]
    A2 --> A1
    A1 --> A3[Compute Absolute Humidity H = 18/29 times Hm]

    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Given Dew Point?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A4[Look up P_w star at T_dp = P_w of system]
    A4 --> A1

    A3 --> R1{Required Quantity?}
    R1 -- H_p --> B1[H_p = H / H_s times 100]
    R1 -- C_s --> B2[C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 H]
    R1 -- V_H --> B3[V_H = RT/P - P_w times 1/29 + H/18]
    R1 -- T_dp --> B4[Find T such that P_w star T = P_w]
    B1 --> Done[Final Answer with Units]
    B2 --> Done
    B3 --> Done
    B4 --> Done
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When solving a numerical, always write down the **three GIVEN quantities** first (T, P, and one of $P_w$, $\phi$, or $H$). Then identify the **FIND** quantity. Use the decision tree to pick the shortest path.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the following terms with one suitable equation each:**
**(a)** Absolute humidity, **(b)** Relative humidity, **(c)** Dew point.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Absolute Humidity ($H$):** It is defined as the mass of water vapour associated with a unit mass of bone-dry air.
$$H = \frac{18 \cdot P_w}{29 \cdot (P - P_w)} \quad [\text{kg water/kg dry air}]$$

**(b) Relative Humidity ($\phi$):** It is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour in the gas to the vapour pressure of water at the gas temperature, expressed as a percentage.
$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_w^*(T)} \times 100\%$$

**(c) Dew Point ($T_{dp}$):** It is the temperature at which a humid gas becomes saturated when cooled at constant total pressure. Below this temperature, water vapour begins to condense.

$$\text{At dew point: } P_w \big\vert_{T_{gas}} = P_w^* \big\vert_{T_{dp}}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Differentiate between humidity and relative humidity. Why is the relative humidity of a gas mixture dependent on temperature?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Humidity (Absolute) | Relative Humidity |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Mass of water vapour per unit mass of dry air | Partial pressure of water vapour divided by saturation pressure at gas temperature |
| Symbol | $H$ | $\phi$ |
| Dependence on T | Slight (via $P_w$ and $P_a$) | Strong (via $P_w^*$) |
| Range | 0 to $\infty$ | 0 to 100% |

**Reason for temperature dependence of RH:**
The relative humidity $\phi = \dfrac{P_w}{P_w^*(T)}$ depends on $P_w^*$, which **increases exponentially** with temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron equation). Hence, even if the actual moisture content ($P_w$) is constant, the value of $\phi$ drops sharply as temperature rises. This is why cold winter air feels "drier" to the skin even when its absolute moisture is high.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Define **humid volume** and **humid heat**. Derive the expressions for both in terms of absolute humidity $H$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sample of air at 90 °C and 1 atm total pressure has a relative humidity of 30%. Calculate the **absolute humidity**, **molar humidity**, **percentage humidity**, and **humid heat**. Given: Vapour pressure of water at 90 °C = 70.14 kPa. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Humid Volume ($V_H$):**
It is the volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air **plus** the water vapour it carries, measured at the gas temperature and pressure.

*Derivation:*
For 1 kg of dry air: $n_a = \dfrac{1}{29}$ kmol; water vapour moles: $n_w = \dfrac{H}{18}$ kmol.

Total moles per kg of dry air: $n_{total} = \dfrac{1}{29} + \dfrac{H}{18}$

Applying ideal gas law at partial pressure of dry air ($P - P_w$):

$$V_H = \frac{n_{total} \, R \, T}{P - P_w} = \frac{RT}{P - P_w} \left( \frac{1}{29} + \frac{H}{18} \right)$$

At reference conditions (T = 288 K, P = 1 atm):

$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H) \cdot \left( \frac{T}{288} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{101.325}{P} \right) \quad \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

**Humid Heat ($C_s$):**
The heat capacity of 1 kg of dry air together with the water vapour it carries.

*Derivation:*
Energy balance for 1 kg dry air + H kg vapour:

$$C_s = C_{p,air} \cdot 1 + C_{p,vapour} \cdot H$$

$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H \quad \text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** T = 90 °C = 363 K, P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa, $\phi$ = 0.30, $P_w^*(90°\text{C})$ = 70.14 kPa

**Step 1: Find $P_w$**

$$P_w = \phi \cdot P_w^* = 0.30 \cdot 70.14 = 21.042 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Molar Humidity**

$$H_m = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} = \frac{21.042}{101.325 - 21.042} = \frac{21.042}{80.283} = 0.2621 \text{ kmol/kmol dry air}$$

**Step 3: Absolute Humidity**

$$H = \frac{18}{29} \cdot H_m = 0.6207 \cdot 0.2621 = 0.1627 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

**Step 4: Saturation Humidity** ($\phi = 1$):
$$H_s = \frac{18 \cdot 70.14}{29 \cdot (101.325 - 70.14)} = \frac{1262.52}{29 \cdot 31.185} = \frac{1262.52}{904.37} = 1.396 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

**Step 5: Percentage Humidity**

$$H_p = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100 = \frac{0.1627}{1.396} \times 100 = 11.66\%$$

**Step 6: Humid Heat**

$$C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot 0.1627 = 1.005 + 0.3065 = 1.312 \text{ kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$

**Final Answers (boxed for the examiner):**
- $H_m$ = **0.2621 kmol/kmol**
- $H$ = **0.1627 kg/kg dry air**
- $H_p$ = **11.66%**
- $C_s$ = **1.312 kJ/(kg dry air·K)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** What is **saturation**? Explain the concept of **dew point** with the help of a temperature-pressure diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** The dew point of a humid air stream at 1 atm is found to be 45 °C. The dry-bulb temperature is 65 °C. Determine the **absolute humidity**, **relative humidity**, and **humid volume** of the air. Given: $P_w^*(45°\text{C}) = 9.59$ kPa, $P_w^*(65°\text{C}) = 25.04$ kPa. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Saturation:**
A gas is said to be saturated with a vapour when it contains the **maximum possible amount** of that vapour at the given temperature and pressure. Beyond this, any additional vapour condenses into liquid. The partial pressure of vapour at saturation is the **vapour pressure** $P_w^*(T)$, which depends *only* on temperature.

**Dew Point Concept:**
The dew point is the temperature to which a gas must be cooled (at constant pressure) to become saturated. At this point, the partial pressure of water vapour equals the saturation pressure at the dew point.

**Temperature–Pressure Diagram (conceptual):**

On a plot of $P_w^*$ vs $T$, the saturation curve rises exponentially. For a given gas at temperature $T_1$ with partial pressure $P_{w,1}$:
- Draw a horizontal line at $P_{w,1}$.
- This line intersects the saturation curve at temperature $T_{dp}$.
- $T_{dp}$ is the **dew point**.

Any cooling below $T_{dp}$ causes condensation. The dew point is the most **direct measure of absolute moisture content** because it is independent of the gas temperature (it only depends on the actual partial pressure of water).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_{dp} = 45°$C
- $T_{gas} = 65°$C
- $P = 1$ atm = 101.325 kPa
- $P_w^*(45°\text{C}) = 9.59$ kPa
- $P_w^*(65°\text{C}) = 25.04$ kPa

**Step 1: Find $P_w$ of the humid air**

At dew point, $P_w$ = $P_w^*(T_{dp})$:
$$P_w = 9.59 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Absolute Humidity**

$$H = \frac{18 \cdot P_w}{29 \cdot (P - P_w)} = \frac{18 \cdot 9.59}{29 \cdot (101.325 - 9.59)} = \frac{172.62}{29 \cdot 91.735}$$

$$H = \frac{172.62}{2660.32} = 0.0649 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

**Step 3: Relative Humidity**

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_w^*(65°\text{C})} = \frac{9.59}{25.04} \times 100 = 38.30\%$$

**Step 4: Humid Volume** (T = 338 K):
$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H) \cdot \left(\frac{T}{288}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{101.325}{P}\right)$$

$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot 0.0649) \cdot \left(\frac{338}{288}\right) \cdot 1$$

$$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.000296) \cdot 1.1736 = 0.003126 \cdot 1.1736 = 0.003669 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $H$ = **0.0649 kg/kg dry air**
- $\phi$ = **38.30%**
- $V_H$ = **0.003669 m³/kg dry air**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Forgetting that $H$ is in **kg water per kg DRY air**, not per kg of moist air. Always state "per kg dry air."
> 2. **Forgetting P – P_w (–1 mark):** The denominator in the humidity formula is $P - P_w$, **not** $P$ alone. This error is a *favourite trap* by KTU examiners.
> 3. **RH ≠ Percentage Humidity in detail (–1 mark):** While numerically close for ideal gases, conceptually $H_p$ is **H/H_s** and $\phi$ is **P_w/P_w\***. KTU 2024 asks for both. State the difference.
> 4. **Using wrong molecular weight (–1 mark):** Always use 18 for water and **29 for air** (not 28.97 unless specified). Use 22.4 L/mol for STP.
> 5. **No unit declaration (–1 mark):** Every numerical answer must carry units: kg/kg dry air, kJ/(kg·K), m³/kg, °C.
> 6. **Confusing dew point with wet-bulb (–2 marks):** Dew point is **saturation temperature on cooling**; wet-bulb is **dynamic equilibrium** with simultaneous heat and mass transfer. They are *not* the same in principle, though numerically close in air–water systems.
> 7. **Linear interpolation skipped in table lookups (–0.5 to –1 mark):** KTU expects interpolation when reading vapour pressure tables.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Humidity & Saturation**

- **Humidity = water vapour content in a gas.** Carrier gas is usually air.
- **Dalton's Law:** $P_{total} = P_{dry\,air} + P_{water\,vapour} = P_a + P_w$.
- **Absolute Humidity (H):** $H = \dfrac{18 \, P_w}{29 \, (P - P_w)}$ — kg water/kg dry air.
- **Molar Humidity ($H_m$):** $H_m = \dfrac{P_w}{P - P_w}$ — kmol water/kmol dry air. Conversion: $H = (18/29) \, H_m$.
- **Relative Humidity ($\phi$):** $\phi = \dfrac{P_w}{P_w^*(T)} \times 100$ — dimensionless (%); $P_w^*$ depends only on T.
- **Saturation:** Gas holds the **maximum** possible water at that T and P. $H_s = \dfrac{18 \, P_w^*}{29 \, (P - P_w^*)}$.
- **Percentage Humidity ($H_p$):** $H_p = \dfrac{H}{H_s} \times 100 \approx \phi$ for low humidity.
- **Dew Point ($T_{dp}$):** $P_w \big\vert_{T_{gas}} = P_w^* \big\vert_{T_{dp}}$. Below $T_{dp}$ → condensation.
- **Humid Heat ($C_s$):** $C_s = 1.005 + 1.884 \cdot H$ kJ/(kg dry air·K).
- **Humid Volume ($V_H$):** Volume of 1 kg dry air + its vapour at gas T, P. $V_H = \dfrac{RT}{P-P_w}\left(\dfrac{1}{29}+\dfrac{H}{18}\right)$.
- **Saturated gas ⟹** RH = 100% ⟹ $T_{dp}$ = $T_{gas}$.
- **Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($T_{as}$):** Equilibrium temperature when gas contacts its own liquid adiabatically; basis of the **psychrometric chart**.
- **Universal Constants to Memorize:** $M_w = 18$, $M_{air} = 29$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg.
- **Steam Table Mastery:** KTU 2024 expects you to read or interpolate $P_w^*$ values from steam tables for any T between 0–150 °C.
- **Common Exam Traps:** Unit slip-ups, missing P − P_w, RH ≠ $H_p$ in theory, no interpolation in table lookups.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** *Always write the GIVEN values in a box, identify the FIND quantity, choose the right formula from the cheat sheet, and end with a boxed answer in proper SI units. Examiners reward clarity and penalize messy work.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Unit Operations and Unit Processes, Law of Conservation of Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Definition of Unit Operations and Unit Processes, Law of Conservation of Mass ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Unit Operations – Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit Operation (UO):** A *unit operation* is a basic physical step or stage in a process in which a **physical change** occurs without any change in chemical identity. The material undergoing the operation experiences a transformation in **state, phase, composition boundary, or physical property** only. In the context of **Bioprocess Engineering**, every bioprocess plant (whether producing ethanol, penicillin, vaccines, or enzymes) is essentially a network of interconnected unit operations sandwiched around biological unit processes (like fermentation). **Common Bioprocess Unit Operations:** - **Mixing and Agitation** - **Filtration (Microfiltration / Ultrafiltration)** - **Centrifugation** (cell separation) - **Distillation** (solvent recovery) - **Drying** (spray drying, lyophilization) - **Heat Exchangers** (sterilization, cooling) - **Chromatography** (protein purification) - **Crystallization** ### 1.2 Unit Processes – Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit Process (UP):** A *unit process* is a basic step in a process in which a **chemical change or biological conversion** takes place, resulting in the transformation of feed materials into chemically or biologically distinct products. In bioprocesses, **fermentation** is the classical unit process where microbes convert sugars into products. Other unit processes include: - **Aerobic / Anaerobic Fermentation** - **Enzymatic Hydrolysis** - **Biosynthesis** (antibiotic production, recombinant protein production) - **Oxidation / Reduction** in chemical pretreatment ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Cooking Kitchen":** > Think of a kitchen preparing a dish. The chef **chops vegetables (physical change – size reduction)**, **boils water (phase change)**, and **filters tea leaves (separation)**. These are **Unit Operations**. But when the chef **adds spices and cooks**, the ingredients undergo a **chemical transformation** – flavor compounds form that did not exist before. This is the **Unit Process**. In a bioprocess plant, the fermenter is the "cooking" vessel, and the downstream train of centrifuges, filters, and dryers are the "chopping and filtering" equipment. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Boundaries - The **General Mass Balance Time Horizon** is expressed in **seconds (s)** or **hours (h)**. - Flow rates are expressed in **kg/h** or **L/h**. - Conversion is expressed as a **dimensionless fraction (0 to 1)** or **percentage (%)**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Boundary:** A process step is classified as a **Unit Operation** if no new chemical species are formed. If new molecular entities appear, it is a **Unit Process**. Hybrid steps (e.g., reactive distillation) contain both, but the **dominant transformation** determines the classification. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – Unit Operations Unit Operations were formalized by **Arthur D. Little (1915)** at MIT. The philosophy states that **any complex chemical or biological process can be broken down into a sequence of standard, well-studied physical steps**, regardless of the industry. The advantages of this classification: - Allows **engineers to design processes across industries** (same pump works in oil refinery and pharmaceutical plant). - Enables **standardized equipment design** and scale-up. - Forms the **basis of process flow diagrams (PFDs)**. **Classification of Unit Operations by Driving Force:** | Physical Principle | Driving Force | Example Unit Operation | |---|---|---| | Momentum Transfer | Pressure gradient | Pumping, Filtration | | Heat Transfer | Temperature gradient | Heat exchangers, Evaporation | | Mass Transfer | Concentration gradient | Distillation, Extraction, Drying | | Simultaneous | Multiple | Drying (heat + mass transfer) | ### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation – Unit Processes Unit Processes are classified by the **type of chemical reaction**: | Type of Unit Process | Example in Industry | |---|---| | Oxidation | Bleaching, fermentation | | Reduction | Hydrogenation of oils | | Hydrolysis | Starch to glucose | | Polymerization | Bio-plastic production | | Fermentation | Beer, wine, antibiotic production | | Nitration | Explosives / chemical synthesis | ### 2.3 The Law of Conservation of Mass > [!IMPORTANT] > **Law of Conservation of Mass (Antoine Lavoisier, 1789):** *Matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction or physical process. The total mass of the reactants equals the total mass of the products.* In process engineering, this law is converted into a working equation called the **General Mass Balance Equation**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Equation | Formula | Application / Condition | |---|---|---| | Conservation of Mass (Reactive) | $\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C$ | **General** – any system | | Steady State (no accumulation) | $0 = F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C$ | $\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = 0$ | | Non-Reactive Steady State | $F_{in} = F_{out}$ | No chemical reaction, $G = C = 0$ | | Component Mass Balance (Reactive) | $\frac{dM_{i}}{dt} = F_{in,i} - F_{out,i} + r_{i} \cdot V$ | For species *i* in reactor | | Overall Steady State (Reactive) | $F_{in} - F_{out} = C - G$ | Mass lost to reaction equals mass generated | | Conversion | $X = \frac{\text{Reactant consumed}}{\text{Reactant fed}}$ | $0 \le X \le 1$ | | Yield | $Y_{P/S} = \frac{\text{Product formed}}{\text{Substrate consumed}}$ | Dimensionless kg/kg | | Selectivity | $S = \frac{\text{Desired product}}{\text{Total products}}$ | Multi-product systems | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** $M_{sys}$ = mass in system (kg), $F$ = mass flow rate (kg/h), $G$ = generation rate (kg/h), $C$ = consumption rate (kg/h), $r_i$ = rate of formation/consumption of species $i$ (kg/(m³·s)), $V$ = reactor volume (m³), $X$ = fractional conversion, $Y_{P/S}$ = product yield on substrate. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the General Mass Balance Equation We start from the **Universal Conservation Law**: $$ \text{(Input)} - \text{(Output)} + \text{(Generation)} - \text{(Consumption)} = \text{(Accumulation)} $$ **Step 1:** Define a control volume (system boundary). Let the **mass inside the system** at time $t$ be $M_{sys}(t)$ in **kg**. **Step 2:** Identify the **rate of mass entering** the system as $F_{in}$ in **kg/h**. **Step 3:** Identify the **rate of mass leaving** the system as $F_{out}$ in **kg/h**. **Step 4:** Define **Generation rate** $G$ as the rate at which mass of a particular species is **created** inside the system (e.g., by chemical reaction or cell growth) in **kg/h**. **Step 5:** Define **Consumption rate** $C$ as the rate at which mass of a particular species is **depleted** inside the system in **kg/h**. **Step 6:** Apply the conservation principle over an infinitesimal time interval $dt$. The net change in mass within the system is: $$ dM_{sys} = (F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C) \, dt $$ **Step 7:** Divide both sides by $dt$ to obtain the **rate form of the mass balance equation**: $$ \frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C $$ **Step 8 – Simplification for Steady State:** A **steady-state** process is one where the system properties do not change with time, so the accumulation term is zero: $$ \frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C = 0 $$ **Step 9 – Simplification for Non-Reactive System:** If no chemical or biological reaction occurs inside the system, then $G = 0$ and $C = 0$. The steady-state equation reduces to: $$ F_{in} = F_{out} $$ **Step 10 – Component (Species) Mass Balance:** For a specific species $i$ inside a reactor of volume $V$, with volumetric reaction rate $r_i$ (kg of *i* generated per m³ per hour): $$ \frac{dM_i}{dt} = F_{in,i} - F_{out,i} + r_i \cdot V $$ This is the **most frequently used form** in bioprocess calculations involving fermenters. ### 3.2 Worked Analytical Matrix – Mass Balance on a Fermenter Consider a continuous stirred-tank fermenter (CSTF) operating at steady state. The cell mass balance gives the equation of operation, and substrate balance gives substrate consumption. The two are linked by the yield coefficient. Let: - $F$ = volumetric flow rate (L/h) = $D \cdot V$, where $D$ is dilution rate (h⁻¹) - $C_{X,in}, C_{S,in}$ = inlet cell and substrate concentrations (g/L) - $C_X, C_S$ = outlet cell and substrate concentrations (g/L) - $r_X = \mu \cdot C_X$ = cell growth rate (g/(L·h)) - $r_S = -\frac{\mu \cdot C_X}{Y_{X/S}}$ = substrate consumption rate (g/(L·h)) **Cell mass balance:** $$ \frac{d(C_X V)}{dt} = F C_{X,in} - F C_X + \mu C_X V $$ At steady state, $\frac{d(C_X V)}{dt} = 0$, and assuming sterile feed ($C_{X,in} = 0$): $$ 0 = -F C_X + \mu C_X V \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{F}{V} = D $$ This is the famous **CSTF Monod Identity:** *the specific growth rate equals the dilution rate* in steady state. **Substrate mass balance:** $$ \frac{d(C_S V)}{dt} = F C_{S,in} - F C_S - \frac{\mu C_X V}{Y_{X/S}} $$ At steady state: $$ D (C_{S,in} - C_S) = \frac{\mu C_X}{Y_{X/S}} $$ Substituting $\mu = D$ and rearranging: $$ C_X = Y_{X/S} \, (C_{S,in} - C_S) $$ This is the **biomass-substrate mass balance** for a continuous bioreactor at steady state. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow – Distinction Between Unit Operations and Unit Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Raw Materials
Feed Streams"] --> B{"Type of
Transformation?"} B -- "Physical change only
No new chemical species" --> C["UNIT OPERATION
No chemical reaction
Examples: Filtration,
Distillation, Drying,
Centrifugation"] B -- "Chemical or biological
change occurs
New species formed" --> D["UNIT PROCESS
Chemical reaction involved
Examples: Fermentation,
Hydrolysis, Oxidation,
Biosynthesis"] C --> E["Intermediate
or Purified Stream"] D --> E E --> F{"Further Processing
Required?"} F -- "Yes, physical step" --> C F -- "Yes, chemical step" --> D F -- "No" --> G["Final Product
Spec Achieved"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block – General Mass Balance on a Process System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Mass In Flow
F_in kg per h"] --> S1((("System
Control Volume
M_sys kg"))) S1 --> A2["Mass Out Flow
F_out kg per h"] G1["Generation Rate
G kg per h
by reaction or cell growth"] --> S1 S1 --> C1["Consumption Rate
C kg per h
by reaction or metabolism"] S1 --> A3["Accumulation
dM_sys / dt"] A1 -. "Input" .-> S1 A2 -. "Output" .-> S1 G1 -. "Source" .-> S1 C1 -. "Sink" .-> S1 A3 -. "Storage" .-> S1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block – Classification of Bioprocess Steps ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Bioprocess Plant"] --> P1["Upstream Processing"] P --> P2["Bioreaction Stage"] P --> P3["Downstream Processing"] P1 --> P1A["Unit Operations
Media preparation
Sterilization
Heat transfer"] P2 --> P2A["Unit Process
Fermentation
Cell growth
Product formation"] P3 --> P3A["Unit Operations
Cell separation
Purification
Concentration
Formulation"] P1A --> P2A P2A --> P3A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each block shows the **direction of mass flow** and the **physical or chemical nature** of the step. The reactor (Unit Process) is the only block where **new molecular species are generated**, while the surrounding unit operations only **separate, purify, or change physical state** of matter. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Define Unit Operations and Unit Processes. List any three examples of each from a typical bioprocess industry.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Unit Operation (1 mark):** A unit operation is a basic step in a process where **only physical changes** occur (state, phase, composition boundaries, or physical properties change) without any alteration in chemical identity. **Unit Process (1 mark):** A unit process is a basic step where **chemical or biological transformation** takes place, producing chemically distinct products from the feed. **Three Examples Each (1 mark):** - **Unit Operations:** Centrifugation, Filtration, Distillation, Drying, Mixing. - **Unit Processes:** Fermentation, Enzymatic hydrolysis, Biosynthesis, Oxidation, Polymerization. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *State the Law of Conservation of Mass. Write the general mass balance equation and explain each term.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Law Statement (1 mark):** *Matter can neither be created nor destroyed in any physical or chemical process. The total mass of all inputs equals the total mass of all outputs plus any accumulation.* **General Mass Balance Equation (1 mark):** $$ \frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C $$ **Explanation of Terms (1 mark):** - $M_{sys}$ = mass of material inside the system boundary (kg). - $F_{in}$ = mass flow rate entering the system (kg/h). - $F_{out}$ = mass flow rate leaving the system (kg/h). - $G$ = rate of mass generation inside the system (kg/h). - $C$ = rate of mass consumption inside the system (kg/h). - $\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt}$ = rate of accumulation of mass inside the system (kg/h). --- ### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the general mass balance equation from the principle of conservation of mass. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A continuous stirred-tank fermenter (CSTF) operates at steady state with a working volume of **1000 L**. The feed stream enters at a flow rate of **100 L/h** with a glucose concentration of **50 g/L**. The effluent contains **10 g/L** glucose. Cells are produced at a rate of **2.5 kg/h**. Calculate: - (i) The rate of glucose consumption (kg/h). - (ii) The yield of cells on substrate, $Y_{X/S}$ (g cells / g glucose). - (iii) The dilution rate, $D$ (h⁻¹). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *Step 1 – State the conservation principle (1 mark):* For any well-defined system (control volume), the law of conservation of mass states: $$ \text{Mass In} + \text{Mass Generated} - \text{Mass Out} - \text{Mass Consumed} = \text{Mass Accumulated} $$ *Step 2 – Define variables (2 marks):* Let the mass inside the system be $M_{sys}$. Let $F_{in}$ and $F_{out}$ be the entering and leaving mass flow rates.
