Let $G$ and $C$ be the generation and consumption rates by reaction. *Step 3 – Formulate the differential form (2 marks):* For a small time interval $dt$: $$ dM_{sys} = (F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C) \, dt $$ Dividing by $dt$: $$ \frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C $$ *Step 4 – State assumptions (2 marks):* - The system is a **closed, well-defined control volume**. - **Conservation holds for both total mass and individual species**. - **Steady state** requires $\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = 0$. - For **non-reactive systems**, $G = 0$ and $C = 0$, leading to $F_{in} = F_{out}$. --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** *Given Data:* - $V = 1000$ L, $F = 100$ L/h - $C_{S,in} = 50$ g/L, $C_{S,out} = 10$ g/L - Cell production rate = $2.5$ kg/h = $2500$ g/h **(i) Rate of Glucose Consumption (3 marks):** Glucose mass balance on fermenter (steady state): $$ F \cdot C_{S,in} - F \cdot C_{S,out} = \text{Rate of glucose consumption} $$ Substitute values: $$ \text{Consumption} = 100 \times 50 - 100 \times 10 = 5000 - 1000 = 4000 \text{ g/h} $$ Converting to **kg/h**: $$ \text{Consumption} = 4.0 \text{ kg/h} $$ **(ii) Yield $Y_{X/S}$ (2 marks):** $$ Y_{X/S} = \frac{\text{Cells produced}}{\text{Substrate consumed}} = \frac{2500 \text{ g/h}}{4000 \text{ g/h}} = 0.625 \text{ g cells / g glucose} $$ **(iii) Dilution Rate $D$ (2 marks):** $$ D = \frac{F}{V} = \frac{100 \text{ L/h}}{1000 \text{ L}} = 0.1 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ **Final Answers:** - Glucose consumption rate = **4.0 kg/h** - $Y_{X/S}$ = **0.625 g/g** - $D$ = **0.1 h⁻¹** --- **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Differentiate between Unit Operations and Unit Processes with **five points of distinction** and **two industrial examples** for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bioprocess plant produces ethanol via fermentation. Molasses feed enters at **500 kg/h** containing **45% w/w fermentable sugar**. The yeast consumes **92%** of the sugar. Ethanol yield on sugar is **0.46 g ethanol / g sugar consumed**, and **5%** of sugar is diverted to cell mass. The effluent contains unconverted sugar, cells, water, and ethanol. Calculate the **mass flow rate of ethanol produced per hour** and verify whether the **law of conservation of mass holds** for the total stream. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Differentiation Table (7 marks):** | S.No. | Aspect | Unit Operation | Unit Process | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Nature of change | Physical only | Chemical / Biological | | 2 | Governing laws | Physics (heat/mass/momentum transfer) | Stoichiometry, kinetics, biochemistry | | 3 | Equipment examples | Centrifuge, heat exchanger, dryer | Fermenter, reactor, bioreactor | | 4 | Energy requirement | Mechanical, thermal | Activated chemical, biochemical energy | | 5 | Products | Same chemical species, different state | New chemical species formed | **Two Examples Each (built into the table above):** - **UO Examples:** Distillation, Filtration. - **UP Examples:** Fermentation, Hydrolysis. --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** *Given:* - $F_{in} = 500$ kg/h total molasses feed - Sugar fraction in feed = **0.45** - Sugar consumed by yeast = **92%** of inlet sugar - $Y_{P/S} = 0.46$ g ethanol per g sugar consumed - Sugar diverted to cells = **5%** of inlet sugar **Step 1 – Mass flow rate of sugar in feed (1 mark):** $$ \dot{m}_{S,in} = 500 \times 0.45 = 225 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 2 – Mass flow rate of sugar consumed (1 mark):** $$ \dot{m}_{S,consumed} = 225 \times 0.92 = 207 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 3 – Mass flow rate of ethanol produced (2 marks):** $$ \dot{m}_{ethanol} = 207 \times 0.46 = 95.22 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 4 – Mass flow rate to cells (1 mark):** $$ \dot{m}_{cells} = 225 \times 0.05 = 11.25 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 5 – Mass flow rate of unconverted sugar in effluent (1 mark):** $$ \dot{m}_{S,out} = 225 - 207 - 11.25 = 6.75 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 6 – Verify Law of Conservation of Mass (1 mark):** Total mass leaving the system = sugar out + cells + ethanol + other (water, ash): Inlet total = 500 kg/h. Since the other components (water, ash) pass through unaltered: Outlet total should also be 500 kg/h (neglecting minor CO₂ loss in headspace accounting, the liquid phase remains conserved at 500 kg/h within the fermenter boundary if we consider total mass in the liquid stream). The **Law of Conservation of Mass is satisfied** because the sum of inlet mass components equals the sum of outlet mass components plus any accumulation (zero at steady state). **Final Answers:** - Ethanol produced = **95.22 kg/h** - Conservation of mass: **Verified.** --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Mass Balance Problems:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often write $F = 100$ L/h but forget to convert g/L × L/h = g/h, then leave the answer in g/h instead of kg/h. Always **convert to base SI or consistent units** before substituting. > 2. **Forgetting the Accumulation Term:** In transient problems (e.g., batch fermenters), the term $\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt}$ is **non-zero**. Many students wrongly set it to zero without checking if the system is at steady state. > 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** $G$ is always **positive** (formation), $C$ is always **positive** (depletion), but the rate $r_i$ can be **negative** for reactants and **positive** for products. Verify the sign before writing the final equation. > 4. **Mixing UO and UP in Classification:** Students sometimes classify *sterilization* as a unit process because microbes are killed. Sterilization is **physical** (heat kills by protein denaturation – a physical phenomenon), so it is a **Unit Operation**. Only reactions producing **new chemical species** qualify as Unit Processes. > 5. **Yield Misdefinition:** $Y_{X/S}$ is the yield **of cells on substrate consumed**, not on substrate fed. This is a **favourite KTU trap** and costs 2–3 marks if confused. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – Definition of UO, UP & Conservation of Mass** > > - **Unit Operation = Physical Change ONLY** (no new molecules). Examples: Filtration, Centrifugation, Distillation, Drying, Mixing. > - **Unit Process = Chemical/Biological Change** (new molecules formed). Examples: Fermentation, Hydrolysis, Oxidation, Biosynthesis. > - **Law of Conservation of Mass** (Lavoisier, 1789): Mass is **never created or destroyed**; it is only converted. > - **General Mass Balance Equation:** > $$\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} + G - C$$ > - **Steady State:** $\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} = 0$ ⇒ $F_{in} + G = F_{out} + C$. > - **Non-Reactive Steady State:** $F_{in} = F_{out}$. > - **Sterile Feed CSTF Cell Balance:** $\mu = D$ (specific growth rate equals dilution rate). > - **CSTF Biomass-Substrate Identity:** $C_X = Y_{X/S} (C_{S,in} - C_S)$. > - **Conversion** $X$ is always between **0 and 1**; **Yield** $Y$ has units consistent with the numerator and denominator. > - **Always perform unit checks** before finalizing numerical answers. > - **Classification Rule:** If a new chemical species appears, it is a **Unit Process**; otherwise, it is a **Unit Operation**.

topics.title: Types of Material Balance Problems: Total and Component Balances, Steady and Unsteady State Processes, Batch and Continuous Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Material Balance Problems: Total and Component Balances, Steady and Unsteady State Processes, Batch and Continuous Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Types of Material Balance Problems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **material balance** is an application of the **Law of Conservation of Mass** to a defined process system. It quantifies the flow, accumulation, and transformation of total mass or individual chemical components across the system boundary. Depending on **what** is being tracked and **how** the process operates, material balance problems are classified into distinct categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:**
> *"A material balance is a quantitative accounting of all materials entering, leaving, accumulated, generated, and consumed within a process system, expressed on a total mass basis or a per-component basis over a defined time interval."*

The four principal classifications are:

1. **Total Material Balance** — tracks the overall mass regardless of chemical identity.
2. **Component (Species) Material Balance** — tracks individual chemical species (e.g., glucose, biomass, product).
3. **Steady-State vs Unsteady-State Processes** — classified based on whether system properties change with time.
4. **Batch vs Continuous (and Semi-Batch) Processes** — classified based on the mode of material input/output.

### 1.2 The Universal Material Balance Equation

The general governing equation for any material balance problem, whether total or component, is:

$$
\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}
$$

In rate form, this becomes the cornerstone of **Bioprocess Calculations**:

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + r_{\text{gen}} \cdot V - r_{\text{cons}} \cdot V
$$

where $\frac{dM}{dt}$ is the rate of mass accumulation in the system, $\dot{m}_{\text{in}}$ and $\dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ are inlet and outlet mass flow rates, and $r$ is the volumetric rate of generation or consumption.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Bucket

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Leaky Bucket**
> Imagine a **bucket with a tap on top (inlet) and a small hole at the bottom (outlet)**. The water level in the bucket is the "accumulation."
> - If **inflow = outflow**, the water level stays constant → **Steady State**.
> - If **inflow > outflow**, the bucket fills up → **Unsteady State with accumulation**.
> - If you pour in **salt water** and drain **fresh water**, the salt concentration inside changes with time → **Component (species) balance** even when total mass is steady.

The same logic applies to bioreactors: cells, substrates, and products all have their own "inlets, outlets, and accumulations" inside the vessel.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/h** or **g/s** |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | **L/h** or **m³/s** |
| Concentration | $C$ | **g/L** or **kg/m³** |
| Mass fraction | $x$ | dimensionless |
| Volumetric reaction rate | $r$ | **g/(L·h)** |
| Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification — Total vs. Component Balances

### 2.1.1 Total Material Balance
- A **total balance** considers the **entire mass** of all species entering, leaving, accumulating, generated, and consumed in the system.
- It is **independent of chemical reactions**; generation and consumption of individual species cancel out in the total because matter is neither created nor destroyed overall.
- **Total balance equation (rate form):**

$$
\frac{dM_{\text{total}}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}
$$

- **When to use:** Equipment sizing, throughput calculations, residence time determination, and as a check on component balances.

### 2.1.2 Component (Species) Material Balance
- A **component balance** is written for **each individual chemical species** (substrate, biomass, product, oxygen, CO₂, etc.).
- This is where **stoichiometry and kinetics** enter the picture — generation and consumption terms are non-zero.
- **Component balance equation (for species $i$):**

$$
\frac{dM_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i} \cdot V - r_{c,i} \cdot V
$$

- **Number of independent component balances** = (Total number of species) − (Number of independent reactions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In a fermenter with glucose, biomass, ethanol, CO₂, and water, you can write **5 component balances** but only **4 are independent** because the 5th is recoverable from the total balance. Always identify independent balances to avoid wasted effort.

### 2.1.3 Worked Mini-Example (Total + Component)

A mixing tank receives two streams: $F_1 = 100$ kg/h with $x_{A,1} = 0.30$ (mass fraction of A), and $F_2 = 200$ kg/h with $x_{A,2} = 0.10$. No reaction. Find outlet mass flow and mass fraction of A.

**Total balance:** $F_3 = F_1 + F_2 = 100 + 200 = 300$ kg/h

**Component balance (A):** $F_1 x_{A,1} + F_2 x_{A,2} = F_3 x_{A,3}$

$$
x_{A,3} = \frac{(100)(0.30) + (200)(0.10)}{300} = \frac{30 + 20}{300} = 0.1667
$$

This single illustration already touches **total balance, component balance, steady state, and continuous** operation.

---

## 2.2 Classification — Steady-State vs. Unsteady-State Processes

### 2.2.1 Steady-State Process
- **Definition:** All process variables (flow rates, concentrations, temperature, hold-up) are **constant with time** at every point in the system.
- **Mathematical condition:** $\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$ and $\frac{dM_i}{dt} = 0$ for all species.
- **Simplified balance:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \quad ; \quad \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} + r_{g,i} V = \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{c,i} V
$$

- **Examples:** Long-running continuous fermenter, oil refinery distillation column, water treatment aeration tank.

### 2.2.2 Unsteady-State (Transient) Process
- **Definition:** At least one process variable changes with time; there is a **net accumulation or depletion** of mass within the system.
- **Mathematical condition:** $\frac{dM}{dt} \neq 0$.
- **Full differential balance** must be solved:

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + r_{g} V - r_{c} V
$$

- **Examples:** Batch fermenter, start-up and shut-down of a CSTR, filling of a storage tank, fed-batch phase of a penicillin production.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Steady = "Standing" — nothing moves with time. Unsteady = "Transitional" — variables are evolving.*

---

## 2.3 Classification — Batch, Semi-Batch, and Continuous Processes

### 2.3.1 Batch Process
- **Definition:** All materials are charged into the system **at the start**; no inflow or outflow of process material during the reaction time. The reaction proceeds for a fixed duration, then the contents are discharged.
- **Boundary conditions:** $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = 0$, $\dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 0$ during operation.
- **Balance reduces to:**

$$
\frac{dM_i}{dt} = r_{g,i} V - r_{c,i} V
$$

- **Industrial examples:** Most antibiotic fermentations (penicillin), beer brewing, baker's yeast propagation, enzyme production.

### 2.3.2 Semi-Batch (Fed-Batch) Process
- **Definition:** One stream flows in (or out) while the other is absent; concentration profiles evolve with time.
- **Balance:**

$$
\frac{dM_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} + r_{g,i} V - r_{c,i} V \quad \text{(no outlet)}
$$

- **Industrial examples:** Fed-batch production of recombinant proteins, fed-batch penicillin production, biodiesel batch with continuous methanol addition.

### 2.3.3 Continuous Process
- **Definition:** Material flows in and out **continuously and simultaneously**; ideally operated at steady state.
- **Steady-state balance:**

$$
\dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} + r_{g,i} V = \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{c,i} V
$$

- **Industrial examples:** Activated sludge wastewater treatment, ethanol fermentation in a CSTR, single-cell protein production, brewing with continuous fermenters.

### 2.3.4 The "Universe Table" Approach (KTU Recommended Method)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern — Universe Table:** Before writing any balance equation, draw a 5-column table with headers: **Component | In | Out | Generation | Consumption | Accumulation**. Fill in zeros and unknowns systematically. This earns the **"Process Identification" mark (1 mark)** in Part B problems.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all governing equations** for material balance problems classified by process type.

| Process Type | Total Balance | Component Balance (Species $i$) | Time Dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **General (any)** | $\dfrac{dM_{\text{tot}}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | $\dfrac{dM_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i}V - r_{c,i}V$ | Unsteady (general form) |
| **Steady-State, Continuous** | $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | $\dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} + r_{g,i}V = \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{c,i}V$ | No time dependence |
| **Steady-State, Continuous (no reaction)** | $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | $\dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}}$ | No time dependence |
| **Unsteady-State, Batch** | $\dfrac{dM_{\text{tot}}}{dt} = 0$ (closed system) | $\dfrac{dM_i}{dt} = r_{g,i}V - r_{c,i}V$ | Time-dependent |
| **Unsteady-State, Semi-Batch (feed only)** | $\dfrac{dM_{\text{tot}}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}}$ | $\dfrac{dM_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} + r_{g,i}V - r_{c,i}V$ | Time-dependent |
| **Unsteady-State, Continuous (general)** | $\dfrac{dM_{\text{tot}}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | $\dfrac{dM_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i}V - r_{c,i}V$ | Time-dependent |

### 2.4.1 Key Derived Identities

- **Residence Time / Space Time:** $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q_{\text{out}}}$, unit = **h**
- **Dilution Rate:** $D = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$, unit = **h⁻¹**
- **Volumetric flow in terms of mass flow and density:** $Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$, unit = **m³/s**
- **Component mass flow:** $\dot{m}_i = Q \cdot C_i$
- **Number of independent balances:** $N_{\text{indep}} = N_{\text{species}} - N_{\text{reactions}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants and Units to Memorise:**
> - Standard volumetric flow unit in bioprocess: **L/h**
> - Standard concentration unit: **g/L**
> - Time unit for rate: **h** (not seconds, in fermentation)
> - Molar mass of glucose = **180 g/mol**, water = **18 g/mol**

### 2.4.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

These classifications are **not academic exercises** — they directly drive:
- **Reactor selection** (batch vs. continuous for a given yield).
- **Process control strategy** (steady-state setpoints vs. dynamic trajectories).
- **Safety analysis** (unsteady start-ups have the highest risk).
- **Economic analysis** (continuous processes suit large-scale commodity products; batch processes suit high-value, low-volume biopharmaceuticals).
- **Regulatory documentation** (FDA expects explicit classification of process mode in biologics manufacturing).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Material Balance

We begin from the **Law of Conservation of Mass** for a defined system of arbitrary shape and content.

**Step 1 — State the fundamental principle.**
The total mass within a closed or open system can only change if mass crosses the system boundary, or if mass is created/destroyed by nuclear reaction. In bioprocess engineering, **nuclear reactions are excluded**, so:

$$
\text{(Rate of mass change in system)} = \text{(Rate of mass in)} - \text{(Rate of mass out)} + \text{(Net generation by reaction)}
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce mathematical notation.**
Let $M(t)$ denote the total mass in the system at time $t$. Then:

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{gen}} - \dot{m}_{\text{cons}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert reaction terms to volumetric form.**
For biological reactions, rates are commonly given as **volumetric rates** $r$ in **g/(L·h)**. The total generation rate is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{gen}} = r_{\text{gen}} \cdot V
$$

where $V$ is the reactor working volume in litres.

**Step 4 — Substituting back, the canonical rate-form balance:**

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + r_{\text{gen}} V - r_{\text{cons}} V
$$

**Step 5 — Specialisation to component $i$.**
For an individual species $i$ (with $C_i$ its concentration in g/L), the mass of that species inside the system is $M_i = C_i V$. If $V$ is constant, $\frac{dM_i}{dt} = V \frac{dC_i}{dt}$, and the component balance becomes:

$$
V \frac{dC_i}{dt} = Q_{\text{in}} C_{i,\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} C_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i} V - r_{c,i} V
$$

Dividing throughout by $V$:

$$
\frac{dC_i}{dt} = D_{\text{in}} C_{i,\text{in}} - D_{\text{out}} C_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i} - r_{c,i}
$$

where $D = Q/V$ is the dilution rate. This is the **most-quoted form in KTU numericals**.

**Step 6 — Application of limiting cases.**
Setting $\frac{dC_i}{dt} = 0$ recovers the **steady-state** form. Setting $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{out}} = 0$ gives the **batch** form. The matrix of limiting cases was tabulated in Section 2.4.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivative Example — Mixing and Dilution (Steady-State, Continuous)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A fermentation broth containing $C_{A,\text{in}} = 50$ g/L of glucose is fed into a continuous stirred tank bioreactor (CSTR) at $Q_{\text{in}} = 10$ L/h. The working volume is $V = 100$ L. Glucose is consumed by the cells with a volumetric rate $r_c = 0.20 \, C_A$ g/(L·h) (first-order kinetics). At steady state, determine (a) the outlet glucose concentration $C_A$, and (b) the dilution rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the process type.**
- Continuous flow (inlet = outlet): ✓
- Variables constant in time: ✓
- **→ Steady-State, Continuous Process with Single Reaction.**

**Step 2 — Write the component balance for glucose (A).**
The general component balance is:

$$
\frac{dM_A}{dt} = \dot{m}_{A,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{A,\text{out}} - r_{c,A} V
$$

For glucose there is **no generation** (it is a substrate, not a product), so $r_{g,A} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Convert to concentration form.**
Since $V$ is constant and $\dot{m}_{A} = Q C_A$:

$$
V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = Q_{\text{in}} C_{A,\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} C_{A,\text{out}} - r_{c,A} V
$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state condition.**
At steady state, $\frac{dC_A}{dt} = 0$. Also $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{out}} = Q$ for a constant-volume CSTR. So:

$$
0 = Q C_{A,\text{in}} - Q C_{A} - r_{c,A} V
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the kinetic expression.**
With $r_{c,A} = 0.20 \, C_A$ g/(L·h):

$$
0 = Q C_{A,\text{in}} - Q C_A - 0.20 \, C_A \, V
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $C_A$.**
Rearrange to collect $C_A$ terms:

$$
Q C_{A,\text{in}} = Q C_A + 0.20 \, V \, C_A
$$

$$
Q C_{A,\text{in}} = C_A (Q + 0.20 \, V)
$$

$$
C_A = \frac{Q C_{A,\text{in}}}{Q + 0.20 \, V}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values.**
$Q = 10$ L/h, $V = 100$ L, $C_{A,\text{in}} = 50$ g/L:

$$
C_A = \frac{(10)(50)}{10 + (0.20)(100)} = \frac{500}{10 + 20} = \frac{500}{30}
$$

$$
C_A = 16.67 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute dilution rate (part b).**

$$
D = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{10 \text{ L/h}}{100 \text{ L}} = 0.10 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 9 — Verification.**
At steady state, glucose consumed in reactor = glucose entering − glucose leaving = $10 \times 50 - 10 \times 16.67 = 333.3$ g/h. Glucose consumed by reaction = $0.20 \times 16.67 \times 100 = 333.4$ g/h. ✓ **Balance closes within rounding.**

### Summary Box of Results

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outlet glucose concentration | $C_A$ | **16.67** | **g/L** |
| Dilution rate | $D$ | **0.10** | **h⁻¹** |
| Residence time | $\tau = 1/D$ | **10** | **h** |
| Glucose consumed in reactor | $\dot{m}_{\text{cons}}$ | **333.3** | **g/h** |

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivative Example — Unsteady-State Batch

**Problem Statement:**
A 1000 L batch fermenter is charged with $C_{S,0} = 100$ g/L of substrate. The cells consume substrate at a zero-order rate $r_c = 2.0$ g/(L·h). No inflow, no outflow. (a) Write the substrate balance equation. (b) Find the time at which substrate is fully depleted.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify process type.**
- No inlet, no outlet, reaction occurring: **Batch (closed system, unsteady state).**

**Step 2 — Component balance for substrate $S$.**

$$
\frac{dM_S}{dt} = \dot{m}_{S,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{S,\text{out}} - r_{c,S} V
$$

**Step 3 — Apply batch conditions.**
$\dot{m}_{S,\text{in}} = 0$ and $\dot{m}_{S,\text{out}} = 0$. Also $M_S = C_S V$ with $V$ constant:

$$
V \frac{dC_S}{dt} = - r_{c,S} V
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and substitute kinetics.**

$$
\frac{dC_S}{dt} = - 2.0 \text{ g/(L·h)}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $t=0$ to $t=t_f$.**

$$
\int_{C_{S,0}}^{0} dC_S = -2.0 \int_{0}^{t_f} dt
$$

$$
0 - C_{S,0} = -2.0 \, t_f
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{C_{S,0}}{2.0} = \frac{100}{2.0} = 50 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 6 — Result.**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Initial substrate | $C_{S,0} = 100$ | g/L |
| Zero-order rate | $r_c = 2.0$ | g/(L·h) |
| **Time to full depletion** | $t_f = 50$ | **h** |
| Total substrate consumed | $100 \times 1000 = 100{,}000$ | g |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The 2.0 g/(L·h) zero-order rate is independent of concentration, which is why the substrate drops linearly with time. For first-order kinetics $r_c = k C_S$, the solution is **exponential decay**: $C_S(t) = C_{S,0} e^{-kt}$. Both forms appear in past KTU papers.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | Batch | Semi-Batch (Fed-Batch) | Continuous (Steady-State) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inflow during operation | No | Yes | Yes |
| Outflow during operation | No | No | Yes |
| Operating time horizon | Finite | Finite | Indefinite |
| Time dependence | Unsteady | Unsteady | Steady (typically) |
| Balance form | $\frac{dM}{dt} = \pm r V$ | $\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} \pm r V$ | $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$, $\dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} + r_g V = \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_c V$ |
| Downtime between batches | Yes | Yes | No |
| Flexibility | High | High | Low |
| Scale | Small to medium | Medium to large | Large |
| Example bioproduct | Penicillin, beer | Recombinant protein, fed-batch antibiotic | Ethanol, SCP, wastewater |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a total material balance and a component material balance. Under what circumstances is each preferred in bioprocess calculations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Total Material Balance (1 mark):** A total balance accounts for the **overall mass** of all species entering, leaving, accumulated, generated, and consumed in the system. The generation and consumption terms cancel each other, so reactions do not appear explicitly. Equation: $\frac{dM_{\text{tot}}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$.
- **Component Material Balance (1 mark):** A component balance is written **for each individual species** (substrate, biomass, product, O₂, CO₂, etc.) and includes **explicit reaction terms** — generation or consumption — based on stoichiometry. Equation: $\frac{dM_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i} V - r_{c,i} V$.
- **When preferred (1 mark):** Total balances are used for **equipment sizing, throughput estimation, and as a cross-check**; component balances are used for **kinetic modelling, yield calculations, and designing fermentation cycles** where individual species concentrations matter.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Classify the following processes as Batch, Semi-Batch (Fed-Batch), or Continuous, and state whether each is Steady-State or Unsteady-State:**
1. A 50,000 L ethanol fermenter running 24×7 with constant feed and product draw-off.
2. A penicillin production reactor where substrate is added continuously for 120 hours with no product removal until harvest.
3. A traditional brewery fermentation lasting 7 days, with yeast pitched once and beer drained at the end.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Continuous, Steady-State** — long-term operation, constant feed and draw-off, no accumulation.
2. **Semi-Batch (Fed-Batch), Unsteady-State** — feed added over time, no product removal during feeding, concentrations change with time.
3. **Batch, Unsteady-State** — no inflow or outflow during fermentation, substrate and biomass concentrations evolve until harvest.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) operates at **steady state** with a working volume of **200 L**. A glucose feed of concentration **40 g/L** enters at **8 L/h**. The outlet stream flow rate is also **8 L/h**. The cells consume glucose according to **Monod kinetics** with $\mu_{\max} = 0.40$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.50$ g/L, and $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$ g biomass/g glucose. The inlet is sterile (no cells). Calculate:
- (i) The dilution rate $D$.
- (ii) The steady-state substrate concentration $C_S$ in the reactor.
- (iii) The steady-state biomass concentration $C_X$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **batch fermenter** with $V = 500$ L is charged with an initial substrate concentration $C_{S,0} = 20$ g/L and initial biomass $C_{X,0} = 0.5$ g/L. The cell growth follows first-order kinetics in substrate, with specific growth rate $\mu = 0.25$ h⁻¹ (assumed constant during exponential phase). Biomass yield $Y_{X/S} = 0.40$ g/g. The fermentation is allowed to run until the substrate drops to $C_S = 2$ g/L. Determine:
- (i) The time required to reach $C_S = 2$ g/L.
- (ii) The final biomass concentration $C_{X,f}$.
- (iii) Classify the process: Batch/Semi-Batch/Continuous and Steady/Unsteady.

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Steady-State CSTR

**Step 1 — Compute dilution rate (i).**

$$
D = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{8 \text{ L/h}}{200 \text{ L}} = 0.04 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Monod kinetics expression (ii).**
At steady state in a CSTR with sterile feed, the cell and substrate balances reduce to:

$$
D = \mu = \frac{\mu_{\max} C_S}{K_s + C_S}
$$

Solving for $C_S$:

$$
D (K_s + C_S) = \mu_{\max} C_S
$$

$$
D \cdot K_s + D \cdot C_S = \mu_{\max} C_S
$$

$$
C_S (\mu_{\max} - D) = D \cdot K_s
$$

$$
C_S = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{\max} - D}
$$

Substitute values $D = 0.04$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.50$ g/L, $\mu_{\max} = 0.40$ h⁻¹:

$$
C_S = \frac{(0.04)(0.50)}{0.40 - 0.04} = \frac{0.020}{0.36} = 0.0556 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Biomass balance (iii).**
For sterile feed, biomass balance at steady state: $D C_X = \mu C_X$ — which is consistent since $D = \mu$. To find $C_X$, use the substrate balance:

$$
D (C_{S,\text{in}} - C_S) = \frac{\mu C_X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Rearranging (with $\mu = D$ at steady state):

$$
C_X = Y_{X/S} (C_{S,\text{in}} - C_S) = 0.45 \times (40 - 0.0556)
$$

$$
C_X = 0.45 \times 39.944 = 17.97 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Summary of Part (a):**
- Dilution rate $D = 0.04$ h⁻¹
- Substrate $C_S = 0.0556$ g/L
- Biomass $C_X = 17.97$ g/L

#### Part (b) — Batch Fermenter

**Step 1 — Identify process (iii).**
- No inflow, no outflow, time-dependent composition → **Batch, Unsteady-State**.

**Step 2 — Time evolution of substrate (i).**
With $\mu = 0.25$ h⁻¹ assumed constant and first-order consumption, the substrate balance:

$$
-\frac{dC_S}{dt} = \frac{\mu C_X}{Y_{X/S}} = \frac{\mu (C_{X,0} + Y_{X/S}(C_{S,0} - C_S))}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

A simpler KTU-style approach: assume exponential growth, so $C_X = C_{X,0} e^{\mu t}$ and substrate decreases proportionally. For small $C_{X,0}$:

$$
C_S(t) = C_{S,0} - \frac{C_{X,0}}{Y_{X/S}} (e^{\mu t} - 1)
$$

But a **simpler exam-friendly route** (often used in KTU): use linear consumption approximation, or directly use the **exponential form** for first-order:

$$
C_S(t) = C_{S,0} \cdot e^{-\mu t / Y_{X/S} \cdot (C_{X,0}/C_{S,0})}
$$

For KTU numericals, the cleanest approach is the **logistic** relation often presented as:

$$
t = \frac{1}{\mu} \ln \frac{C_{S,0}}{C_S}
$$

(derivable from $\frac{dC_S}{dt} = -\frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}} C_X$ with $C_X \approx Y_{X/S} (C_{S,0} - C_S)$).

Substituting $C_{S,0} = 20$ g/L, $C_S = 2$ g/L, $\mu = 0.25$ h⁻¹:

$$
t = \frac{1}{0.25} \ln \frac{20}{2} = 4 \times \ln(10) = 4 \times 2.3026 = 9.21 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 3 — Final biomass (ii).**

$$
C_{X,f} = C_{X,0} + Y_{X/S} (C_{S,0} - C_S) = 0.5 + 0.40 \times (20 - 2)
$$

$$
C_{X,f} = 0.5 + 0.40 \times 18 = 0.5 + 7.2 = 7.7 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Summary of Part (b):**
- Time to reach $C_S = 2$ g/L: $t = 9.21$ h
- Final biomass: $C_{X,f} = 7.7$ g/L
- Process type: **Batch, Unsteady-State**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **flow diagram and a universe table**, explain the procedure for solving a material balance problem involving a **continuous bioreactor operating at steady state** with a single biological reaction. Clearly state the assumptions, governing equations, and degrees of freedom analysis. List three industrial examples of such processes.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **fed-batch fermenter** is operated for 30 hours. The initial volume is $V_0 = 50$ L. Substrate is fed at concentration $C_{S,\text{in}} = 200$ g/L with a constant volumetric flow rate $Q = 1.5$ L/h. The initial substrate concentration in the reactor is $C_{S,0} = 10$ g/L, and the initial biomass is $C_{X,0} = 1$ g/L. Cells consume substrate with $Y_{X/S} = 0.50$ g/g. Assume substrate consumption is rapid enough that $C_S \approx 0$ in the reactor at all times (quasi-steady assumption), and that cell concentration builds up due to accumulation. Find:
- (i) The total volume at the end of the feed.
- (ii) The total mass of biomass produced.
- (iii) The total mass of substrate consumed.
- (iv) Classify the process: Batch/Semi-Batch/Continuous and Steady/Unsteady.

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Procedure for Steady-State Continuous Bioreactor

**Step 1 — Draw the flow diagram (1 mark).**
A CSTR is depicted as a vessel with **inlet stream** (substrate + medium), **outlet stream** (broth containing cells, residual substrate, products), and a **gas sparger** (for aerobic processes). Controller loops for temperature, pH, and dissolved oxygen are indicated.

**Step 2 — Universe Table (1 mark).**

| Component | In (g/h) | Out (g/h) | Generation (g/h) | Consumption (g/h) | Accumulation (g/h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Biomass (X) | 0 | $Q C_X$ | $r_X V$ | 0 | 0 |
| Substrate (S) | $Q C_{S,\text{in}}$ | $Q C_S$ | 0 | $r_S V$ | 0 |
| Product (P) | 0 | $Q C_P$ | $r_P V$ | 0 | 0 |
| O₂ | $Q_{O_2} C_{O_2,L}$ | $Q_{\text{out,gas}} C_{O_2,\text{out}}$ | 0 | $r_{O_2} V$ | 0 |
| **Total** | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}}$ | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | — | — | **0** |

**Step 3 — Governing equations (2 marks).**
For each species $i$ at steady state:

$$
\dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_{g,i} V - r_{c,i} V = 0
$$

The total balance: $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ (constant hold-up).

**Step 4 — Assumptions (1 mark).**
- Well-mixed (CSTR): uniform composition throughout.
- Steady state: all derivatives with respect to time vanish.
- Constant density and volume.
- Single limiting substrate; Monod kinetics applicable.
- No cell death or maintenance neglected unless stated.

**Step 5 — Degrees of freedom (1 mark).**
Number of unknowns − number of independent equations ≥ 0 for solvability. Identify feed conditions ($Q$, $C_{S,\text{in}}$), kinetic parameters ($\mu_{\max}$, $K_s$), yield $Y_{X/S}$, and reactor size $V$. Solve sequentially.

**Step 6 — Industrial examples (1 mark).**
- Continuous ethanol fermentation (Saccharomyces cerevisiae)
- Single-cell protein production from methanol (Methylophilus methylotrophus)
- Activated sludge process in wastewater treatment

#### Part (b) — Fed-Batch Fermenter Calculations

**Step 1 — Identify process (iv).**
- Substrate feed in, no outlet during feeding, accumulation occurs → **Semi-Batch (Fed-Batch), Unsteady-State**.

**Step 2 — Final volume (i).**
Volume increases linearly with feed flow:

$$
V_f = V_0 + Q \cdot t_f = 50 + 1.5 \times 30 = 50 + 45 = 95 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Substrate consumed (iii).**
Total substrate fed:

$$
M_{S,\text{in}} = C_{S,\text{in}} \cdot Q \cdot t_f = 200 \times 1.5 \times 30 = 9000 \text{ g}
$$

Since $C_S \approx 0$ in the reactor (quasi-steady), essentially all fed substrate is consumed:

$$
M_{S,\text{cons}} \approx 9000 \text{ g} = 9 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Biomass produced (ii).**
Using yield coefficient $Y_{X/S} = 0.50$ g/g:

$$
M_X = Y_{X/S} \cdot M_{S,\text{cons}} = 0.50 \times 9000 = 4500 \text{ g} = 4.5 \text{ kg}
$$

(Initial biomass $M_{X,0} = C_{X,0} \cdot V_0 = 1 \times 50 = 50$ g, so net production ≈ 4450 g.)

**Summary of Part (b):**
- Final volume: $V_f = 95$ L
- Biomass produced: $M_X = 4.5$ kg
- Substrate consumed: $M_{S,\text{cons}} = 9$ kg
- Process type: **Semi-Batch (Fed-Batch), Unsteady-State**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Material Balance Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set $\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$ for steady state** — many students write the full differential balance and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing component balance with total balance** — total balance has **no** reaction term; component balance does. Examiners deduct 2 marks if this is wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — using seconds instead of hours for fermentation time loses the answer-mark even if the formula is right. **Always use h (hours) for fermentation kinetics.**
> 4. **Sign convention errors** — generation is **positive** (mass produced), consumption is **negative** (mass removed). Swapping signs gives negative concentrations, which is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Omitting the universe table** in long problems — the 1 mark for "process identification" is forfeited.
> 6. **Incorrect classification** — calling a fed-batch a "batch" or a steady-state CSTR an "unsteady-state" process is a 1-mark deduction in Part A and can cascade into full wrong setup in Part B.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Universal balance equation:** $\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + r_g V - r_c V$. **Memorise this single line.**
- **Steady state** ⇒ set $\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$ ⇒ simplified algebraic form.
- **Batch** ⇒ $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 0$ ⇒ only reaction terms remain.
- **Continuous** ⇒ flow in = flow out, and steady state is usually assumed.
- **Semi-Batch / Fed-Batch** ⇒ one stream flows, the other does not; accumulation dominates.
- **Total balance** has no reaction term; **component balance** does.
- **Number of independent balances** = (number of species) − (number of independent reactions).
- **Standard units in KTU bioprocess problems:** concentration in **g/L**, flow in **L/h**, time in **h**, rate in **g/(L·h)**, dilution rate in **h⁻¹**.
- **Dilution rate** $D = Q/V$ and **residence time** $\tau = V/Q$ are reciprocals; both are favourite KTU question topics.
- **Universe table** before writing the balance is a guaranteed 1-mark insurance in Part B.
- For **Monod kinetics** at CSTR steady state: $D = \mu = \dfrac{\mu_{\max} C_S}{K_s + C_S}$ ⇒ $C_S = \dfrac{D K_s}{\mu_{\max} - D}$.
- For **zero-order batch consumption:** $C_S(t) = C_{S,0} - r t$ ⇒ linear decrease.
- For **first-order / Monod-driven batch:** use $t = \frac{1}{\mu}\ln\frac{C_{S,0}}{C_S}$ (simplified exponential form).
- The **monod, yield, and dilution rate** triad ($D = \mu$, $C_X = Y_{X/S}(C_{S,\text{in}} - C_S)$) is the **single most-tested cluster** in KTU bioprocess calculations.
- **Always check** at the end: mass in + generation = mass out + accumulation + consumption. If the numbers don't close, **revisit sign conventions first**.
- **Industrial rule of thumb:** continuous for high-volume low-margin (ethanol, SCP), batch for low-volume high-margin (therapeutic proteins, antibiotics).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Tie Element and the Basis for Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Tie Element and the Basis for Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Basis for Calculations

The **Basis of Calculation** is a **chosen reference quantity** (mass, moles, volume, or time) upon which all subsequent material and energy balance calculations are anchored. It is the *anchor point* of every chemical engineering problem — without it, the numerical values obtained in a balance are meaningless because they lack a frame of reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The *Basis* is an arbitrary but convenient quantity of a stream or component chosen as the starting reference (e.g., **100 kg of feed**, **1 hour of operation**, or **1 kmol of product**) on which the entire material balance is constructed.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Scaler"

Think of cooking. When a recipe says *"add 2 cups of flour to make 12 cookies"*, the **basis is 12 cookies (or the equivalent 2 cups of flour)**. If you want 36 cookies, you don't redo the recipe from scratch — you simply **scale up the basis by a factor of 3**. In the same way, in bioprocess calculations:

- The *basis* is like the recipe quantity.
- All stream flows and compositions are *ratios* linked to that basis.
- Changing the basis simply multiplies every number by a constant — the **ratios remain invariant**.

> [!NOTE]
> A basis is **not a physical truth**, it is a **mathematical convenience**. The final answer must always be reported back to the *real* operating condition (e.g., per day, per year) using a scale-up factor.

---

## 1.2 The Concept of Tie Element (Tie Substance / Tie Component)

A **Tie Element** (also called the *Tie Substance* or *Reference Component*) is a **chemically inert or non-transferred component** that is present in *one or more streams* of a process and whose **amount is known or can be tracked unambiguously** across the system. It acts as the *mathematical bridge* that allows us to relate the unknown flow rates of different streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Tie Element* is a component that **passes through a process unit unchanged** (i.e., inert, non-reactive, non-volatile, or non-diffusing) and whose inlet and outlet quantities can be equated to link the unknown flow rates of adjacent streams.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Money Trail"

Imagine you walk into a shop with **₹1000** and walk out with some items and **₹250** change. You don't need to weigh every item; you already *know* the money trail: **₹1000 in = ₹250 out → ₹750 spent on items**.

The **money is the tie element**:
- It enters in a known amount.
- It leaves in a known amount.
- The difference tells you about the unknown items.

Similarly, in a fermenter, if **dry cell mass** enters as 5 kg and leaves as 50 kg, but you *know* the substrate glucose is fully consumed (inlet 0, outlet 0), you can use **dry cell mass** or **ash** as the tie element to relate inlet broth to outlet broth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Tie Elements in Bioprocesses:**
> - **Inert gases** (N₂, O₂) — used in gas-phase balances (e.g., aeration).
> - **Ash** — used in combustion of biomass.
> - **Water (in drying)** — when drying is on a dry basis.
> - **Dry solid / Cell mass** — when volatile components are stripped off.
> - **Non-volatile solute** — in evaporation/crystallization.
> - **Solvent** — in liquid–liquid extraction.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Concepts Are Inseparable

The **Basis** tells us *how much* of a known stream to start with.
The **Tie Element** tells us *which component* to use to relate the unknown streams to that known starting quantity.

Without a basis → numbers float without scale.
Without a tie element → multiple unknowns, under-determined system.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of KTU Bioprocess Calculations:**
> **"Choose the basis on the stream whose composition is *most completely known*, then identify the tie element on the stream that connects the unknown streams back to that basis."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Criteria for Choosing the Basis of Calculation

A basis should be selected based on the following engineering logic:

1. **Choose a stream where the composition is fully known** (e.g., pure component, known mass fractions).
2. **Choose a round number** — e.g., **100 kg**, **1 kmol**, **1 ton**, **1 hour** — to avoid decimals.
3. **Choose the smallest convenient quantity** if the problem has no time reference.
4. **If the product is unknown, never choose the product as the basis.**
5. **If the feed is unknown but the product is known, choose the product as the basis and work backwards.**

### 2.1.1 Common Basis Choices Used in KTU Problems

| **Situation** | **Recommended Basis** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Steady-state process with known feed | **100 kg (or 100 kmol) of feed** | Composition is fully specified |
| Batch process | **1 batch** | Process is discrete |
| Continuous process with time | **1 hour (or 1 day) of operation** | Time defines the throughput |
| Unknown feed, known product | **1 kg (or 1 ton) of product** | Works backwards through the system |
| Air-related process (e.g., drying) | **100 kmol of dry air** | Dry air is the conserved phase |
| Combustion / Incineration | **100 kg of fuel** | Fuel composition is given |

> [!NOTE]
> Once a basis is chosen, **all compositions must be expressed on the same reference** (e.g., if basis is 100 kg of wet feed, moisture must be expressed as kg water per 100 kg wet feed).

---

## 2.2 Properties of an Ideal Tie Element

A component is a *good* tie element if it satisfies the following:

| **Property** | **Why It Matters** |
|---|---|
| **Inert / Non-reactive** | No generation or consumption across the unit |
| **Single phase behaviour** | Doesn't split ambiguously between phases |
| **Known in at least one stream** | Provides the numerical anchor |
| **Non-volatile (if liquid–vapour)** | Doesn't transfer with the vapour |
| **Conserved across the unit** | In = Out (no accumulation) |

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Statement of the Tie Element Principle

For a tie element *i* passing through a process unit with no reaction and no accumulation (steady state):

$$n_{i,\text{in}} = n_{i,\text{out}}$$

where $n$ is the moles (or mass) of component *i*. If the inlet stream has flow $F_1$ with mole fraction $x_{i,1}$, and the outlet stream has flow $F_2$ with mole fraction $x_{i,2}$:

$$F_1 \cdot x_{i,1} = F_2 \cdot x_{i,2}$$

This single equation **closes the relationship between $F_1$ and $F_2$** even when other components are reacting or transferring.

---

## 2.3 General Material Balance Framework

For any process unit (with reaction terms):

$$\text{Input} + \text{Generation} = \text{Output} + \text{Consumption} + \text{Accumulation}$$

For a **Tie Element** (no reaction, no accumulation):

$$\text{Input} = \text{Output}$$

For an **Overall Mass Balance** (steady state):

$$\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete Cheat Code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1: Essential Formulas for Tie Element & Basis Calculations**

| **#** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_1 \cdot x_{i,1} = F_2 \cdot x_{i,2}$ | Tie element balance across one unit |
| 2 | $\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ | Overall steady-state mass balance |
| 3 | $\sum x_i = 1$ | Summation of mass/mole fractions |
| 4 | $\text{Scale-up factor} = \dfrac{\text{Desired basis}}{\text{Chosen basis}}$ | Converts from assumed to actual basis |
| 5 | $x_{\text{dry basis}} = \dfrac{x_{\text{wet basis}}}{1 - x_{\text{wet basis}}}$ | Wet → Dry basis conversion (mass frac) |
| 6 | $x_{\text{wet basis}} = \dfrac{x_{\text{dry basis}}}{1 + x_{\text{dry basis}}}$ | Dry → Wet basis conversion |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{\text{product}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Desired feed}}{\text{Chosen basis}}$ | Time-based basis scaling |
| 8 | $F_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{i,\text{in}}}{x_{i,\text{out}}}$ | Tie element flow-rate relation |
| 9 | $\% \text{ Recovery} = \dfrac{\text{Product obtained}}{\text{Feed input}} \times 100$ | Component recovery calculation |
| 10 | $m_{\text{dry}} = m_{\text{wet}} \cdot (1 - w)$ | Dry mass from wet mass (w = moisture mass frac) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drying Operations** → Use **dry solid** as the tie element to find moisture removal.
- **Evaporation / Crystallization** → Use **non-volatile solute** as the tie element to find water evaporated.
- **Fermentation** → Use **inert gas (N₂)** or **ash** to relate inlet air to outlet gas.
- **Membrane Separation** → Use **retained solute** as the tie element to find permeate composition.
- **Biomass Combustion** → Use **ash** (incombustible) as the tie element to find flue gas flow.
- **Distillation** → Use a *non-distributing* heavy component as the tie element to relate feed, distillate, and bottoms.

> [!TIP]
> In a **KTU exam**, after writing the basis, **always draw the block diagram** with all stream labels and tick-marks for known compositions. Examiners award marks for *visual clarity*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Multi-Stream Bioprocess Balance

### **Problem Statement**

A fermentation broth is fed to a **centrifuge** for cell separation. The **feed broth** contains:
- **Cells:** 15 % by mass
- **Water:** 80 % by mass
- **Other soluble metabolites:** 5 % by mass

The centrifuge produces:
- A **cell concentrate (heavy phase)** containing **25 % cells, 70 % water, 5 % metabolites**
- A **clarified liquid (light phase)** containing **1 % cells, 94 % water, 5 % metabolites**

**Calculate per 1000 kg of feed broth:**
1. Mass of cell concentrate produced
2. Mass of clarified liquid produced
3. Verify using an overall mass balance

---

### **Step 1 — Draw the Block Diagram and Label All Streams**

```
                    F1 = 1000 kg  (Feed Broth)
                    Cells = 15 %, Water = 80 %, Metabolites = 5 %
                                 |
                                 v
                          +------------+
                          |            |
                          | Centrifuge |
                          |            |
                          +------------+
                                 |
                  ________________|________________
                 |                                |
                 v                                v
        F2 (Cell Concentrate)          F3 (Clarified Liquid)
        Cells = 25 %                   Cells = 1 %
        Water = 70 %                   Water = 94 %
        Metabolites = 5 %              Metabolites = 5 %
```

---

### **Step 2 — Choose the Basis of Calculation**

The **feed composition is fully known** (15 %, 80 %, 5 %). Choose:

> **Basis:** **1000 kg of feed broth (F1)**

This is the actual operating quantity, so the final answers will **not require a scale-up factor**.

---

### **Step 3 — Identify the Tie Element**

Examine each component's behaviour across the centrifuge:

| Component | Behaviour in Centrifuge | Suitable as Tie? |
|---|---|---|
| Cells | Partially retained (15 % → 25 % in concentrate, 1 % in liquid) | **No** (splits) |
| Water | Splits (80 % → 70 % in conc., 94 % in liquid) | **No** (splits) |
| **Metabolites (soluble)** | Stays fully in the liquid phase (5 % → 5 % in *both* phases) | **Possible** |
| **Total mass** | Conserved | **Yes — for overall balance** |

**Key observation:** Looking carefully at the **metabolites** — they are *soluble* in water and at 5 % in **every** stream. This is a **trap**! A 5 % → 5 % with no split means it *appears* conserved, but it is conserved only in *concentration*, not necessarily in *absolute amount* (we need to verify with mass balance).

The **truly safe tie element** for this problem is the **overall mass balance** (conservation of total mass), because the centrifuge is a *physical separation* with no reaction.

For a **second independent equation**, we can use the **cells** as a quasi-tie by writing the cell balance (since cells are conserved across the unit — they only *redistribute*, not generate or consume):

$$\text{Cells In} = \text{Cells Out}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cells are conserved in physical separation** (no reaction, no generation, no consumption). They can be used as a tie element here.

---

### **Step 4 — List the Unknowns**

- $F_2$ = mass of cell concentrate (kg) ← **Unknown 1**
- $F_3$ = mass of clarified liquid (kg) ← **Unknown 2**

We have **2 unknowns** → we need **2 independent equations**.

---

### **Step 5 — Write the Independent Equations**

**Equation A — Overall Mass Balance (Total mass is conserved):**

$$F_1 = F_2 + F_3$$

Substituting the basis:

$$1000 = F_2 + F_3 \quad \text{...(A)}$$

**Equation B — Cell Mass Balance (Cell tie element):**

Cells entering in feed = Cells leaving in both outlets.

$$F_1 \cdot x_{\text{cells},1} = F_2 \cdot x_{\text{cells},2} + F_3 \cdot x_{\text{cells},3}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$1000 \times 0.15 = F_2 \times 0.25 + F_3 \times 0.01$$

$$150 = 0.25 F_2 + 0.01 F_3 \quad \text{...(B)}$$

---

### **Step 6 — Solve the System of Equations Algebraically**

From Equation (A):

$$F_3 = 1000 - F_2$$

Substitute into Equation (B):

$$150 = 0.25 F_2 + 0.01 (1000 - F_2)$$

Expand the second term:

$$150 = 0.25 F_2 + 10 - 0.01 F_2$$

Combine like terms:

$$150 = (0.25 - 0.01) F_2 + 10$$

$$150 = 0.24 F_2 + 10$$

Isolate $F_2$:

$$0.24 F_2 = 150 - 10 = 140$$

$$F_2 = \frac{140}{0.24} = 583.33 \text{ kg}$$

Then:

$$F_3 = 1000 - 583.33 = 416.67 \text{ kg}$$

---

### **Step 7 — State the Final Answers**

| **Stream** | **Mass (kg)** | **Cells (kg)** | **Water (kg)** | **Metabolites (kg)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed (F1) | 1000.00 | 150.00 | 800.00 | 50.00 |
| Concentrate (F2) | **583.33** | 145.83 | 408.33 | 29.17 |
| Clarified (F3) | **416.67** | 4.17 | 391.67 | 20.83 |
| **Total Out** | **1000.00** | **150.00** | **800.00** | **50.00** |

---

### **Step 8 — Verification (Sanity Check)**

**Cell mass check:** $145.83 + 4.17 = 150.00$ kg ✓ (matches feed)
**Total mass check:** $583.33 + 416.67 = 1000.00$ kg ✓

> [!TIP]
> Always perform a **conservation check** by adding outlet component masses and comparing with inlet. KTU examiners award bonus marks for this verification step.

---

## 3.2 Second Worked Example — Choosing Basis on a Product Stream

### **Problem Statement**

A dryer takes in a **wet solid feed** containing **50 % water (wet basis)** and produces a **product** containing **10 % water (wet basis)**. The dryer removes **400 kg of water per hour**.

**Calculate:**
1. The mass flow rate of the wet feed (kg/h)
2. The mass flow rate of the dried product (kg/h)
3. Verify using the **dry-solid tie element**

---

### **Step 1 — Choose the Basis**

The unknown stream is the **feed**, but the **water removed (400 kg/h)** is given as a *time-based rate*. Choose:

> **Basis:** **1 hour of operation** (so all final answers are in kg/h directly)

---

### **Step 2 — Identify the Tie Element**

In drying, **water is removed but dry solid is conserved** (it is non-volatile).

**Tie Element = Dry Solid**

Let $m_{\text{feed}}$ = mass of wet feed per hour, and $m_{\text{prod}}$ = mass of dry product per hour.

---

### **Step 3 — Write the Component Balances**

**Dry basis conversion of compositions:**

- Feed: 50 % water (wet) → Dry solid = 50 %
- Product: 10 % water (wet) → Dry solid = 90 %

**Dry solid tie balance (In = Out):**

$$m_{\text{feed}} \times 0.50 = m_{\text{prod}} \times 0.90 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Water mass balance:**

$$m_{\text{feed}} \times 0.50 = m_{\text{prod}} \times 0.10 + 400 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

(Water in feed = Water leaving in product + Water evaporated)

---

### **Step 4 — Solve the System**

From (1):

$$m_{\text{feed}} = \frac{0.90}{0.50} \cdot m_{\text{prod}} = 1.8 \, m_{\text{prod}}$$

Substitute into (2):

$$1.8 \, m_{\text{prod}} \times 0.50 = 0.10 \, m_{\text{prod}} + 400$$

$$0.90 \, m_{\text{prod}} = 0.10 \, m_{\text{prod}} + 400$$

$$0.80 \, m_{\text{prod}} = 400$$

$$m_{\text{prod}} = 500 \text{ kg/h}$$

Then:

$$m_{\text{feed}} = 1.8 \times 500 = 900 \text{ kg/h}$$

---

### **Step 5 — Final Answer Table**

| **Stream** | **Total (kg/h)** | **Dry Solid (kg/h)** | **Water (kg/h)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wet Feed | **900** | 450 | 450 |
| Dry Product | **500** | 450 | 50 |
| Water Evaporated | **400** | 0 | 400 |
| **Total Check** | **900 ✓** | **450 ✓** | **500 = 450 + 50 ✓** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often forget to convert wet-basis to dry-basis compositions before applying the tie element balance. The 50 % water on a wet basis is **NOT** the same as 50 % water on a dry basis.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Strategy Matrix — Choosing the Right Tie Element

| **Process Type** | **Best Tie Element** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Drying of solids | **Dry solid (or bone-dry solid)** | Non-volatile, fully retained |
| Evaporation of solution | **Non-volatile solute** | Stays in liquid phase |
| Distillation | **Non-key heavy component** | Does not vaporize |
| Combustion of biomass | **Ash** | Incombustible residue |
| Fermentation (gas–liquid) | **Inert gas (N₂)** | Does not react |
| Crystallization | **Anhydrous salt** | Solute conserved in solid + mother liquor |
| Absorption (gas scrubbing) | **Inert carrier gas** | Not absorbed |
| Membrane filtration | **Retained solute** | Stays in retentate |
| Liquid–liquid extraction | **Carrier solvent (raffinate diluent)** | Insoluble in extract phase |
| Leaching | **Insoluble solid matrix** | Stays in solid phase |

> [!TIP]
> **General Rule of Thumb:** The tie element is *always* the component that **does NOT participate in mass transfer or reaction** across the boundary of the unit operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Methodology Flowchart — Solving a Material Balance Problem

The following flowchart captures the **systematic procedure** a KTU student must follow when solving any material balance problem using the basis + tie element framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read the Problem Statement] --> B[Step 1: Draw the Block Diagram]
    B --> C[Step 2: List all Known Compositions and Flow Rates]
    C --> D{Is the Feed Composition Fully Known?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Choose Basis = 100 kg of Feed]
    D -- No --> F{Is the Product Composition Fully Known?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Choose Basis = 1 kg or 1 ton of Product]
    F -- No --> H[Choose Basis on Stream with Most Data]
    E --> I[Step 3: Identify Unknown Streams and Unknowns]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step 4: Identify the Tie Element]
    J --> K{Tie Element Inert and Conserved?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Write Tie Element Balance: F1 xi1 = F2 xi2]
    K -- No --> M[Re-examine: Look for Inert Phase or Non-volatile Component]
    M --> J
    L --> N[Step 5: Write Overall Mass Balance]
    N --> O[Step 6: Write Component Balances as Needed]
    O --> P[Step 7: Solve the System of Equations]
    P --> Q[Step 8: Verify by Checking Total Mass In = Total Mass Out]
    Q --> R[END: Report Final Answers with Units]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Tie Element Linking Multiple Streams

The diagram below illustrates how a **single tie element** creates a *mathematical bridge* between two otherwise unrelated streams in a process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inlet[INLET STREAM F1]
        F1A[Component A: 40%]
        F1B[Tie Element T: 30%]
        F1C[Component B: 30%]
    end

    Inlet --> SEP[SEPARATION UNIT]

    subgraph Outlet1[OUTLET STREAM F2 - HEAVY PHASE]
        F2A[Component A: 70%]
        F2B[Tie Element T: 20%]
        F2C[Component B: 10%]
    end

    subgraph Outlet2[OUTLET STREAM F3 - LIGHT PHASE]
        F3A[Component A: 5%]
        F3B[Tie Element T: 45%]
        F3C[Component B: 50%]
    end

    SEP --> Outlet1
    SEP --> Outlet2

    F1B -. TIE ELEMENT BRIDGE .-> F2B
    F1B -. TIE ELEMENT BRIDGE .-> F3B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed arrows** in the diagram show the *information flow* (not physical flow). The tie element T appears in all three streams, and its mass balance **F1 × xT,1 = F2 × xT,2 + F3 × xT,3** provides the crucial equation to solve the otherwise under-determined system.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Single-Unit Tie Element Application

A simplified, single-tie-element view suitable for a quick KTU exam solution sketch:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEED: F1 kg<br/>Composition Known<br/>Tie T = xT1] -->|Process| B[UNIT OPERATION]
    B -->|Outlet| C[PRODUCT: F2 kg<br/>Composition Known<br/>Tie T = xT2]

    A2[Tie Element Equation:<br/>F1 xT1 = F2 xT2] -.->|Links Streams| A
    A2 -.-> C
```

> [!TIP]
> In the exam, the **flowchart with tie element annotation** is considered *good practice* by KTU evaluators — it shows structured thinking and earns *method marks* even if the final numerical answer is incorrect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the terms (a) Basis of calculation, and (b) Tie element, with two examples each from bioprocess engineering.**

#### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Basis of Calculation:**
The basis of calculation is an **arbitrarily chosen, convenient reference quantity** of a process stream (such as 100 kg of feed, 1 kmol of product, or 1 hour of operation) on which all material balance equations are constructed. It establishes the *numerical scale* of the problem. Once chosen, all stream flow rates and compositions are expressed relative to this basis. The final answer is then scaled to the actual plant operating condition using a ratio.

> **Examples in Bioprocess:**
> - **100 kg of fermentation broth** taken as basis for a centrifuge material balance.
> - **1 hour of operation** taken as basis for a continuous dryer calculating water evaporation rate.

**(b) Tie Element:**
A tie element is a **component that passes through a process unit unchanged** (i.e., it does not react, evaporate, or transfer between phases) and is therefore used to **mathematically link the unknown flow rates of different streams** by equating its input and output amounts.

> **Examples in Bioprocess:**
> - **Dry solid (bone-dry solid)** in a drying operation — non-volatile, fully retained.
> - **Ash (inert mineral matter)** in biomass combustion — non-combustible, conserved.
> - *(Additional valid example: Inert N₂ gas in aeration of a fermenter.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Note (3-Mark Allocation):**
> - 1 mark for each formal definition.
> - 0.5 marks for each relevant bioprocess example.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List any six criteria for the selection of a suitable basis of calculation. Why is it not advisable to choose the product stream as the basis when the feed composition is known?**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Criteria for selecting a basis:**

1. The stream chosen should have a **fully known composition**.
2. The numerical value should be **round and convenient** (e.g., 100, 1000) to avoid decimals.
3. The basis should be the **smallest convenient quantity** that captures the essence of the problem.
4. For **batch processes**, the basis is typically **one batch**.
5. For **continuous processes**, the basis is often **one hour (or one day) of operation**.
6. The basis should **not be a stream whose flow rate is itself the unknown to be found**.
7. The basis should allow **easy conversion** to the actual plant condition at the end of the calculation.

**Why the product stream should not be the basis when the feed is known:**

When the feed composition is **fully specified** and the product is **not**, choosing the product as the basis would require us to *work backwards* through the process to find the feed, which **introduces unnecessary complexity and increases the chance of algebraic error**. Instead, taking the feed (where the composition is already known) as the basis allows a *forward calculation* — every downstream stream can be computed directly using material balances. Additionally, the final answer is more directly meaningful because the plant operator knows the feed rate, not the product rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

A **single-effect evaporator** concentrates a **fruit juice** from an initial concentration of **12 % total solids (by mass)** to a final concentration of **50 % total solids (by mass)**. The feed rate is **2000 kg/h**.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The mass flow rate of the **concentrated product (kg/h)** and the mass flow rate of the **water evaporated (kg/h)**.
**(b)** If the evaporator is operated for **8 hours per day for 25 days per month**, calculate the **monthly water consumption (in tons)** and the **monthly concentrated juice production (in tons)**. Use the dry-solid tie element to solve the problem.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Step 1 — Choose the Basis:**

The feed is given directly as 2000 kg/h, so the natural basis is:

> **Basis: 1 hour of operation**

This means all answers will be in kg/h directly.

**Step 2 — Identify the Tie Element:**

In an evaporator, water is removed by vaporization. The **total solids (non-volatile sugars, fibres, etc.)** are conserved. Therefore:

> **Tie Element = Total Dry Solids**

**Step 3 — Set Up the Diagram and Unknowns:**

```
   F1 = 2000 kg/h (Feed: 12% solids, 88% water)
              |
              v
       +--------------+
       |              |
       |  EVAPORATOR  |
       |              |
       +--------------+
              |
   ___________|___________
  |                       |
  v                       v
  F2 (Concentrated       F3 (Water Vapour,
  Juice)                  Condensate)
  50% solids             0% solids (pure water)
  50% water
```

Unknowns: $F_2$ and $F_3$

**Step 4 — Write the Equations:**

**Equation (a) — Overall Mass Balance:**

$$F_1 = F_2 + F_3$$

$$2000 = F_2 + F_3 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Equation (b) — Tie Element Balance (Dry Solids, In = Out):**

$$F_1 \cdot x_{s,1} = F_2 \cdot x_{s,2} + F_3 \cdot x_{s,3}$$

Since $F_3$ is pure water vapour, $x_{s,3} = 0$:

$$2000 \times 0.12 = F_2 \times 0.50 + F_3 \times 0$$

$$240 = 0.50 \, F_2 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

From (2):

$$F_2 = \frac{240}{0.50} = 480 \text{ kg/h}$$

From (1):

$$F_3 = 2000 - 480 = 1520 \text{ kg/h}$$

---

**Part (a) Final Answer:**

| **Stream** | **Mass Flow Rate** |
|---|---|
| Concentrated Juice (F2) | **480 kg/h** |
| Water Evaporated (F3) | **1520 kg/h** |

---

**Part (b) — Scale-up to Monthly Operation:**

Operating schedule: **8 hours/day × 25 days/month = 200 hours/month**

**Scale-up factor:** 200 hours/month

**Monthly Concentrated Juice Production:**

$$\text{Monthly} = 480 \text{ kg/h} \times 200 \text{ h/month} = 96{,}000 \text{ kg/month} = \mathbf{96 \text{ tons/month}}$$

**Monthly Water Evaporated (Consumption from feed):**

$$\text{Monthly} = 1520 \text{ kg/h} \times 200 \text{ h/month} = 304{,}000 \text{ kg/month} = \mathbf{304 \text{ tons/month}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Units:** 1 ton = 1000 kg. The KTU convention in bioprocess problems is to use SI units throughout; conversions to tons are only for reporting.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

A **biomass combustion unit** burns a **wood waste** containing **45 % carbon, 6 % hydrogen, 38 % oxygen, 1 % nitrogen, and 10 % ash** (all on a dry mass basis). The wood is fed at a rate of **500 kg/h** with a moisture content of **40 % (wet basis)**. The combustion uses **20 % excess air** (composition: 21 % O₂, 79 % N₂ by volume). The dry flue gas analysis shows **12 % CO₂, 3 % O₂, 85 % N₂** (by volume) on a dry basis.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The **mass of dry air supplied per hour** using N₂ as the tie element.
**(b)** The **actual air–fuel ratio** and compare it with the **stoichiometric requirement**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Step 1 — Choose the Basis:**

> **Basis: 1 hour of operation** → All flow rates in kg/h

**Step 2 — Compute the Composition of the Wet Wood Feed:**

Dry wood composition (per 100 kg dry wood):
- C: 45 kg, H: 6 kg, O: 38 kg, N: 1 kg, Ash: 10 kg

Since moisture is 40 % (wet basis):

$$w_{\text{moisture}} = 0.40 \implies m_{\text{dry wood}} = m_{\text{wet}} \times (1 - 0.40) = 0.60 \, m_{\text{wet}}$$

Per 500 kg/h wet feed:

$$m_{\text{dry wood}} = 500 \times 0.60 = 300 \text{ kg/h}$$

Component masses in dry wood feed:

| Component | Mass (kg/h) |
|---|---|
| Carbon | $0.45 \times 300 = 135.0$ |
| Hydrogen | $0.06 \times 300 = 18.0$ |
| Oxygen | $0.38 \times 300 = 114.0$ |
| Nitrogen | $0.01 \times 300 = 3.0$ |
| Ash | $0.10 \times 300 = 30.0$ |
| Moisture (water) | $0.40 \times 500 = 200.0$ |

**Step 3 — Identify the Tie Element:**

In combustion, **N₂ from the air** is the ideal tie element because:
- It is **inert** (does not react in normal combustion).
- It passes entirely from air to the flue gas.
- The wood contributes only a small, known amount of nitrogen.

> **Tie Element = N₂ (from air)**

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement:**

The combustion reactions are:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

$$4\text{H} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad (\text{neglect S here})$$

Moles of O₂ required (per hour, using mass / molar mass):

- For C: $\dfrac{135}{12} = 11.25$ kmol C → 11.25 kmol O₂
- For H: $\dfrac{18}{2} = 9.0$ kmol H → $\dfrac{9.0}{2} = 4.5$ kmol O₂
- Internal O₂ from fuel: $\dfrac{114}{32} = 3.5625$ kmol O₂ (available)

**Net stoichiometric O₂ required:**

$$n_{\text{O}_2,\text{stoich}} = 11.25 + 4.5 - 3.5625 = 12.1875 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Actual O₂ Supplied (20 % excess):**

$$n_{\text{O}_2,\text{actual}} = 1.20 \times 12.1875 = 14.625 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{h}$$

**Step 6 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement:**

In air, O₂ is 21 % by volume (≈ 21 % by mole):

$$n_{\text{air,stoich}} = \frac{12.1875}{0.21} = 58.0357 \text{ kmol air/h}$$

$$m_{\text{air,stoich}} = 58.0357 \times 28.97 = 1681.5 \text{ kg air/h}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the N₂ Tie Element Balance:**

Actual N₂ supplied with air:

$$n_{\text{N}_2,\text{in air}} = 14.625 \times \frac{0.79}{0.21} = 14.625 \times 3.7619 = 55.015 \text{ kmol N}_2/\text{h}$$

Add N₂ from fuel: $\dfrac{3.0}{28} = 0.107$ kmol N₂/h (negligible, but include for accuracy)

$$n_{\text{N}_2,\text{total in}} = 55.015 + 0.107 = 55.122 \text{ kmol N}_2/\text{h}$$

The dry flue gas is **85 % N₂** (on a dry basis). Let $n_{\text{dry flue gas}}$ be the total kmol of dry flue gas. By N₂ balance:

$$0.85 \cdot n_{\text{dry flue gas}} = 55.122$$

$$n_{\text{dry flue gas}} = 64.85 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Mass of Air Actually Supplied:**

Moles of air supplied (per hour) = moles of N₂ in air / 0.79:

$$n_{\text{air,actual}} = \frac{55.015}{0.79} = 69.639 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$m_{\text{air,actual}} = 69.639 \times 28.97 = 2017.5 \text{ kg air/h}$$

**Step 9 — Air–Fuel Ratios:**

$$\text{AF}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1681.5}{500} = 3.36 \text{ kg air/kg wet wood}$$

$$\text{AF}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{2017.5}{500} = 4.04 \text{ kg air/kg wet wood}$$

**Ratio of actual to stoichiometric:**

$$\frac{\text{AF}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{AF}_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{4.04}{3.36} = 1.20 \quad \checkmark \text{ (consistent with 20% excess air)}$$

---

**Part (a) Final Answer:** Mass of dry air supplied = **2017.5 kg/h**

**Part (b) Final Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Stoichiometric air | 1681.5 kg/h |
| Actual air supplied | 2017.5 kg/h |
| Stoichiometric A/F | 3.36 |
| Actual A/F | 4.04 |
| Excess air | 20 % ✓ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Basis & Tie Element Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the basis explicitly** (–1 mark). The phrase *"Let the basis be 100 kg of feed"* **must** be the first line of the solution.
>
> 2. **Not drawing a labelled block diagram** (–2 marks). Even a rough sketch with stream labels earns full *method marks*.
>
> 3. **Choosing a product as the basis when the feed is known** (–1 mark penalty in some valuation schemes). The *forward* calculation is always preferred.
>
> 4. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis compositions** (–2 to –3 marks). The single most common error. Always state explicitly *"on a wet basis"* or *"on a dry basis"* and convert if needed.
>
> 5. **Using a component that is not truly inert as the tie element** (entire problem invalidated). E.g., using *glucose* as a tie in a *fermenter* is **wrong** because glucose is consumed.
>
> 6. **Skipping the verification step** (–1 mark). Always add the outlet masses and check they equal the inlet mass.
>
> 7. **Wrong unit conversions** (–1 mark). Remember: **1 ton = 1000 kg**; **1 kmol = 1000 mol**; **1 wt% = mass fraction / 100**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Concept of Tie Element and Basis for Calculations**

- **Basis of Calculation:** An *arbitrary, convenient* reference quantity (e.g., 100 kg, 1 hour) chosen at the **start** of every material balance problem.
- **Tie Element:** A component that is **chemically and physically conserved** across a process unit; it provides the *bridge equation* between unknown stream flow rates.
- **Tie Element Equation (steady state):** $F_{\text{in}} \cdot x_{T,\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{T,\text{out}}$ (or sum of all outlet terms if there are multiple outlets).
- **Criteria for choosing a basis:** Stream with **fully known composition** + **round number** + **convenient scale**.
- **Criteria for choosing a tie element:** **Inert**, **non-volatile**, **single-phase**, **conserved**, **known in at least one stream**.
- **Common tie elements in bioprocesses:** Dry solid, ash, N₂ (inert gas), non-volatile solute, insoluble matrix, retained solute, carrier solvent.
- **Wet basis vs Dry basis:** Always specify and convert using $x_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{x_{\text{wet}}}{1 - x_{\text{wet}}}$.
- **Verification step:** Total mass in = Total mass out + Accumulation. For steady state: In = Out.
- **Number of unknowns = Number of independent equations** (this determines if the problem is *fully specified*, *under-specified*, or *over-specified*).
- **Scale-up factor:** $\text{New value} = \text{Calculated value} \times \dfrac{\text{Actual basis}}{\text{Chosen basis}}$.
- **Drawing a block diagram** is **mandatory** in KTU solutions — it is worth **2 marks** by itself.
- **Most-used basis in KTU exams:** **100 kg of feed** (when feed composition is known).
- **Most-used tie element in KTU exams:** **Dry solid** (for drying/evaporation) or **N₂** (for combustion/aeration).
- **Always** write: *"Basis: 100 kg of [stream]"* as the **first** line of the answer.
- **Always** write: *"Tie Element: [Component name] because [reason]"* as the **second** key declaration.
- **Always** end with a **conservation table** to verify the solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Independent Material Balance Equations, Degrees of Freedom, and Steps for Solving Material Balance Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Independent Material Balance Equations, Degrees of Freedom, and Steps for Solving Material Balance Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Independent Material Balance Equations

An **Independent Material Balance Equation** is a material balance statement for a single chemical species (component) whose information cannot be derived by combining (adding or subtracting) any of the other material balance equations written for the system. In other words, it contributes a *new, non-redundant piece of information* to the problem.

For any non-reactive process involving **N_C chemical components**, the maximum number of independent material balances is exactly **N_C** (one for each component). However, because the **overall (total mass) balance is simply the sum of all N_C component balances**, it is mathematically dependent on the component balances. Therefore:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Independent Material Balances = N_C (number of components)**
> - If you write the overall balance + (N_C − 1) component balances → still only **N_C independent** equations.
> - If you write only the N_C component balances → still only **N_C independent** equations.
> - You **cannot** write all N_C component balances **plus** the overall balance and treat them as N_C + 1 independent equations — the overall balance is *redundant*.

In **reactive systems** with **R independent reactions**, one additional dependent relationship is introduced for each reaction (the stoichiometric constraint). Hence, the number of independent balances reduces to:

$$
N_{\text{indep}} = N_C - R
$$

## 1.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

The **Degrees of Freedom** of a material balance problem is defined as:

$$
\text{DOF} \;=\; N_{\text{unknown variables}} \;-\; N_{\text{independent equations}}
$$

where the *unknown variables* are the unknown stream flow rates, compositions, or split fractions, and the *independent equations* include the material balances plus any auxiliary specifications (e.g., compositions given, split ratios, conversion percentages).

> [!NOTE]
> - **DOF = 0** → System is *exactly specified* and has a unique solution.
> - **DOF > 0** → System is *underspecified* (more unknowns than equations) — needs additional information.
> - **DOF < 0** → System is *overspecified* (more equations than unknowns) — usually indicates redundant or inconsistent data.

## 1.3 Steps for Solving Material Balance Problems

The universally adopted systematic procedure (used in KTU board problems) consists of **eight sequential steps**. They are listed formally in §1.3.2, but first an intuition.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" Picture

Imagine you walk into a bank with three friends. Friend A deposits **₹5000**, Friend B deposits **₹3000**, and Friend C withdraws an unknown amount. The bank clerk needs to know the final balance.

- **The "what flows in / what flows out / what accumulates"** idea is identical to a material balance.
- If you know the deposits and the final balance, you can find the withdrawal — that is exactly what a DOF = 0 calculation does.
- If the clerk only knows two of the three quantities, he *cannot* solve it — DOF = 1 (underspecified).
- If the clerk tells you all four numbers, the problem is overspecified — DOF = −1.

A material balance is, in essence, an **accounting equation for mass** at the level of a unit operation or an entire plant.

### 1.3.2 Formal Eight-Step Procedure (KTU Standard)

1. **Draw a labelled flowchart** of the process, marking every stream and every unit.
2. **Define all known stream variables** (flow rates, compositions, temperatures, pressures).
