3. **Choose a basis of calculation** (usually 100 kg or 100 kg/h for steady-state flow, or a batch amount for unsteady state).
4. **List all unknown variables** explicitly.
5. **Perform a Degrees-of-Freedom (DOF) analysis** to confirm the problem is solvable.
6. **Write the independent material balance equations** (one per component, or the overall balance + (N_C − 1) component balances).
7. **Solve the equations simultaneously** (substitution, matrix, or software).
8. **Check the solution** by verifying the overall balance and ensuring all compositions sum to **1.0 (or 100 %)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard KTU valuation keys require **all eight steps** to be visible in the answer script. A missing DOF analysis almost always costs **2 marks**.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants / Metrics

- **Molar mass** units: **g/mol** or **kg/kmol**.
- **Mass flow rate** units: **kg/h** or **kg/s** (the **kg/h** unit is preferred in KTU problems).
- **Composition** units: **mass fraction (wt/wt)**, **mole fraction (mol/mol)**, or **vol %** (must be stated explicitly).
- **Universal gas constant**: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.08314 bar·L/(mol·K)**.
- **Standard temperature and pressure (STP)**: **273.15 K, 1 atm (1.01325 bar)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need "Independent" Balances?

Imagine a ternary mixture (A, B, C) entering a reactor. The total mass balance is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}}
$$

If we also write component balances for A, B, and C:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} \, x_{A,\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \, x_{A,\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}} \, x_{A,\text{acc}}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} \, x_{B,\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \, x_{B,\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}} \, x_{B,\text{acc}}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} \, x_{C,\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \, x_{C,\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}} \, x_{C,\text{acc}}
$$

Adding the three component balances:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} \underbrace{(x_A + x_B + x_C)}_{= \, 1} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \underbrace{(x_A + x_B + x_C)}_{= \, 1} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}} \underbrace{(x_A + x_B + x_C)}_{= \, 1}
$$

which reproduces the overall balance. **Therefore, one balance is always redundant.**

## 2.2 DOF Analysis — The "Card Game" Intuition

Think of a problem as a **card game**:
- **Each unknown** is a face-down card you must flip.
- **Each independent equation** is a single "rule" that flips one card.
- **DOF** is the number of face-down cards *left over* after applying all rules.

If the rules are enough to flip every card, **DOF = 0** and you can solve the problem.

## 2.3 Reactive Systems — Effect on Independence

For a system with **N_C components** undergoing **R independent chemical reactions**, the species are linearly dependent because each reaction stoichiometry links them. Hence:

$$
N_{\text{indep}} = N_C - R
$$

For example, in the reaction **N₂ + 3 H₂ ⇌ 2 NH₃**, the three species are linked by **1 reaction**, giving only **3 − 1 = 2** independent balances.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Independent material balances and DOF analysis are foundational to:

- **Bioprocess design**: fermenter mass balances for substrate, biomass, and product.
- **Downstream processing**: centrifugation, filtration, and chromatography separations.
- **Plant-wide simulation**: sequential-modular or equation-oriented process simulators (ASPEN Plus, SuperPro Designer) use exactly this logic.
- **Process control**: identifies the minimum number of sensors (measurements) required for full state estimation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Statement | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Max. independent balances (non-reactive) | $N_{\text{indep}} = N_C$ | dimensionless |
| Max. independent balances (reactive) | $N_{\text{indep}} = N_C - R$ | dimensionless |
| Degrees of Freedom | $\text{DOF} = N_{\text{unknowns}} - N_{\text{indep eqns}}$ | dimensionless |
| Solvability condition | $\text{DOF} = 0$ | exact specification |
| Overall mass balance (steady) | $\displaystyle \sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{kg h}^{-1}$ |
| Component balance (steady) | $\displaystyle \sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} x_{i,\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} x_{i,\text{out}}$ | $\text{kg h}^{-1}$ |
| Unsteady / Batch balance | $\displaystyle \frac{dm_i}{dt} = \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + r_i V$ | $\text{kg s}^{-1}$ |
| Composition sum constraint | $\displaystyle \sum_i x_i = 1$ | dimensionless |
| Molar vs mass conversion | $n_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}$ | $\text{kmol}$ |
| Reactive atom balance | $N_{\text{indep}} = N_C - R$ | applies to atoms for checking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, the formula $N_{\text{indep}} = N_C$ (or $N_C - R$ for reactive) is **mandatory** to be written before counting independent equations.

## 2.6 The Eight Steps — Detailed Sub-logic

1. **Flowchart** — reduces the problem to a "node-and-stream" picture (process schematic).
2. **Define knowns** — explicit list avoids confusion between *given* and *assumed* values.
3. **Basis** — fixes one variable (often the feed flow) so all others can be expressed relative to it.
4. **Unknowns list** — establishes the *N_unknowns* used in DOF.
5. **DOF analysis** — verifies the problem is solvable.
6. **Write independent equations** — one per independent component, plus any auxiliary specs.
7. **Solve** — algebraic substitution, matrix method, or numerical solver.
8. **Verify** — overall balance check, composition sums to 1, and physical plausibility.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DOF Identity

The total number of independent pieces of information needed to specify a system equals the number of unknowns. Each independent equation supplies one piece. Hence:

$$
\text{Information needed} = N_{\text{unknowns}}
$$
$$
\text{Information available} = N_{\text{indep equations}}
$$

The *deficit* (or *surplus*) is the Degrees of Freedom:

$$
\text{DOF} = N_{\text{unknowns}} - N_{\text{indep equations}}
$$

If $\text{DOF} = 0$, the information available equals the information needed → unique solution exists.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Binary Mixing (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem (adapted from KTU-style, Module 2 mixing):**

Two ethanol–water streams are mixed in a stirred tank at steady state.

- **Stream 1 (S1):** $\dot{m}_1 = 200 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$, $x_{E,1} = 0.30$ (mass fraction ethanol)
- **Stream 2 (S2):** $\dot{m}_2 = 100 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$, $x_{E,2} = 0.70$
- **Stream 3 (S3):** flow rate and composition unknown.

**Find** the mass flow rate and composition of the exit stream **S3** using the 8-step procedure.

### Step 1 — Flowchart

```
        S1 (200 kg/h, 30% EtOH)
            │
            ▼
        ┌───────┐
        │ MIXER │ ─────►  S3 (?)
        └───────┘
            ▲
            │
        S2 (100 kg/h, 70% EtOH)
```

### Step 2 — Define Knowns

$$
\dot{m}_1 = 200, \quad x_{E,1} = 0.30, \quad x_{W,1} = 0.70
$$
$$
\dot{m}_2 = 100, \quad x_{E,2} = 0.70, \quad x_{W,2} = 0.30
$$

### Step 3 — Choose a Basis

**Basis:** 1 hour of operation (steady state, so all flows in **kg/h**). No further scaling required.

### Step 4 — List Unknowns

$$
\dot{m}_3 \;? \qquad x_{E,3} \;? \qquad x_{W,3} \;?
$$

**Total unknowns: 3** (note: $x_{W,3} = 1 - x_{E,3}$, so only 2 are independent).

### Step 5 — DOF Analysis

- **Unknowns (independent)**: 2 (since $x_{W,3}$ is fixed by $x_{E,3}$)
- **Independent equations**: 1 component balance (EtOH) + composition sum = 1 → effectively 2 independent equations.

$$
\text{DOF} = 2 - 2 = 0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

System is exactly specified.

### Step 6 — Write Independent Balances

**Overall Mass Balance** (steady state):

$$
\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3
$$

**Ethanol Component Balance**:

$$
\dot{m}_1 \, x_{E,1} + \dot{m}_2 \, x_{E,2} = \dot{m}_3 \, x_{E,3}
$$

### Step 7 — Solve

**Step 7.1 — Apply overall balance:**

$$
\dot{m}_3 = 200 + 100 = 300 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7.2 — Substitute into ethanol balance:**

$$
200 \times 0.30 + 100 \times 0.70 = 300 \times x_{E,3}
$$

**Step 7.3 — Evaluate LHS:**

$$
60 + 70 = 130 = 300 \cdot x_{E,3}
$$

**Step 7.4 — Solve for $x_{E,3}$:**

$$
x_{E,3} = \frac{130}{300} = 0.4333
$$

**Step 7.5 — By the composition sum constraint:**

$$
x_{W,3} = 1 - 0.4333 = 0.5667
$$

### Step 8 — Verify

- Ethanol in: $60 + 70 = 130 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$
- Ethanol out: $300 \times 0.4333 = 130 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$ ✓
- Water in: $200(0.70) + 100(0.30) = 140 + 30 = 170 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$
- Water out: $300 \times 0.5667 = 170 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$ ✓
- Total: $130 + 170 = 300 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}$ ✓

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_3 = 300 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}, \quad x_{E,3} = 0.4333, \quad x_{W,3} = 0.5667}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DOF Patterns in Common Unit Operations

| Unit Operation | Typical Unknowns | Independent Balances | DOF | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mixer (2 in, 1 out, binary) | $\dot{m}_3,\, x_{E,3}$ | 1 component + 1 sum = **2** | 0 | Solvable directly |
| Distillation (1 in, 2 out, binary) | $\dot{m}_D,\, \dot{m}_B,\, x_D$ | 2 component + 1 sum = **3** | 0 | Standard shortcut |
| Reactor with extent of reaction | $\dot{m}_{\text{out}},\, \xi$ | $N_C - R$ + 1 overall | varies | $\xi$ = extent of reaction |
| Splitter (1 in, 2 out) | $\dot{m}_1,\, \dot{m}_2$ (if compositions given) | 1 overall + 1 component | 0 | Composition same in/out |
| Separator with 3 components and 3 streams | 6 compositions + 3 flows = 9 | 2 component + 1 sum per stream = 6 | varies | Need additional specs |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Centrifuge (Reactive-style Multi-Component)

**Problem:** A feed of 1000 kg/h containing 25 % cells, 5 % product, 70 % water is sent to a centrifuge. The **concentrate (heavy phase)** has 80 % cells, 4 % product, 16 % water. The **light phase** has 1 % cells, 5.5 % product, 93.5 % water. Find the flow rates of concentrate and light phase.

**Step 1 — Flowchart:**

```
        Feed (1000 kg/h, 25/5/70)
              │
              ▼
        ┌────────────┐
        │ CENTRIFUGE │
        └────────────┘
            │       │
   Concentrate     Light phase
  (x_C, x_P, x_W)  (x_C, x_P, x_W)
```

**Step 2 — Knowns:** compositions of all three streams; feed flow.

**Step 3 — Basis:** 1 hour of operation (steady state).

**Step 4 — Unknowns:** $\dot{m}_{\text{conc}},\, \dot{m}_{\text{light}} = 2$.

**Step 5 — DOF:** $2 - 2 = 0$ (overall balance + 1 component balance — say, cells).

**Step 6 — Balances:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{conc}} + \dot{m}_{\text{light}}
$$
$$
1000(0.25) = 0.80 \, \dot{m}_{\text{conc}} + 0.01 \, \dot{m}_{\text{light}}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve:** Let $\dot{m}_{\text{light}} = 1000 - \dot{m}_{\text{conc}}$. Substitute:

$$
250 = 0.80 \, \dot{m}_{\text{conc}} + 0.01(1000 - \dot{m}_{\text{conc}})
$$
$$
250 = 0.80 \, \dot{m}_{\text{conc}} + 10 - 0.01 \, \dot{m}_{\text{conc}}
$$
$$
240 = 0.79 \, \dot{m}_{\text{conc}}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{conc}} = 303.8 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{light}} = 1000 - 303.8 = 696.2 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 8 — Verify** with product balance:

$$
\text{Product in} = 1000(0.05) = 50
$$
$$
\text{Product out} = 303.8(0.04) + 696.2(0.055) = 12.15 + 38.29 = 50.44
$$

Slight rounding error (≈ 1 %) — acceptable; in KTU, an exact **50** is expected if the data is consistent. The small discrepancy arises because the data is rounded. The student should report the answer as **303.8 / 696.2 kg/h** with a note that the data is internally consistent to within rounding.

## 3.5 Reconciling Component Balances Algebraically

A general matrix form for a system of $N$ independent component balances in $N$ unknowns is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \, \mathbf{x} &= \mathbf{b} \\
\mathbf{x} &= A^{-1} \, \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the binary mixer example, the matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0.30 & -0.4333 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{m}_3 \\ \dot{m}_1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \dot{m}_2 \\ \dot{m}_2 x_{E,2} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matrix formulation is the bridge between hand calculation and computer-aided flowsheeting (the same method used in **SuperPro Designer** for bioprocess plants).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 8-Step Solution Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Draw Flowchart"] --> B["Step 2: Label Knowns"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Choose Basis"]
    C --> D["Step 4: List Unknowns"]
    D --> E["Step 5: DOF Analysis"]
    E --> F{"DOF = 0 ?"}
    F -- "No, DOF greater 0" --> G["Add Specifications / Data"]
    G --> B
    F -- "No, DOF less 0" --> H["Check Redundancy / Consistency"]
    H --> B
    F -- "Yes, DOF = 0" --> I["Step 6: Write Independent Balances"]
    I --> J["Step 7: Solve Algebraically"]
    J --> K["Step 8: Verify Solution"]
    K --> L["Final Answer"]
```

## 4.2 Degree-of-Freedom (DOF) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Identify all Unknowns N_u"] --> P2["Count Independent Equations N_e"]
    P2 --> P3["Compute DOF = N_u minus N_e"]
    P3 --> Q1{"DOF value"}
    Q1 -- "DOF = 0" --> R1["Exactly Specified: Proceed to Solve"]
    Q1 -- "DOF greater 0" --> R2["Underspecified: Need DOF more Specifications"]
    Q1 -- "DOF less 0" --> R3["Overspecified: Check for Redundancy or Error"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bioprocess Material Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedBank["Feed Preparation Section"]
        F1["Raw Substrate Stream"] --> F2["Mixing Tank"]
        F3["Nutrient Stream"] --> F2
        F4["Water Stream"] --> F2
    end
    F2 --> R1["Fermenter / Bioreactor"]
    R1 --> R2["Cell Separator / Centrifuge"]
    R2 --> R3["Purification Column"]
    R3 --> P1["Final Product Stream"]
    R2 --> W1["Waste / Effluent Stream"]
    R1 --> G1["Off-gas Stream"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a *unit operation*; each arrow is a *stream*. The DOF analysis is performed **per block** and then at the **plant level**. If any block has a non-zero DOF, the plant cannot be simulated, regardless of how well the rest of the plant is specified.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "Degrees of Freedom" (DOF) as applied to material balance problems. What does a DOF value of zero, positive, or negative indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a material balance problem is defined as the difference between the number of unknown process variables and the number of independent equations (material balances + auxiliary specifications) that relate them:
> $$\text{DOF} = N_{\text{unknowns}} - N_{\text{independent equations}}$$
>
> **Interpretation (2 marks):**
> - $\text{DOF} = 0$ → system is *exactly specified*; a unique solution exists.
> - $\text{DOF} > 0$ → system is *underspecified*; additional information (compositions, splits, conversion) is required.
> - $\text{DOF} < 0$ → system is *overspecified*; data is redundant or internally inconsistent.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is meant by an "independent material balance equation"? For a process involving four components with no reaction, how many independent material balances can be written?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An independent material balance equation is one that provides a new piece of information which cannot be obtained by algebraic combination of the other balance equations. The overall (total) mass balance is the sum of the individual component balances and is therefore *not* an additional independent equation.
>
> **Application (1.5 marks):** For a non-reactive system with $N_C = 4$ components, the maximum number of independent material balances is:
> $$N_{\text{indep}} = N_C = 4$$
> One can write any **four** of the following five: the overall balance + four component balances — but only four are independent. Equivalently, one can write the overall balance and **three** component balances.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *(Choose either A or B)*

**Q-A(a)** With a neat flowchart, list and explain the eight steps to be followed for solving a material balance problem in bioprocess engineering. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Flowchart | Draw a labelled block diagram of the process. Identify all streams and unit operations. | 1 |
| 2. Label data | Mark all known flow rates, compositions, temperatures, pressures on the streams. | 0.5 |
| 3. Choose basis | Select 100 kg, 100 kg/h, 1 hour, or any convenient basis. | 1 |
| 4. List unknowns | Tabulate all unknown variables to be solved. | 0.5 |
| 5. DOF analysis | Compute $\text{DOF} = N_u - N_e$. Verify $\text{DOF} = 0$. | 1 |
| 6. Write balances | Frame the independent material balance equations (per component or overall + components). | 1 |
| 7. Solve | Apply algebraic substitution, matrix method, or numerical solver. | 1 |
| 8. Verify | Check overall balance, composition sums to 1, and physical plausibility. | 1 |

---

**Q-A(b)** A feed of 1000 kg/h containing 50 wt% ethanol and 50 wt% water is fed to a distillation column. The distillate contains 90 wt% ethanol and the bottoms contain 5 wt% ethanol. Calculate the flow rates of distillate and bottoms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Flowchart:**

```
        Feed (1000 kg/h, 50% EtOH)
              │
              ▼
        ┌──────────────┐
        │ DISTILLATION │
        └──────────────┘
           │          │
       Distillate   Bottoms
       (x_D=0.90)  (x_B=0.05)
```

**Step 2 — Knowns:** $F = 1000$, $x_F = 0.50$, $x_D = 0.90$, $x_B = 0.05$. Unknowns: $D,\, B$.

**Step 3 — Basis:** 1 hour of steady-state operation (kg/h).

**Step 4 — DOF:** $2 \text{ unknowns} - 2 \text{ equations} = 0$. ✓

**Step 5 — Balances:**

Overall: $F = D + B$  →  $1000 = D + B$  ...(i)

Ethanol: $F x_F = D x_D + B x_B$  →  $1000(0.50) = 0.90 D + 0.05 B$  ...(ii)

**Step 6 — Solve:**

From (i): $B = 1000 - D$. Substitute in (ii):

$$
500 = 0.90 D + 0.05(1000 - D)
$$
$$
500 = 0.90 D + 50 - 0.05 D
$$
$$
450 = 0.85 D
$$
$$
D = 529.4 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}
$$
$$
B = 1000 - 529.4 = 470.6 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify (water balance):**

Water in: $1000(0.50) = 500$ kg/h.
Water out: $529.4(0.10) + 470.6(0.95) = 52.94 + 447.07 = 500.01$ kg/h ✓

**Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{D = 529.4 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}, \quad B = 470.6 \; \text{kg h}^{-1}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**Q-B(a)** Explain the concept of independent material balance equations for non-reactive and reactive systems. Show that for a reactive system with $N_C$ components and $R$ independent reactions, the number of independent balances reduces to $N_C - R$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Non-reactive systems (3 marks):** For a non-reactive system with $N_C$ components, the total mass balance is the sum of the $N_C$ component balances. Therefore, only $N_C$ of the $(N_C + 1)$ possible balances are independent. For example, with three components A, B, C:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} x_{A,\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} x_{A,\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}} x_{A,\text{acc}}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} x_{B,\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} x_{B,\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{acc}} x_{B,\text{acc}}
$$

Adding all three component balances reproduces the total balance → **3 independent, not 4**.

**Reactive systems (4 marks):** When $R$ independent chemical reactions occur, the mole (or mass) balances are constrained by the reaction stoichiometry. Each reaction introduces one linear dependency among the species, reducing the number of independent balances:

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{indep}} = N_C - R}
$$

**Proof sketch:** Consider $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$. There are $N_C = 3$ species, $R = 1$ reaction, hence $3 - 1 = 2$ independent balances. The stoichiometric relation $-r_{N_2} = -\tfrac{1}{3} r_{H_2} = \tfrac{1}{2} r_{NH_3}$ provides the extra dependency. The element balances (N, H) are the two truly independent constraints.

**General case:** $N_{\text{indep}} = N_{\text{atoms}}$ also equals $N_C - R$ (conservation of elements).

---

**Q-B(b)** A wet solid containing 30 % water and 70 % solids is dried to a moisture content of 5 % water and 95 % solids. If 1000 kg of wet feed is supplied, calculate (i) the mass of water removed, and (ii) the mass of dry product obtained. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Flowchart:**

```
   Wet Feed (1000 kg, 30% water)
              │
              ▼
        ┌──────────┐
        │  DRYER   │
        └──────────┘
           │       │
       Dry Product  Water Vapour
       (95% solids)  (pure H2O)
```

**Step 2 — Knowns:** $F = 1000$ kg, $x_{W,F} = 0.30$, $x_{S,F} = 0.70$, $x_{S,P} = 0.95$, $x_{W,P} = 0.05$.

**Step 3 — Basis:** 1000 kg of wet feed (batch-like, but balance still applies).

**Step 4 — Unknowns:** $P$ (dry product), $V$ (water vapour). Since the vapour is pure water, only $P$ is truly unknown. DOF = 1.

**Step 5 — Balance on Solids** (solids do not evaporate):

$$
F \, x_{S,F} = P \, x_{S,P}
$$
$$
1000 \times 0.70 = P \times 0.95
$$
$$
P = \frac{700}{0.95} = 736.84 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Water removed:**

$$
V = F - P = 1000 - 736.84 = 263.16 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify (water balance):**

Water in: $1000 \times 0.30 = 300$ kg.
Water out (in product): $736.84 \times 0.05 = 36.84$ kg.
Water removed: $300 - 36.84 = 263.16$ kg ✓

**Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{P = 736.84 \; \text{kg}, \quad V = 263.16 \; \text{kg}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Skipping the DOF analysis** — KTU examiners typically allocate **1 to 2 marks** for explicitly computing DOF and stating that it equals zero. Skipping it is the #1 reason students lose marks despite correct arithmetic.
> 2. **Writing N_C + 1 independent balances** — Many students write the overall balance *plus* all $N_C$ component balances, treating them as $N_C + 1$ independent equations. This is the single most common conceptual error. Always state explicitly: "Only $N_C$ of these are independent."
> 3. **Forgetting the basis** — without an explicit basis, the answer is dimensionally ambiguous.
> 4. **Not verifying the answer** — a quick overall-balance check (Step 8) often reveals arithmetic errors and earns a "step" mark.
> 5. **Inconsistent units** — mixing kg and kg/h, or wt% and mol% in the same equation.
> 6. **Composition sum not equal to 1** — if a calculated composition gives $x_A + x_B + x_C = 0.97$, the error is in the data or arithmetic; flag it explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Independent Material Balances**: at most $N_C$ (non-reactive) or $N_C - R$ (reactive).
- **Overall balance is redundant** when all $N_C$ component balances are written.
- **DOF = Unknowns − Independent Equations**; DOF = 0 means *exactly specified* and *solvable*.
- **DOF > 0 → underspecified**; **DOF < 0 → overspecified**.
- **Eight steps** are mandatory in KTU answers: flowchart, label, basis, unknowns, DOF, balances, solve, verify.
- **Basis of calculation** is essential: choose **100 kg** (batch) or **100 kg/h** (continuous) unless otherwise stated.
- **Composition sum constraint** $\sum x_i = 1$ is an *auxiliary equation*, not a balance.
- **Atoms are conserved** even when species are not — useful for checking reactive balances.
- **Units must be consistent**: kg ↔ kg/h, wt% ↔ wt%, etc.
- **Verification step** (overall balance) earns an extra mark and catches arithmetic slips.
- **Standard unit for KTU flowsheet problems**: **kg/h**.
- **Mole balance vs mass balance**: pick the basis that simplifies the algebra (mole for reactions, mass for separations).
- **KTU-friendly trick**: for a binary system, you only need **2 independent equations** (overall + 1 component). The second component follows from $\sum x_i = 1$.
- **Reactive shortcut**: use **element balances** (C, H, O, N) — they are *always* independent and bypass the $N_C - R$ derivation.
- **Common exam tags to look for**: "mixer", "splitter", "distillation", "drying", "extraction", "reactor" — each implies a known DOF pattern (see §3.3).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Energy Balances: Law of Conservation of Energy for Non-Reactive Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Energy Balances: Law of Conservation of Energy for Non-Reactive Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Law of Conservation of Energy**, also known as the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, is a foundational postulate in engineering thermodynamics and bioprocess calculations. For any process occurring in a defined system, energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another or transferred across the system boundary as **heat (Q)** or **work (W)**.

Mathematically, the general energy balance over a system is expressed as:

$$
\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum_{in} \dot{m}_{in}\,e_{in} - \sum_{out} \dot{m}_{out}\,e_{out}
$$

where $E_{system}$ is the total energy accumulated within the system boundary, $\dot{Q}$ and $\dot{W}$ are the rates of heat transfer into the system and work done by the system, respectively, and $e$ denotes the **specific total energy** carried by each stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Non-Reactive Systems):**
> For a **non-reactive** process, there is no chemical reaction taking place, which means the **generation and consumption terms vanish**. The general energy balance reduces to a pure accounting of physical energy transfers and conversions (sensible, latent, kinetic, potential, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water reservoir (your system)** connected to an inlet pipe and an outlet pipe, with a small turbine attached to the outlet.

- **Water in the inlet pipe** = energy entering the system ($\dot{m}_{in} e_{in}$).
- **Water leaving through the outlet** = energy leaving the system ($\dot{m}_{out} e_{out}$).
- **Sunlight warming the reservoir** = heat transfer ($\dot{Q}$).
- **Turbine extracting mechanical energy** = work output ($\dot{W}$).
- **Change in water level over time** = accumulation of energy ($\frac{dE_{system}}{dt}$).

> **Energy, like water, is neither created nor destroyed — it is merely redistributed, transformed, and transferred.**

Just as a bank account tracks money in, money out, and balance change, an energy balance tracks energy in, energy out, and the change in stored energy.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Constants Used in KTU Energy Balances:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - **Standard Acceleration Due to Gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²
> - **Standard Reference Conditions:** $T_{ref} = 25^\circ C = 298.15$ K, $P_{ref} = 1$ atm = 101.325 kPa
> - **Conversion:** 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3600 kJ
> - **Heat Capacity of Liquid Water:** $C_{p,w} = 4.184$ kJ/(kg·K)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Fundamental Forms of Energy in Process Systems

In a non-reactive bioprocess system, energy can manifest in the following forms:

1. **Internal Energy ($U$):** The microscopic energy of molecular motion, vibration, rotation, and electronic interactions. Cannot be measured in absolute terms, only its *change* $\Delta U$ is meaningful.
2. **Kinetic Energy ($KE$):** Energy due to bulk motion of the system, $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.
3. **Potential Energy ($PE$):** Energy due to position in a gravitational field, $PE = mgh$.
4. **Enthalpy ($H$):** A composite thermodynamic property defined as $H = U + PV$, which conveniently accounts for flow work in open systems.
5. **Heat ($Q$) and Work ($W$):** Energy in transit across the system boundary, never stored inside the system.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Energy Balance Procedure

The "Why" and "How" behind each step:

- **Step 1 — Draw and Label the Flow Diagram:** A visual schematic ensures no stream is missed and clearly defines the system boundary. *Why?* Because energy is path-dependent; a clear boundary is essential.
- **Step 2 — Perform Material Balances First:** Energy balances depend on knowing the *mass flow rates* of every stream. *Why?* You cannot compute enthalpy flow without knowing how much material carries it.
- **Step 3 — Choose a Reference State:** Select arbitrary $T_{ref}$ and $P_{ref}$ values from which all energy changes are measured. *Why?* Only $\Delta H$ and $\Delta U$ are physically meaningful; the reference cancels out in any difference.
- **Step 4 — Tabulate Enthalpies of Each Stream:** Use $C_p$ data, latent heat values, or steam tables. *Why?* Sensible heat and phase-change contributions must be quantified.
- **Step 5 — Apply the Energy Balance Equation:** Substitute all known values and solve for the unknown (typically $\dot{Q}$ or $\dot{W}$). *Why?* This is the only step that mathematically *enforces* energy conservation.
- **Step 6 — Verify with Units and Physical Reasonableness:** A check on units and on the sign of $\dot{Q}$ (heating vs. cooling) catches errors early.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formula Reference for KTU Examinations (Non-Reactive Systems):**

| **#** | **Equation / Term** | **Mathematical Form** | **Applicable System / Condition** | **Standard Units (SI)** |
|-----|---------------------|-----------------------|------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Total Energy | $E = U + KE + PE$ | Universal | J |
| 2 | Specific Internal Energy | $u$ | Universal | J/kg |
| 3 | Specific Enthalpy | $h = u + Pv$ | Open / Flow systems | J/kg |
| 4 | Specific Kinetic Energy | $\tfrac{1}{2}v^2$ | Bulk fluid motion | m²/s² |
| 5 | Specific Potential Energy | $gz$ | Gravitational field | m²/s² |
| 6 | Closed System Balance | $\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W$ | No mass transfer across boundary | J |
| 7 | Open System (Steady-State) Balance | $\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$ | No accumulation, flow process | J/kg (per unit mass) |
| 8 | Sensible Heat | $Q = m \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p\, dT \approx m\,C_p\,(T_2 - T_1)$ | No phase change | J or kJ |
| 9 | Latent Heat (Phase Change) | $Q = m\,\Delta H_{vap}$ | Phase transition at constant T | J or kJ |
| 10 | Total Stream Energy Flow | $\dot{m}\left(h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz\right)$ | Enthalpy + KE + PE per stream | W |
| 11 | Heat Capacity Relation (Ideal Gas) | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Ideal gas | J/(mol·K) |
| 12 | Constant-$C_p$ Approximation | $\bar{C_p}$ = mean value over temperature range | Simplification | J/(kg·K) |
| 13 | Non-Reactive Generation Term | $E_{gen} = E_{cons} = 0$ | No chemical reaction | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Equation Form:**
> The sign convention used here follows the **KTU 2024 standard**: $\dot{Q}$ is positive when heat flows *into* the system, and $\dot{W}$ is positive when work is done *by* the system on the surroundings.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The energy balance for non-reactive systems is the **backbone** of countless engineering applications:

- **Bioprocess Sterilization:** Calculating the steam requirement to heat fermentation media from 25 °C to 121 °C.
- **Heat Exchanger Design:** Determining the heat duty ($\dot{Q}$) for heating broth in a continuous bioreactor.
- **Refrigeration Cycles:** Evaluating compressor work and condenser/evaporator heat loads in cold-chain bioproduct storage.
- **Distillation Columns:** Performing reboiler and condenser energy audits.
- **Effluent Treatment:** Estimating cooling water demand for thermal pollution control in upstream/downstream processing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Energy Balance (Closed System)

We begin with the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed system (no mass crosses the boundary):

**Statement:** The change in total energy of a closed system equals the net heat added to the system minus the net work done by the system.

**Step 1 — Write the First Law in differential form for a closed system:**

$$
dU = \delta Q - \delta W
$$

*Explanation:* The infinitesimal change in internal energy $dU$ equals the infinitesimal heat transfer $\delta Q$ entering the system minus the infinitesimal work $\delta W$ leaving the system.

**Step 2 — Recognize that $U$ is only one form of energy. Generalize by including kinetic and potential energy:**

$$
d(U + KE + PE) = \delta Q - \delta W
$$

*Explanation:* Energy inside a moving or elevated system is not purely internal; it can also be kinetic (bulk motion) and potential (position in a gravitational field). All three forms must be tracked.

**Step 3 — Write the explicit forms of $KE$ and $PE$:**

$$
KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2, \quad PE = mgh
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute these into the energy balance:**

$$
d(U + \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgh) = \delta Q - \delta W
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the process from state 1 (initial) to state 2 (final):**

$$
\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W
$$

*Explanation:* The integral of a differential is the difference between the final and initial states. The accumulated heat $Q$ and work $W$ are finite quantities for the whole process.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **General Closed-System Energy Balance** equation. It is the governing relationship for batch reactors, mixing tanks with no inlet/outlet flow, and pressurization of vessels.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the General Energy Balance (Open System / Control Volume)

For an **open system**, mass can enter and leave, and each mass stream carries enthalpy ($H$), kinetic energy, and potential energy. Additionally, the boundary may be a **control volume** with accumulation.

**Step 1 — Start from the closed-system result and add mass-flow terms. The general conservation statement is:**

$$
\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum_{in} \dot{m}_{in}\,e_{in} - \sum_{out} \dot{m}_{out}\,e_{out}
$$

*Explanation:* The left side is the rate of accumulation. The right side has net heat in, net work out, plus the net rate of energy carried in by mass streams minus that carried out.

**Step 2 — Express the specific total energy $e$ carried by each stream:**

$$
e = h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz
$$

*Explanation:* For a flowing stream, the relevant total energy per unit mass is the **enthalpy** (which already includes flow work $Pv$), plus the kinetic and potential energy per unit mass.

**Step 3 — Write the work term, separating it into shaft work $\dot{W}_s$ and flow work $P\dot{V}$:**

$$
\dot{W} = \dot{W}_s + \sum_{out} \dot{m}_{out}(Pv)_{out} - \sum_{in} \dot{m}_{in}(Pv)_{in}
$$

*Explanation:* Work done by the system has two parts: (a) useful **shaft work** (e.g., a turbine, compressor, agitator) and (b) **flow work** that pushes mass across the boundary against the prevailing pressure.

**Step 4 — Substitute this into the general balance, recognizing that the flow work terms cancel with the $PV$ part of enthalpy:**

After substitution and cancellation:

$$
\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz\right)_{in} - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz\right)_{out}
$$

*Explanation:* The flow work from the work term combines with internal energy in $h = u + Pv$ to give enthalpy, which is the property tabulated in steam tables and $C_p$ data.

**Step 5 — Apply the steady-state assumption (no accumulation):**

$$
\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = 0
$$

*Explanation:* At steady state, the system properties do not change with time — this is the most common operating condition in industrial bioprocess equipment.

**Step 6 — The simplified steady-state, open-system energy balance is:**

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz\right)_{out} - \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz\right)_{in}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **workhorse equation** for energy balances in continuous bioreactors, heat exchangers, distillation columns, and pipelines.

**Step 7 — Per-unit-mass form (dividing by total mass flow rate at steady state):**

$$
\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s
$$

*Explanation:* Per unit mass of process stream, the balance takes the same compact form as the closed-system equation, but with $H$ replacing $U$ and $W_s$ replacing $W$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Energy Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Problems (Non-Reactive Systems):**

| **Process Type** | **Mass Transfer?** | **Accumulation?** | **Applicable Equation** |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Closed, Batch | No | Yes | $\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W$ |
| Open, Steady State | Yes | No | $\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$ |
| Open, Transient | Yes | Yes | Full differential form with $dE_{sys}/dt$ |
| Throttling Valve | Yes | No | $\Delta H = 0$ (adiabatic, no work) |
| Heat Exchanger (no phase change) | Yes | No | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\,C_p\,(T_{out} - T_{in})$ |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Heating Water in a Tank (Closed System)

**Problem:** A rigid, well-insulated tank contains **200 kg** of liquid water initially at **25 °C**. An electrical immersion heater rated at **15 kW** is switched on for **45 minutes**. Assume no heat loss to the surroundings and that the tank is stationary. Determine the final temperature of the water.

**Given:**
- $m = 200$ kg
- $T_1 = 25^\circ C$
- $\dot{W}_{elec,in} = -15$ kW (work *into* the system, so $\dot{W} = -15$ kW in our sign convention)
- $t = 45$ min = 2700 s
- $C_p = 4.184$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $Q = 0$ (insulated, adiabatic)
- $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$ (stationary, no elevation change)

**Step 1 — Apply the closed-system energy balance:**

$$
\Delta U + \cancel{\Delta KE}^{0} + \cancel{\Delta PE}^{0} = \cancel{Q}^{0} - W
$$

$$
\Delta U = -W = W_{in}
$$

*Explanation:* Since the tank is insulated, no heat escapes, and all electrical work done on the water becomes an increase in its internal energy.

**Step 2 — Express internal energy change in terms of sensible heat:**

$$
\Delta U = m\,C_p\,(T_2 - T_1)
$$

*Explanation:* For an incompressible liquid (water), $\Delta U \approx \Delta H \approx m\,C_p\,\Delta T$ because $V\Delta P$ is negligible.

**Step 3 — Compute the total electrical work input:**

$$
W_{in} = \dot{W}_{elec}\,t = 15\,\text{kW} \times 2700\,\text{s} = 40{,}500\,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $T_2$:**

$$
m\,C_p\,(T_2 - T_1) = W_{in}
$$

$$
200 \times 4.184 \times (T_2 - 25) = 40{,}500
$$

$$
836.8 \times (T_2 - 25) = 40{,}500
$$

$$
T_2 - 25 = \frac{40{,}500}{836.8} = 48.40
$$

$$
T_2 = 73.40\,^\circ C
$$

> **Final Answer:** The water temperature rises to approximately **73.4 °C**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Adiabatic Mixing of Two Streams (Open System)

**Problem:** A feedwater heater operates at steady state. Stream 1 is liquid water at **1.0 kg/s**, **40 °C**, and **1 atm**. Stream 2 is saturated steam at the same pressure, entering at **0.2 kg/s**. The mixed stream leaves at **1 atm**. The mixing is adiabatic ($\dot{Q} = 0$) and there is no shaft work ($\dot{W}_s = 0$). The mixing point is at the same elevation. Determine the temperature of the exit stream.

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_1 = 1.0$ kg/s, $T_1 = 40^\circ C$, $h_1 = C_p(T_1 - T_{ref}) = 4.184 \times 40 = 167.36$ kJ/kg (taking $T_{ref} = 0^\circ C$)
- $\dot{m}_2 = 0.2$ kg/s, saturated steam at 1 atm, $T_{sat} = 100^\circ C$, $h_2 = h_{fg} + h_f \approx 2257 + 419 = 2676$ kJ/kg
- $\dot{Q} = 0$, $\dot{W}_s = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$

**Step 1 — Apply the steady-state open-system energy balance:**

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{out}h_{out} - \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{in}h_{in}
$$

$$
0 = \dot{m}_3 h_3 - \dot{m}_1 h_1 - \dot{m}_2 h_2
$$

*Explanation:* No heat or work crosses the boundary; energy is conserved only through stream enthalpies.

**Step 2 — Perform material balance to find $\dot{m}_3$:**

$$
\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = 1.0 + 0.2 = 1.2\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total inlet enthalpy flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_1 h_1 = 1.0 \times 167.36 = 167.36\,\text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_2 h_2 = 0.2 \times 2676 = 535.2\,\text{kW}
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 702.56\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $h_3$:**

$$
h_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2}{\dot{m}_3} = \frac{702.56}{1.2} = 585.47\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine $T_3$:**

Since $h_3 = 585.47$ kJ/kg lies between $h_f = 419$ kJ/kg and $h_g = 2676$ kJ/kg at 1 atm, the exit is a **saturated mixture**. Quality $x$ is found from:

$$
h_3 = h_f + x(h_{fg})
$$

$$
585.47 = 419 + x(2257)
$$

$$
x = \frac{585.47 - 419}{2257} = 0.0737
$$

**Step 6 — Since it is a saturated mixture at 1 atm, the temperature is the saturation temperature:**

$$
T_3 = 100\,^\circ C
$$

> **Final Answer:** The exit stream is at **100 °C** with a quality (vapor fraction) of approximately **7.4%**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of the General Energy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Inlet Stream Energy: m_in x h_in"]
    nodeB["Inlet Stream KE: m_in x 0.5 v_in^2"]
    nodeC["Inlet Stream PE: m_in x g z_in"]
    nodeD["Control Volume / Process System"]
    nodeE["Heat In Q_in"]
    nodeF["Shaft Work In W_s,in"]
    nodeG["Heat Out Q_out"]
    nodeH["Shaft Work Out W_s,out"]
    nodeI["Outlet Stream Energy: m_out x h_out"]
    nodeJ["Outlet Stream KE: m_out x 0.5 v_out^2"]
    nodeK["Outlet Stream PE: m_out x g z_out"]
    nodeL["Accumulation: dE_system / dt"]

    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Diagram:**
> The control volume (process) exchanges **heat** and **shaft work** with the surroundings, while **mass streams** carry **enthalpy, kinetic, and potential energy** in and out. The **accumulation term** $\frac{dE_{system}}{dt}$ captures any change in stored energy inside the system. For steady-state operation, this accumulation term is zero.

---

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Right Energy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start: Identify Process"]
    nodeQ1{"Mass transfer across boundary?"}
    nodeQ2{"Time-dependent accumulation?"}
    nodePathA["Closed System: dU = deltaQ - deltaW"]
    nodePathB["Open Transient: Full Differential Form"]
    nodeQ3{"Steady state?"}
    nodePathC["Open Steady State: deltaH + deltaKE + deltaPE = Q - W_s"]
    nodePathD["Closed Transient: Integrate over time"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodePathA
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodePathD
    nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodePathC
    nodeQ3 -- No --> nodePathB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** Examiners often award partial credit for correctly identifying whether the system is **closed or open** and whether it operates at **steady state or transient**. Always state these classifications explicitly in your solution.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics. List the various forms of energy that must be considered when writing a general energy balance for a non-reactive process system.**

**Model Answer:**

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another or transferred between a system and its surroundings as heat or work.

The various forms of energy considered in a general energy balance are:

1. **Internal Energy ($U$):** Microscopic kinetic and potential energy of molecules.
2. **Kinetic Energy ($KE$):** Energy due to bulk motion of the system, $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.
3. **Potential Energy ($PE$):** Energy due to position in a gravitational field, $PE = mgh$.
4. **Enthalpy ($H$):** Defined as $H = U + PV$, used for flow processes.
5. **Heat ($Q$) and Work ($W$):** Energy in transit across the system boundary.

> **[3 Marks: Definition = 1, Listing 4-5 forms = 2]**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between the energy balance equations for a closed system and an open system operating at steady state. State the conditions under which each is applied.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Closed System** | **Open System (Steady State)** |
|------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| Mass transfer | None | Across the boundary |
| Governing equation | $\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W$ | $\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$ |
| Property used | Internal energy ($U$) | Enthalpy ($H = U + PV$) |
| Accumulation | Yes (transient) | No (steady state) |
| Example | Heating water in a sealed rigid tank | Heat exchanger in a continuous bioprocess |

**Conditions for application:**
- **Closed system:** No mass crosses the boundary; used for batch reactors, pressurized vessels, and mixing in rigid containers.
- **Open system (steady state):** Continuous flow in and out, with constant process variables; used for pipelines, heat exchangers, and continuous bioreactors.

> **[3 Marks: Equation for closed = 1, Equation for open = 1, Conditions = 1]**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) A perfectly insulated rigid tank contains 5 kg of air initially at 300 K and 200 kPa. The tank is connected to a supply line carrying air at 500 kPa and 400 K. A valve is opened and air flows in until the tank pressure reaches 400 kPa, after which the valve is closed. The process is adiabatic, and the air behaves as an ideal gas with constant $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K). Determine:**
**(i) The mass of air that entered the tank.**
**(ii) The final temperature of the air in the tank.**

**(b) Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for solving energy balance problems in non-reactive systems. List the key assumptions made in deriving the general energy balance equation.**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Given:**
- Initial mass in tank: $m_1 = 5$ kg, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 200$ kPa
- Supply line: $P_{in} = 500$ kPa, $T_{in} = 400$ K
- Final pressure: $P_2 = 400$ kPa
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $Q = 0$ (adiabatic), $W = 0$ (rigid tank, no shaft work)
- $\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$ (negligible)

**Step 1 — Compute initial volume of the tank using ideal gas law:**

$$
V = \frac{m_1 R T_1}{P_1}
$$

For air, $R = C_p - C_v = 1.005 - 0.718 = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K)

$$
V = \frac{5 \times 0.287 \times 300}{200} = \frac{430.5}{200} = 2.1525\,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the final mass in the tank using ideal gas law at the new state:**

$$
m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T_2}
$$

*Note:* This requires $T_2$, so we apply the energy balance first.

**Step 3 — Apply the energy balance for an open, transient, adiabatic, rigid tank:**

For a charging process, the general energy balance reduces to:

$$
U_2 - U_1 = m_{in} h_{in}
$$

*Explanation:* No heat, no work, no KE/PE change. The internal energy gained equals the enthalpy brought in by the entering mass (because the entering gas must do flow work $P_{in}V_{in}$ to push the existing gas).

**Step 4 — Express the internal energies in terms of $C_v$ and the mass entering $\Delta m = m_2 - m_1$:**

$$
m_2 C_v T_2 - m_1 C_v T_1 = (m_2 - m_1) C_p T_{in}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_2$ by eliminating $m_2$ using the ideal gas law:**

From the ideal gas law: $m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T_2}$

$$
\frac{P_2 V}{R T_2} C_v T_2 - m_1 C_v T_1 = \left(\frac{P_2 V}{R T_2} - m_1\right) C_p T_{in}
$$

$$
\frac{P_2 V}{R} C_v - m_1 C_v T_1 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T_2} C_p T_{in} - m_1 C_p T_{in}
$$

Substitute known values:

- $V = 2.1525$ m³
- $P_2 = 400$ kPa
- $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_{in} = 400$ K
- $m_1 = 5$ kg
- $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

$$
\frac{400 \times 2.1525}{0.287} \times 0.718 - 5 \times 0.718 \times 300 = \frac{400 \times 2.1525}{0.287 \times T_2} \times 1.005 \times 400 - 5 \times 1.005 \times 400
$$

Compute each term:

- Left: $\frac{861}{0.287} \times 0.718 - 1077 = 3000 \times 0.718 - 1077 = 2154 - 1077 = 1077$ kJ
- Right: $\frac{861 \times 402}{0.287 \times T_2} - 2010 = \frac{346{,}122}{0.287 \times T_2} - 2010$

$$
1077 = \frac{346{,}122}{0.287 \times T_2} - 2010
$$

$$
3087 = \frac{346{,}122}{0.287 \times T_2}
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{346{,}122}{0.287 \times 3087} = \frac{346{,}122}{886.0} = 390.66\,\text{K}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute final mass $m_2$:**

$$
m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T_2} = \frac{400 \times 2.1525}{0.287 \times 390.66} = \frac{861}{112.12} = 7.68\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Mass that entered the tank:**

$$
\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 7.68 - 5 = 2.68\,\text{kg}
$$

> **(i) Mass of air that entered: ≈ 2.68 kg**
> **(ii) Final temperature: ≈ 390.7 K (≈ 117.5 °C)**

> **[7 Marks: Material balance setup = 2, Energy balance derivation = 3, Final calculations = 2]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step-by-Step Procedure for Energy Balance Problems in Non-Reactive Systems:**

1. **Draw and label the flow diagram:** Identify all inlet and outlet streams, equipment, and energy transfer arrows ($\dot{Q}$, $\dot{W}$).
2. **Choose a reference state:** Select $T_{ref}$ and $P_{ref}$ from which all enthalpy values are measured. This choice is arbitrary since only differences matter.
3. **Perform complete material balances:** Determine all unknown mass flow rates, compositions, and phases before any energy calculation.
4. **Tabulate stream enthalpies:** For each stream, compute $h$ using $C_p$ data (sensible heat), $\Delta H_{vap}$ (phase change), or steam tables.
5. **Apply the energy balance equation:** Select the appropriate form (closed/open, steady-state/transient) and substitute known values.
6. **Solve for the unknown:** Typically, this is $\dot{Q}$ or $\dot{W}_s$, but may also be an unknown stream temperature or flow rate.
7. **Verify and check units:** Ensure all terms are dimensionally consistent and the sign of $\dot{Q}$ matches physical intuition (e.g., a heating coil should give $\dot{Q} > 0$).

**Key Assumptions in the General Energy Balance (Non-Reactive Systems):**

- **No chemical reaction:** Generation and consumption terms for energy are zero.
- **Negligible change in kinetic and potential energy** (unless explicitly stated).
- **No accumulation at steady state.**
- **Constant heat capacity** (or use of mean $C_p$ over the temperature range).
- **No significant heat loss to surroundings** (unless otherwise noted).
- **Ideal gas behavior** (when applicable) for easy $PV$ work calculation.

> **[7 Marks: Procedure steps = 4, Assumptions listing = 3]**

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) A horizontal pipe carries oil (mean $C_p = 2.1$ kJ/(kg·K)) at a steady flow rate of 0.5 kg/s, entering at 80 °C. The pipe passes through a furnace section where it is heated by a constant heat flux. If the oil leaves the section at 150 °C, calculate:**
**(i) The rate of heat transfer to the oil (in kW).**
**(ii) The rate of entropy generation, assuming the oil enters and leaves at the same pressure.**

**(b) Steam at 200 kPa with quality 0.9 (Stream 1) is mixed adiabatically with liquid water at 200 kPa and 40 °C (Stream 2) in a steady-state process. The mixed stream (Stream 3) leaves at 200 kPa. If the mass flow rate of Stream 1 is 1.0 kg/s, determine:**
**(i) The mass flow rate of Stream 2 required to obtain a saturated liquid exit stream.**
**(ii) The rate of entropy generation in the mixing process.**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s, $C_p = 2.1$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $T_1 = 80^\circ C$, $T_2 = 150^\circ C$
- $\dot{W}_s = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$, steady state

**Step 1 — Apply steady-state open-system energy balance:**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) = \dot{m} C_p (T_2 - T_1)
$$

*Explanation:* For an incompressible liquid, $h \approx C_p T$ relative to a reference, so the enthalpy change is simply $C_p \Delta T$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot{Q}$:**

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.5 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 80) = 0.5 \times 2.1 \times 70 = 73.5\,\text{kW}
$$

> **(i) Rate of heat transfer: $\dot{Q} = 73.5$ kW**

**Step 3 — Compute entropy generation using the entropy balance:**

For a steady-state process:

$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_2 - s_1) - \frac{\dot{Q}}{T_{boundary}}
$$

*Explanation:* Entropy is generated internally due to the finite temperature difference in the furnace. The boundary temperature $T_{boundary}$ is the furnace temperature.

**Step 4 — Approximate $T_{boundary}$ as the higher of the oil temperatures (or use a stated furnace temperature).** Assuming a mean furnace temperature of 500 °C = 773 K:

$$
s_2 - s_1 = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 2.1 \times \ln\left(\frac{423}{353}\right) = 2.1 \times 0.1809 = 0.3800\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}(s_2 - s_1) = 0.5 \times 0.3800 = 0.1900\,\text{kW/K}
$$

$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = 0.1900 - \frac{73.5}{773} = 0.1900 - 0.0951 = 0.0949\,\text{kW/K}
$$

> **(ii) Rate of entropy generation: $\dot{S}_{gen} ≈ 0.095$ kW/K**

> **[7 Marks: Energy balance = 3, Entropy balance = 4]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- Stream 1: Saturated mixture at 200 kPa, $x_1 = 0.9$
- Stream 2: Compressed liquid at 200 kPa, $T_2 = 40^\circ C$
- Stream 3: Saturated liquid at 200 kPa
- $\dot{m}_1 = 1.0$ kg/s
- $\dot{Q} = 0$, $\dot{W}_s = 0$, adiabatic, no KE/PE change

**Step 1 — Determine steam-table properties at 200 kPa:**

- $T_{sat} = 120.23^\circ C$
- $h_f = 504.7$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2201.6$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 2706.3$ kJ/kg
- $s_f = 1.5301$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_{fg} = 5.5970$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 2 — Enthalpy of Stream 1:**

$$
h_1 = h_f + x_1 h_{fg} = 504.7 + 0.9 \times 2201.6 = 504.7 + 1981.44 = 2486.14\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpy of Stream 2 (compressed liquid ≈ saturated liquid at 40 °C):**

$$
h_2 \approx h_f(40^\circ C) = 167.57\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy of Stream 3 (saturated liquid at 200 kPa):**

$$
h_3 = h_f = 504.7\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply energy balance (adiabatic, steady-state, no work):**

$$
0 = \dot{m}_3 h_3 - \dot{m}_1 h_1 - \dot{m}_2 h_2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_3 h_3 = \dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2
$$

**Step 6 — Use mass balance: $\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2$:**

$$
(\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2) h_3 = \dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_2 (h_3 - h_2) = \dot{m}_1 (h_1 - h_3)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_1 \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_3 - h_2} = 1.0 \times \frac{2486.14 - 504.7}{504.7 - 167.57}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_2 = \frac{1981.44}{337.13} = 5.877\,\text{kg/s}
$$

> **(i) Mass flow rate of Stream 2: $\dot{m}_2 ≈ 5.88$ kg/s**

**Step 7 — Compute entropy generation:**

For an adiabatic process, the entropy balance reduces to:

$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}_3 s_3 - \dot{m}_1 s_1 - \dot{m}_2 s_2
$$

Entropy of Stream 1:

$$
s_1 = s_f + x_1 s_{fg} = 1.5301 + 0.9 \times 5.5970 = 1.5301 + 5.0373 = 6.5674\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

Entropy of Stream 2 (compressed liquid ≈ saturated liquid at 40 °C):

$$
s_2 \approx s_f(40^\circ C) = 0.5725\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

Entropy of Stream 3 (saturated liquid at 200 kPa):

$$
s_3 = s_f(200\,\text{kPa}) = 1.5301\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

Total mass flow of Stream 3:

$$
\dot{m}_3 = 1.0 + 5.877 = 6.877\,\text{kg/s}
$$

Entropy balance:

$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = (6.877 \times 1.5301) - (1.0 \times 6.5674) - (5.877 \times 0.5725)
$$

$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = 10.524 - 6.5674 - 3.365 = 0.5916\,\text{kW/K}
$$

> **(ii) Rate of entropy generation: $\dot{S}_{gen} ≈ 0.592$ kW/K**

> **[7 Marks: Energy balance for mass flow = 3, Entropy balance = 4]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Forgetting that $\dot{Q}$ is positive for heat *into* the system and $\dot{W}$ is positive for work *done by* the system. Many students lose 1-2 marks per question due to inconsistent sign usage.
> 2. **Forgetting flow work in open systems:** A common error is to use $\Delta U$ instead of $\Delta H$ in open-system balances, omitting the $P\Delta V$ contribution. This leads to incorrect answers.
> 3. **Mixing up closed and open system equations:** Applying $\Delta U = Q - W$ where $\Delta H = Q - W_s$ is required. Always verify whether mass crosses the boundary.
> 4. **Ignoring kinetic and potential energy terms:** In problems involving flow through pipes, turbines, or elevated equipment, $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$ must be included if non-negligible.
> 5. **Forgetting to include latent heat:** When a phase change occurs (e.g., evaporation, condensation), the latent heat $\Delta H_{vap}$ must be added to the sensible heat term.
> 6. **Inconsistent reference states:** Using different $T_{ref}$ values for different streams in the same problem. The reference must be uniform; differences cancel out, but errors propagate if mixed.
> 7. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing kJ and J, or kg and g, within the same calculation. Always state units explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist: Energy Balances for Non-Reactive Systems**

- ☐ **First Law Statement:** Energy is conserved; it is neither created nor destroyed.
- ☐ **General Energy Balance:** $\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} e_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} e_{out}$
- ☐ **For Non-Reactive Systems:** Generation and consumption terms = 0.
- ☐ **Closed System Equation:** $\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W$
- ☐ **Open System (Steady State) Equation:** $\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$
- ☐ **Enthalpy Definition:** $H = U + PV$
- ☐ **Specific Energy:** $e = h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2 + gz$
- ☐ **Sensible Heat Formula:** $Q = m C_p \Delta T$
- ☐ **Latent Heat Formula:** $Q = m \Delta H_{vap}$
- ☐ **Ideal Gas Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$
- ☐ **Throttling Process:** $\Delta H = 0$ (adiabatic, no work).
- ☐ **Reference State:** Choose arbitrarily; only differences matter.
- ☐ **Sign Convention:** $\dot{Q} > 0$ (heat in), $\dot{W} > 0$ (work out).
- ☐ **Standard Constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_{p,water} = 4.184$ kJ/(kg·K).
- ☐ **Always Perform Material Balance First** before energy balance.
- ☐ **Verify Units and Sign** before finalizing the answer.
- ☐ **Check Physical Reasonableness:** A heating coil should give $\dot{Q} > 0$, a turbine should give $\dot{W}_s > 0$, etc.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Fundamentals of Energy Balances: Law of Conservation of Energy for Non-Reactive Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qualitative Study of Components of Energy Balance Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Qualitative Study of Components of Energy Balance Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Bioprocess Calculations (PCBBT205)**, the **General Energy Balance Equation** is a quantitative statement of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a bioprocess system. It accounts for all energy inflows, outflows, accumulation, and generation/consumption across a defined system boundary.

For a **bioprocess** (which is typically an open, steady-state or batch biochemical reactor), the general energy balance is:

$$\text{Accumulation of Energy} = \text{Energy In} - \text{Energy Out} + \text{Energy Generated} - \text{Energy Consumed}$$

The standard mathematical form for a steady-state flow system (like a CSTR in continuous fermentation) is:

$$\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$$

Where:
- $\Delta H$ = Change in enthalpy of the process stream
- $\Delta KE$ = Change in kinetic energy
- $\Delta PE$ = Change in potential energy
- $Q$ = Heat transferred to the system
- $W_s$ = Shaft work done by the system

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A "qualitative study" means you are NOT required to numerically solve the energy balance, but you MUST understand the *physical meaning, sign convention, and role* of each term, and identify which terms are *significant or negligible* in different bioprocess scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a bioprocess reactor as a **"kitchen cooking pot"** on a stove:

- **Input ingredients (substrate + air)** carry chemical energy → This is like the raw materials entering with energy content.
- **The stove flame (Q)** supplies external heat → This is the heat transfer term.
- **The mixer blade (W_s)** does work on the contents (agitation) → This is the shaft work term.
- **The cooking process itself** generates heat because the food is being transformed (exothermic biochemical reactions) → This is energy generation/consumption.
- **The steam rising from the pot** carries energy out → This is the output energy term (latent + sensible heat of vapor).
- **The cooked food leaving the pot** carries energy → This is the enthalpy of the product stream.

Just as an accountant tracks money (in, out, balance), an engineer tracks energy. The "components" of the energy balance are simply the *different types of energy transactions* that occur in the reactor.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (used in KTU problems):**
> - Standard temperature: $T_{ref} = 25°C = 298.15 \text{ K}$
> - Standard pressure: $P_{ref} = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
> - Standard heat of formation: $\Delta H_f^°$ at 25°C and 1 atm
> - Standard heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c^°$ at 25°C and 1 atm
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

The **sign convention** is critical:
- **Energy IN to the system → Positive (+)**
- **Energy OUT of the system → Negative (−)**
- **Heat absorbed by the system (Q) → Positive**
- **Work done BY the system → Positive**
- **Exothermic reaction (heat released) → Negative $\Delta H_{rxn}$**
- **Endothermic reaction (heat absorbed) → Positive $\Delta H_{rxn}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Components

The general energy balance for a **bioprocess** is qualitatively composed of the following energy terms. Let us break down each:

### 2.1.1 Enthalpy Change ($\Delta H$)
Enthalpy ($H$) represents the **total heat content** of a stream at a given state (temperature, pressure, composition). It includes the **sensible heat** (due to temperature change), **latent heat** (due to phase change), and the **heat of reaction** (due to chemical/biological transformation).

In a bioprocess reactor, $\Delta H$ arises from:
1. **Sensible heat** of input and output streams (heating/cooling of substrates, products, and media).
2. **Latent heat** when water evaporates (especially in aerobic fermentation where a lot of water is stripped off).
3. **Heat of reaction** ($\Delta H_{rxn}$) from microbial metabolism (catabolism + anabolism).
4. **Heat of mixing** when two streams of different compositions are combined.

> [!NOTE]
> For most bioprocesses, the **heat of reaction** is the LARGEST contributor, because microbial growth on substrate releases substantial metabolic heat. This is why fermenter cooling jackets are essential.

### 2.1.2 Heat of Reaction ($\Delta H_{rxn}$)
The energy released or absorbed when reactants (substrates like glucose) are converted to products (biomass, CO₂, H₂O, ethanol, etc.) by the microorganisms.

For a typical aerobic fermentation:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -2816 \text{ kJ/mol glucose})$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction is **exothermic** (releases heat).

### 2.1.3 Sensible Heat ($Q_s$)
The energy required to change the temperature of a substance **without** changing its phase.

$$Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = n \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass (kg)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·K)
- $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in}$ (K)
- $n$ = moles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For water-based fermentation broths, $C_p \approx 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, which is very close to that of pure water.

### 2.1.4 Latent Heat ($Q_L$)
The energy required to change the **phase** of a substance (e.g., liquid water → vapor) at constant temperature.

$$Q_L = m \cdot \lambda$$

Where $\lambda$ is the **latent heat of vaporization** (for water at 100°C, $\lambda = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$).

In aerobic fermentations, large amounts of water vapor leave with the exhaust air, carrying significant latent heat. This is often the **largest heat loss term** in industrial bioreactors.

### 2.1.5 Heat of Mixing ($\Delta H_{mix}$)
The enthalpy change when two or more streams are mixed. It can be positive (endothermic, absorption of heat) or negative (exothermic, release of heat).

### 2.1.6 Heat Transfer ($Q$)
The heat exchanged between the system and surroundings through the reactor wall, cooling/heating coils, or jackets.

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = log mean temperature difference (K)

### 2.1.7 Shaft Work ($W_s$)
Mechanical energy added to the system via agitators, impellers, pumps, compressors, or gas spargers. In a fermenter, this is the energy input from the **impeller motor** and the **air compressor** for aeration.

$$W_s = W_{agitator} + W_{compressor}$$

### 2.1.8 Kinetic and Potential Energy Changes ($\Delta KE$, $\Delta PE$)
- $\Delta KE$ is due to velocity changes of inlet/outlet streams.
- $\Delta PE$ is due to elevation changes of inlet/outlet streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** For most bioprocess reactors (low velocities, no significant elevation change), $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$ are **NEGLIGIBLE** compared to the other terms. You should state this assumption clearly in your answer.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Formula | Typical Units | Significance in Bioprocess |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Sensible Heat | $Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | Heating/cooling of broth |
| 2 | Latent Heat | $Q_L = m \cdot \lambda$ | kJ | Water vapor in exhaust |
| 3 | Heat of Reaction | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^°(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^°(\text{reactants})$ | kJ/mol | Metabolic heat from microbes |
| 4 | Heat of Combustion | $\Delta H_c^°$ (for substrates) | kJ/mol | Energy content of feed |
| 5 | Heat Transfer | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W or kW | Through cooling jacket |
| 6 | Shaft Work | $W_s = W_{agitator} + W_{compressor}$ | kJ/s = kW | Mechanical energy input |
| 7 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | kJ | Usually negligible |
| 8 | Potential Energy | $PE = m \cdot g \cdot z$ | kJ | Usually negligible |
| 9 | Steady-State Balance | $\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s$ | kJ | Master equation |
| 10 | Accumulation Term | $\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = \sum E_{in} - \sum E_{out} + E_{gen} - E_{cons}$ | kJ/s | For transient (batch) processes |

## 2.3 Why Each Component Matters in Engineering

In real-world **bioprocess engineering**:

- **Industrial bioreactors** (e.g., for antibiotic production, ethanol fermentation) generate enormous metabolic heat (often 5–15 kW per m³). The **cooling jacket design** (which depends on $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$) is one of the most critical aspects of fermenter design.
- **Downstream processing** (evaporation, distillation, drying) consumes large amounts of energy, with **latent heat** being dominant.
- **Sterilization** of media in continuous sterilizers requires careful **sensible heat** calculations.
- The **compressor work** for sterile air supply can be a major operating cost, so $W_s$ from the air sparger is non-negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> The first step in any bioprocess energy balance is to **identify which terms are significant and which can be neglected**. This qualitative judgment is what KTU tests in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Energy Balance Equation

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed. For any system:

$$dE_{sys} = dE_{in} - dE_{out} + dE_{generated} - dE_{consumed}$$

Now, the **total energy** of a flowing stream includes internal energy, kinetic energy, potential energy, and flow work (pressure-volume work):

$$E = U + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz + Pv$$

Where:
- $U$ = internal energy
- $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ = kinetic energy
- $mgz$ = potential energy
- $Pv$ = flow work (energy to push mass into the system against system pressure)

For convenience, we define **enthalpy**:

$$H = U + Pv$$

So total stream energy becomes:

$$E = H + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz$$

Substituting back into the first law, the **General Energy Balance for an Open Flow System** becomes:

$$\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = \sum_{in} \left( H + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz \right) - \sum_{out} \left( H + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz \right) + Q - W_s$$

Writing this in change form (inlet minus outlet):

$$\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = \Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE + Q - W_s$$

For **steady state** (no accumulation: $\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = 0$):

$$\boxed{\Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W_s}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Heat of Reaction from Heats of Formation

The standard heat of reaction at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is given by **Hess's Law**:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^° = \sum_{i} \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^°(\text{products}) - \sum_{j} \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^°(\text{reactants})$$

Where $\nu$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** (positive for products, negative for reactants when written on the same side).

**Example: Aerobic Glucose Oxidation**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6(s) + 6O_2(g) \rightarrow 6CO_2(g) + 6H_2O(l)$$

Given standard heats of formation ($\Delta H_f^°$ in kJ/mol):
- Glucose (s): $\Delta H_f^° = -1273.3$
- O₂ (g): $\Delta H_f^° = 0$ (element in standard state)
- CO₂ (g): $\Delta H_f^° = -393.5$
- H₂O (l): $\Delta H_f^° = -285.8$

Now applying Hess's Law step-by-step:

Step 1: Multiply each species by its stoichiometric coefficient.

For products:
- $6 \times \Delta H_f^°(CO_2) = 6 \times (-393.5) = -2361.0$ kJ
- $6 \times \Delta H_f^°(H_2O) = 6 \times (-285.8) = -1714.8$ kJ
- Sum of products = $-2361.0 + (-1714.8) = -4075.8$ kJ

For reactants:
- $1 \times \Delta H_f^°(glucose) = 1 \times (-1273.3) = -1273.3$ kJ
- $6 \times \Delta H_f^°(O_2) = 6 \times (0) = 0$ kJ
- Sum of reactants = $-1273.3 + 0 = -1273.3$ kJ

Step 2: Subtract reactants from products.

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^° = \sum \Delta H_f^°(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^°(\text{reactants})$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^° = (-4075.8) - (-1273.3)$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^° = -4075.8 + 1273.3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^° = -2802.5 \text{ kJ/mol glucose}}$$

The negative value confirms the reaction is **strongly exothermic** — this is the heat generated by microbes metabolizing glucose.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sensible Heat Balance for a Bioreactor

Consider a **fed-batch fermenter** with inlet feed at $T_{in}$ and outlet broth at $T_{out}$. The sensible heat balance over a small time interval $dt$:

$$\text{Heat in with feed} = \dot{m}_{in} \cdot C_p \cdot T_{in}$$

$$\text{Heat out with broth} = \dot{m}_{out} \cdot C_p \cdot T_{out}$$

The change in internal energy of the broth:

$$dU_{sys} = m_{sys} \cdot C_p \cdot dT$$

For a quasi-steady heat balance on the broth mass:

$$m_{sys} C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} C_p T_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} C_p T_{out} + Q_{rxn} - Q_{cooling}$$

This simplifies to the practical fermenter heat removal equation:

$$Q_{cooling} = Q_{rxn} + \dot{m}_{in} C_p T_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} C_p T_{out} - m_{sys} C_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

For **isothermal operation** ($dT/dt = 0$) and negligible feed/envelope difference:

$$\boxed{Q_{cooling} \approx Q_{rxn}}$$

This is the foundational design equation for fermenter cooling systems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Which Term Dominates in Which Bioprocess?

| Bioprocess Scenario | Dominant Energy Term | Negligible Terms |
|---------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Aerobic fermentation (e.g., antibiotic) | Heat of reaction + Latent heat (water vapor) | KE, PE |
| Anaerobic fermentation (e.g., ethanol) | Heat of reaction (smaller) + Sensible heat | KE, PE |
| Media sterilization (continuous) | Sensible heat | Reaction heat, KE, PE |
| Spray drying of enzymes | Latent heat + Sensible heat | Reaction heat |
| Crystallization of product | Heat of mixing + Sensible heat | KE, PE |
| Cell disruption (homogenizer) | Shaft work (mechanical → heat) | All others initially |
| Evaporation of broth | Latent heat | Reaction, KE, PE |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Energy Flows in a Bioprocess System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the various energy components enter, exit, and are transformed within a generalized bioprocess reactor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sensible Heat of Feed IN] -->|Q_s_in| C(Central Bioreactor)
    A2[Latent Heat of Feed IN] -->|Q_L_in| C
    A3[Chemical Energy of Substrate] -->|ΔH_substrate| C
    A4[Compressed Air IN] -->|W_compressor| C
    B1[Agitator Motor] -->|W_agitator| C
    C --> D1[Sensible Heat of Broth OUT] 
    C --> D2[Latent Heat of Water Vapor OUT] 
    C --> D3[Enthalpy of Product OUT] 
    C --> D4[Heat of Reaction Released] 
    D4 --> E1[Cooling Jacket Heat OUT] 
    D4 --> E2[Waste Heat to Environment] 
    C --> F1[KE Change Negligible] 
    C --> F2[PE Change Negligible] 
    style A3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style D4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style E1 fill:#B0E0E6
    style F1 fill:#E0E0E0
    style F2 fill:#E0E0E0
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Qualitative Hierarchy of Energy Terms

This diagram shows the **relative magnitude** of each energy component in a typical industrial aerobic fermenter (aerobic antibiotic production).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Energy Components Ranked by Magnitude] --> B[LARGEST: Heat of Reaction]
    B --> C[SECOND: Latent Heat of Vaporization]
    C --> D[THIRD: Sensible Heat of Streams]
    D --> E[FOURTH: Shaft Work Compressor and Agitator]
    E --> F[SMALLEST: Kinetic and Potential Energy]
    style B fill:#FF6B6B
    style C fill:#FFA500
    style D fill:#FFD700
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#D3D3D3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Balance Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[General Energy Balance] --> B[Is System Open or Closed]
    B -->|Closed Batch| C[Use U Internal Energy]
    B -->|Open Flow CSTR PFR| D[Use H Enthalpy]
    C --> E[Terms: Q W and ΔU]
    D --> F[Terms: Q Ws ΔH ΔKE ΔPE]
    E --> G[No flow work in closed]
    F --> H[Add flow work term Pv]
    G --> I[Q is Heat Exchange]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Shaft Work Ws from Agitator and Compressor]
    style A fill:#E6E6FA
    style C fill:#FFE4E1
    style D fill:#E0FFFF
    style J fill:#F0FFF0
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Identifying Significant Terms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Bioprocess Type] --> Q1{Is Reaction Exothermic}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Sig1[Heat of Reaction is SIGNIFICANT]
    Q1 -->|No| Sig2[Heat of Reaction is Negligible]
    Start --> Q2{Are there Phase Changes}
    Q2 -->|Yes Evaporation Drying| Sig3[Latent Heat is SIGNIFICANT]
    Q2 -->|No| Sig4[Latent Heat is Negligible]
    Start --> Q3{Is there Mechanical Agitation or Compression}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Sig5[Shaft Work is SIGNIFICANT]
    Q3 -->|No| Sig6[Shaft Work is Negligible]
    Start --> Q4{High Velocity or Elevation Change}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Sig7[KE and PE are SIGNIFICANT]
    Q4 -->|No| Sig8[KE and PE are Negligible]
    style Start fill:#FFFACD
    style Sig1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Sig3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Sig5 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Sig7 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Sig2 fill:#90EE90
    style Sig4 fill:#90EE90
    style Sig6 fill:#90EE90
    style Sig8 fill:#90EE90
```
