## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Heat of Reaction". Distinguish clearly between exothermic and endothermic reactions with one example of each from a bioprocess.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Heat of Reaction** ($\Delta H_{rxn}$) is the quantity of heat energy evolved (released) or absorbed during a chemical reaction, measured at constant pressure and at a specified temperature, when the reaction proceeds from reactants to products in stoichiometric proportions as written in the balanced equation.

**Distinction:**

| Feature | Exothermic | Endothermic |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of $\Delta H_{rxn}$ | Negative ($-$) | Positive ($+$) |
| Heat flow | System $\rightarrow$ Surroundings | Surroundings $\rightarrow$ System |
| Temperature of uninsulated system | Rises | Falls |
| Bioprocess example | Aerobic oxidation of glucose: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O$ ($\Delta H = -2802$ kJ/mol) | Photosynthetic fixation: $6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$ ($\Delta H = +2802$ kJ/mol) |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for distinction table + 1 for bioprocess example)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State Hess's Law. Why is it applicable in calculating the heat of reaction for processes that cannot be carried out experimentally in a single step?*

**Model Answer:**

**Hess's Law:** *When a reaction can be expressed as the algebraic sum of two or more other reactions, the heat of reaction of the overall reaction equals the algebraic sum of the heats of reaction of the individual steps, provided all reactions are measured at the same temperature.*

**Why it is applicable to multi-step / un-measurable reactions:**

1. **Enthalpy is a state function** — its change depends only on the *initial state* and the *final state*, not on the intermediate path.
2. Many reactions of practical interest (e.g., formation of $C_2H_2$ from $C$ and $H_2$, formation of $NH_3$ from $N_2$ and $H_2$, formation of $NO$ from elements) are **difficult, slow, or impossible to perform directly in a calorimeter** because the desired product cannot be obtained in pure form in a single experimental step.
3. Using Hess's Law, we can construct a *hypothetical multi-step pathway* using reactions whose enthalpies **are** easily measurable (e.g., combustion reactions), and algebraically combine them to obtain the desired $\Delta H$.

**(3 marks: 1 for statement + 1 for state function explanation + 1 for practical justification)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Heat of Reaction from Heats of Formation**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Calculate the standard heat of reaction for the complete oxidation of ethanol given the standard heats of formation at 25 °C. The reaction is:

$$C_2H_5OH(l) + 3O_2(g) \longrightarrow 2CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l)$$

Given:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ [C_2H_5OH(l)] = -277.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^\circ [CO_2(g)] = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^\circ [H_2O(l)] = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^\circ [O_2(g)] = 0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(b)** *(7 marks)* If 50 g of ethanol is completely combusted in a calorimetric fermentation off-gas analyser, calculate:
- (i) The number of moles of ethanol consumed
- (ii) The total heat released (in kJ)
- (iii) The bioprocess implication if this heat is not removed in a continuous bioreactor fed at the rate of 1.5 mol ethanol/h.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Write the formula:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{prod}) - \sum n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{react})$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the products sum:

$$\sum n_i \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{prod}) = 2 \times \Delta H_f^\circ(CO_2) + 3 \times \Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O)$$

$$= 2 \times (-393.5) + 3 \times (-285.8)$$

$$= -787.0 + (-857.4) = -1644.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the reactants sum:

$$\sum n_j \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{react}) = 1 \times \Delta H_f^\circ(C_2H_5OH) + 3 \times \Delta H_f^\circ(O_2)$$

$$= 1 \times (-277.0) + 3 \times 0 = -277.0 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = -1644.4 - (-277.0) = -1644.4 + 277.0 = -1367.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Result:** $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = -1367.4$ kJ per mole of ethanol. The negative sign confirms an **exothermic** reaction.

**(7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for writing formula, 3 marks for product/reactant sums, 1 mark for final subtraction, 1 mark for interpretation of sign)**

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-step Calculation:**

**(i) Moles of ethanol consumed:**

Molar mass of ethanol, $C_2H_5OH$ = $(2 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + 16 = 46$ g/mol.

$$n_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{46 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.087 \text{ mol}$$

**(ii) Total heat released:**

$$Q_{\text{released}} = n \times (-\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ) = 1.087 \text{ mol} \times 1367.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$Q_{\text{released}} = 1486.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Bioprocess implication:**

Heat generation rate from continuous feed:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{gen}} = 1.5 \text{ mol/h} \times 1367.4 \text{ kJ/mol} = 2051.1 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Converting to watts (1 kJ/h = 1/3600 kW):

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{gen}} = \frac{2051.1}{3600} = 0.570 \text{ kW} = 570 \text{ W}$$

**Implication:** This continuous heat generation of **570 W** would cause the broth temperature to rise steadily, denaturing enzymes and killing the microbial culture. A **cooling system** (cooling jacket, internal coil, or external heat exchanger) capable of removing at least **570 W** must be designed and operated in real-time with a **PID temperature controller** to maintain the broth at the optimal setpoint (typically 30–37 °C for most industrial microbes).

**(7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including engineering interpretation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Multi-Step Hess's Law Application**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* The standard heats of combustion of graphite, hydrogen, and acetylene ($C_2H_2$) at 25 °C are given below:

| Reaction | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ (kJ/mol) |
|---|---|
| $C(\text{graphite}) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g)$ | $-393.5$ |
| $H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$ | $-285.8$ |
| $C_2H_2(g) + \tfrac{5}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow 2CO_2(g) + H_2O(l)$ | $-1299.5$ |

Using Hess's Law, calculate the standard heat of formation of $C_2H_2(g)$ at 25 °C.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* The same $C_2H_2(g)$ is now considered for use as a reducing agent in a bioprocess at 65 °C. The mean molar heat capacities in the temperature range 25–65 °C are: $C_p[C(\text{graphite})] = 8.5$ J/(mol·K), $C_p[H_2(g)] = 28.8$ J/(mol·K), $C_p[C_2H_2(g)] = 43.9$ J/(mol·K). Using Kirchhoff's Law, estimate $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of $C_2H_2$ at 65 °C (338.15 K).

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Hess's Law Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Write the target reaction (formation of acetylene from elements):

$$2C(\text{graphite}) + H_2(g) \longrightarrow C_2H_2(g)$$

This is exactly the *reverse* of a combustion of acetylene. Note that the heat of **formation** equals the **negative** of the heat of combustion only when the compound contains exactly C, H, and O — and even then, careful stoichiometric bookkeeping is required. We will use Hess's Law systematically.

**Step 2:** Multiply Reaction 1 by 2 (we need 2 moles of $CO_2$ to cancel):

$$2C(\text{graphite}) + 2O_2(g) \longrightarrow 2CO_2(g) \quad \Delta H = 2 \times (-393.5) = -787.0 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Keep Reaction 2 as is:

$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \longrightarrow H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H = -285.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4:** Reverse Reaction 3 (we need $C_2H_2$ as product, not reactant):

$$2CO_2(g) + H_2O(l) \longrightarrow C_2H_2(g) + \tfrac{5}{2}O_2(g) \quad \Delta H = +1299.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5:** Add the three modified reactions:

- LHS total: $2C + 2O_2 + H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2CO_2 + H_2O$
- RHS total: $2CO_2 + H_2O + C_2H_2 + \tfrac{5}{2}O_2$

- Cancelling $2CO_2$ from both sides
- Cancelling $H_2O$ from both sides
- Net $O_2$ on LHS: $2 + 0.5 = 2.5$ moles; on RHS: $2.5$ moles → **cancels**

The net reaction is:

$$2C(\text{graphite}) + H_2(g) \longrightarrow C_2H_2(g)$$

**Step 6:** Sum the enthalpies:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(C_2H_2) = (-787.0) + (-285.8) + (+1299.5) = 226.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Result:** $\Delta H_f^\circ[C_2H_2(g)] = +226.7$ kJ/mol. The **positive** sign indicates acetylene is an **endothermic compound** (energy stored in its bonds — that's why it is a useful fuel).

**(7 marks: 1 for writing target reaction, 2 for setting up modified equations with correct sign flips and multiplications, 2 for cancellation/algebra, 1 for arithmetic sum, 1 for sign interpretation)**

---

**Part (b) — Kirchhoff's Law Application:**

**Step 1:** Identify $T_1 = 298.15$ K, $T_2 = 338.15$ K, so $\Delta T = 40$ K.

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta C_p$ for the formation reaction $2C + H_2 \rightarrow C_2H_2$:

$$\Delta C_p = \sum n_i C_{p,i}(\text{prod}) - \sum n_j C_{p,j}(\text{react})$$

$$\Delta C_p = [1 \times C_p(C_2H_2)] - [2 \times C_p(C) + 1 \times C_p(H_2)]$$

$$\Delta C_p = [1 \times 43.9] - [2 \times 8.5 + 1 \times 28.8]$$

$$\Delta C_p = 43.9 - [17.0 + 28.8] = 43.9 - 45.8 = -1.9 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Kirchhoff's Law (constant $C_p$ form):

$$\Delta H_{rxn}(T_2) = \Delta H_{rxn}(T_1) + \Delta C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}(338.15 \text{ K}) = 226.7 \text{ kJ/mol} + (-1.9 \text{ J/(mol·K)}) \times (40 \text{ K})$$

**Step 4:** Convert units to be consistent. Convert $\Delta C_p$ to kJ/(mol·K):

$$\Delta C_p = -1.9 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = -0.0019 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}(338.15 \text{ K}) = 226.7 + (-0.0019)(40)$$

$$= 226.7 - 0.076 = 226.624 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6:** Round to appropriate significant figures:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ[C_2H_2 \text{ at } 65 °C] \approx 226.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Interpretation:** The heat of formation has decreased very slightly (by 0.076 kJ/mol) as temperature increased from 25 °C to 65 °C, because the products have a *smaller* total heat capacity than the reactants.

**(7 marks: 1 for identifying temperatures, 2 for computing $\Delta C_p$, 1 for unit conversion, 2 for substituting into Kirchhoff's formula, 1 for final answer and interpretation)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner-Verified):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Combustion Route (–4 to –6 marks):** The single most common mistake is plugging the heats of combustion in the wrong order. **Always remember:** in the heat-of-combustion formula, *reactants are subtracted from reactants* on the *negative* side and *products on the positive* side — the *opposite* of the formation route. Mnemonic: **"Combustion flips the formula."**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Stoichiometric Coefficients (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often forget to multiply $\Delta H_f$ or $\Delta H_c$ values by their stoichiometric coefficients $n_i$. The summation is over $n_i \cdot \Delta H_i$, not just $\Delta H_i$.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency in Kirchhoff's Law (–2 marks):** $C_p$ is typically in J/(mol·K) while $\Delta H$ is in kJ/mol. **Convert all to the same base unit** (either kJ throughout or J throughout) *before* substituting.
>
> 4. **Not Reversing/Multiplying Reactions Properly in Multi-Step Hess's Law (–3 marks):** When a reaction is reversed, **change the sign** of its $\Delta H$. When it is multiplied by a factor $n$, **multiply $\Delta H$ by the same factor $n$**. Forgetting either sign-flip or factor is a major mark-loss.
>
> 5. **Final-Answer Sign Missing (–1 mark):** Always write the final $\Delta H_{rxn}$ with its sign (negative for exothermic, positive for endothermic) and a clear unit (kJ/mol). Examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguous answers.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Heat of reaction** is the enthalpy change at constant $T$ and $P$ for a reaction as written.
> - **Exothermic** $\Rightarrow \Delta H_{rxn} < 0$; **Endothermic** $\Rightarrow \Delta H_{rxn} > 0$.
> - **Standard state** = 1 atm and **25 °C (298.15 K)**. Look for the superscript **°** symbol.
> - **Hess's Law** rests on enthalpy being a **state function** — path-independent.
> - **Route 1 (Formation):** $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum n \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{prod}) - \sum n \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{react})$.
> - **Route 2 (Combustion):** $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum n \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{react}) - \sum n \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{prod})$.
> - **Route 3 (Kirchhoff's Law):** $\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$.
> - **Hess's Law operational steps:** (1) Write target reaction, (2) Modify given reactions (reverse or multiply), (3) Sign-flip and scale $\Delta H$ values accordingly, (4) Add reactions to cancel intermediates, (5) Add corresponding $\Delta H$ values.
> - **Bioprocess link:** $\Delta H_{rxn}$ determines the **cooling duty** $Q_{gen} = r_S \cdot V \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn})$, which governs fermenter cooling-jacket design.
> - **Standard heat of formation of any element in its standard state = 0** (e.g., $\Delta H_f^\circ[O_2(g)] = 0$).
> - **Sign discipline is non-negotiable** — examiners actively look for it.
> - **Always carry units** (kJ/mol for $\Delta H$, J/(mol·K) for $C_p$); be ready to convert between them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stoichiometry of Cell Growth and Product Formation: Elemental Balances, Degree of Reduction, Respiratory Quotient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stoichiometry of Cell Growth and Product Formation: Elemental Balances, Degree of Reduction, Respiratory Quotient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stoichiometry of Cell Growth — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometry of cell growth** is the quantitative study of the mass and elemental balances governing the conversion of substrates (carbon source, nitrogen source, oxygen) into **biomass**, **metabolic products** (CO₂, H₂O, ethanol, lactic acid, etc.), and **energy** during microbial growth. It is expressed as a single, balanced chemical equation in which **all atoms (C, H, O, N) and the overall electron balance must reconcile**.

For any aerobic growth process on a single organic substrate, the generalized balanced equation is:

$$
\text{C}_w\text{H}_x\text{O}_y\text{N}_z + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow c\,\text{CH}_\alpha\text{O}_\beta\text{N}_\delta + d\,\text{CO}_2 + e\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

where the **biomass** is represented by a representative empirical formula $\text{CH}_\alpha\text{O}_\beta\text{N}_\delta$ (e.g., for many bacteria and yeasts, $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$).

## 1.2 Elemental Balance — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Elemental (or Element) Balance** is a macroscopic accounting principle based on the **Law of Conservation of Mass**, applied to each element (C, H, O, N, P, S) entering and leaving a bioreactor. The total mass of any element supplied in the feed (substrate + air/oxygen + ammonia) must equal the total mass of that element recovered in the outlet streams (biomass + product + CO₂ + H₂O + residual substrate).

The four primary elemental balances for aerobic growth on a glucose–ammonia medium are:

| Element | Balance Equation | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | $6 = c + d$ | Glucose C → Biomass C + CO₂ C |
| **H** | $12 + 3b = 1.8c + 2e$ | Glucose H + NH₃ H → Biomass H + H₂O H |
| **N** | $b = 0.2c$ | NH₃ N → Biomass N |
| **O** | $6 + 2a = 0.5c + 2d + e$ | Glucose O + O₂ O → Biomass O + CO₂ O + H₂O O |

## 1.3 Degree of Reduction (γ) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Reduction (γ)** is the number of moles of electrons (or equivalents of O₂ required) released when **one mole of carbon** in a compound is completely oxidized to **CO₂ and NH₃** (the fully oxidized reference state for biogenic elements). It is a **dimensionless quantity** normalized per mole of carbon atom and quantifies the **reducing power** (i.e., the energy content per C-atom) of a substrate relative to biomass.

For a generalized empirical formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c$:

$$
\gamma \;=\; \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{n} \;=\; 4 + \frac{a}{n} - 2\frac{b}{n} - 3\frac{c}{n}
$$

The reference **oxidation numbers** used are: **C = +4, H = +1, O = –2, N = –3**.

## 1.4 Respiratory Quotient (RQ) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** is the **dimensionless ratio** of the moles of **CO₂ produced** to the moles of **O₂ consumed** during a biological reaction. It is a direct experimental diagnostic for the **type of substrate** being metabolized and the **oxidation state** of that substrate.

$$
\text{RQ} \;=\; \frac{\text{moles of CO}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{moles of O}_2 \text{ consumed}} \;=\; \frac{r_{\text{CO}_2}}{r_{\text{O}_2}}
$$

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Account of Atoms

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a "Carbon Bank Account" analogy:**
> Microbes act as a **chemical accountant** running a bank. Every morning (substrate feed), the microbe **deposits** carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen atoms into its balance sheet. Throughout the day (metabolism), it **withdraws** some atoms to:
> - **Build new cells** (biomass — the "savings account"),
> - **Generate energy** (CO₂ + H₂O — the "currency exchange"),
> - **Secrete by-products** (ethanol, lactate, citrate — the "discretionary spending").
> An **elemental balance** is the **monthly ledger statement** that must balance to zero — every atom deposited must be accounted for in the output streams. The **Degree of Reduction** is like the **"credit rating"** of the substrate — methane (γ = 8) is a "rich customer" with lots of electrons to donate, while CO₂ (γ = 0) is a "bankrupt customer" with no reducing power left. The **RQ** is the **"withdrawal pattern"** — a chemist measuring it can tell whether the cell is burning a sugar (RQ ≈ 1.0) or a fatty acid (RQ < 1.0) just by looking at the gas exchange.

**Standard Reference Values of γ for Key Substrates:**

| Compound | Formula | γ (per C-atom) |
|---|---|---|
| **CO₂** | CO₂ | **0** |
| **Formic acid** | CH₂O₂ | **2** |
| **Methanol** | CH₄O | **6** |
| **Glucose** | C₆H₁₂O₆ | **4** |
| **Acetic acid** | C₂H₄O₂ | **4** |
| **Ethanol** | C₂H₆O | **6** |
| **Methane** | CH₄ | **8** |
| **Biomass (typical)** | CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂ | **4.2** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Framework

The stoichiometry of cell growth rests on **three pillars of conservation** simultaneously enforced:

1. **Conservation of Mass (per element)** → C, H, O, N atom balance
2. **Conservation of Charge / Electrons** → Degree of Reduction balance (the "electron balance")
3. **Conservation of Energy** → Yield coefficients and heat evolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theoretical Insight (Roels, 1980):** The degree of reduction is **conserved** in microbial metabolism. The **average γ** of all substrates consumed must equal the **average γ** of all products formed (biomass + CO₂ + excreted metabolites), divided by the carbon fraction. This is the **Electron Balance** and is a powerful tool to check elemental balances for internal consistency.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Solving Stoichiometric Problems

1. **Identify the system boundary** — what enters (substrate, O₂, NH₃) and what leaves (biomass, CO₂, H₂O, product).
2. **Write the balanced stoichiometric equation** with unknown stoichiometric coefficients.
3. **Apply the four elemental balances** (C, H, O, N) — these yield 4 linear equations.
4. **Choose a basis** — usually **1 mole of substrate** (or 1 g substrate) and solve for the remaining coefficients.
5. **Calculate yield coefficients** — e.g., $Y_{X/S}$ (g biomass / g substrate), $Y_{CO_2/S}$, $Y_{O_2/S}$.
6. **Cross-verify using the electron (γ) balance** as a consistency check.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following equations are **exam-locked essentials** — every KTU question from this module can be solved using a combination of these.

### Core Stoichiometric Equation

$$
\text{C}_w\text{H}_x\text{O}_y\text{N}_z + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow c\,\text{CH}_\alpha\text{O}_\beta\text{N}_\delta + d\,\text{CO}_2 + e\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Elemental Balance Equations

| Element | Balance Expression | Equation No. |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon (C)** | $w = c + d$ | (1) |
| **Hydrogen (H)** | $x + 3b = \alpha c + 2e$ | (2) |
| **Nitrogen (N)** | $z + b = \delta c$ | (3) |
| **Oxygen (O)** | $y + 2a = \beta c + 2d + e$ | (4) |

### Degree of Reduction Formulas

| Situation | Formula |
|---|---|
| **General compound** $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{n}$ |
| **Per gram of substrate** | $\gamma_S = \dfrac{4 + (a/n) - 2(b/n) - 3(c/n)}{12 + a + 16b + 14c}$ (mol e⁻ / g) |
| **Electron balance (Roels)** | $\gamma_S \cdot F_S = \gamma_X \cdot F_X + \gamma_{CO_2} \cdot F_{CO_2} + \sum \gamma_{P_i} F_{P_i}$ |
| **Biomass (typical)** | $\gamma_X = 4.2$ (for $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$) |

### Respiratory Quotient Formulas

| Situation | Formula |
|---|---|
| **General** | $\text{RQ} = \dfrac{r_{\text{CO}_2}}{r_{\text{O}_2}}$ |
| **Pure oxidation** $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b \to \text{CO}_2$ | $\text{RQ} = \dfrac{n}{n + (a/4) - (b/2)}$ |
| **For biomass formation from glucose** | $\text{RQ}_{\text{growth}} \approx 1.0$ |
| **RQ → substrate identity** | RQ = 1 → carbohydrate; RQ < 1 → lipid/CH₄; RQ > 1 → organic acid |

### Yield Coefficient Definitions

| Coefficient | Definition | Typical Value (aerobic, glucose) |
|---|---|---|
| $Y_{X/S}$ | g biomass / g substrate consumed | **0.4 – 0.5 g/g** |
| $Y_{X/O_2}$ | g biomass / g O₂ consumed | **0.9 – 1.4 g/g** |
| $Y_{CO_2/S}$ | mol CO₂ / mol substrate | **3 – 6** (for glucose) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioprocess Design**: Yield coefficients (Yₓ/s, Yₓ/O₂) are essential inputs for sizing **bioreactor volume, aeration rate, and cooling duty**.
- **Fermentation Monitoring**: Online **off-gas analysis (CO₂ and O₂ in exhaust)** is used to compute the **RQ in real time** and detect metabolic shifts (e.g., diauxic growth, Crabtree effect in yeast).
- **Metabolic Engineering**: Degree of Reduction is used to **identify rate-limiting steps** in electron flux and to **redesign redox cofactor balances** (NADH/NADPH).
- **Bioprocess Economics**: Maximum theoretical yield sets the **upper bound** for cost of goods — actual yields in industry are 50–90% of theoretical.
- **Environmental Biotechnology**: RQ and elemental balances underpin **biogas process design** and **microbial fuel cell** electron recovery calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Elemental Balance for Aerobic Growth on Glucose

**Problem:** *Write the stoichiometric equation for the aerobic growth of a microorganism with biomass formula $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$ on glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$) with ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) as the nitrogen source. Calculate the maximum theoretical yield coefficient $Y_{X/S}$.*

### Step 1 — Write the generalized balanced equation

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow c\,\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2} + d\,\text{CO}_2 + e\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

We have **5 unknowns** ($a, b, c, d, e$) and **4 elemental balances** → **1 degree of freedom remains** (we will set $c$ as the independent variable).

### Step 2 — Carbon Balance (Equation 1)

Carbons in: **6** (from glucose). Carbons out: $1 \cdot c$ (in biomass) + $1 \cdot d$ (in CO₂).

$$
6 = c + d
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
d = 6 - c
$$

### Step 3 — Nitrogen Balance (Equation 2)

Nitrogens in: $0 + b$ (from NH₃). Nitrogens out: $0.2 \cdot c$ (in biomass).

$$
b = 0.2\,c
$$

### Step 4 — Hydrogen Balance (Equation 3)

Hydrogens in: $12$ (glucose) + $3b$ (from NH₃). Hydrogens out: $1.8c$ (in biomass) + $2e$ (in H₂O).

$$
12 + 3b = 1.8\,c + 2e
$$

Substitute $b = 0.2c$:

$$
12 + 3(0.2c) = 1.8c + 2e
$$

$$
12 + 0.6c = 1.8c + 2e
$$

$$
12 = 1.2c + 2e
$$

Solving for $e$:

$$
2e = 12 - 1.2c \quad\Rightarrow\quad e = 6 - 0.6c
$$

### Step 5 — Oxygen Balance (Equation 4)

Oxygens in: $6$ (glucose) + $2a$ (from O₂). Oxygens out: $0.5c$ (in biomass) + $2d$ (in CO₂) + $e$ (in H₂O).

$$
6 + 2a = 0.5\,c + 2d + e
$$

Substitute $d = 6 - c$ and $e = 6 - 0.6c$:

$$
6 + 2a = 0.5c + 2(6 - c) + (6 - 0.6c)
$$

$$
6 + 2a = 0.5c + 12 - 2c + 6 - 0.6c
$$

$$
6 + 2a = 18 + (0.5 - 2 - 0.6)c
$$

$$
6 + 2a = 18 - 2.1c
$$

Solving for $a$:

$$
2a = 12 - 2.1c \quad\Rightarrow\quad a = 6 - 1.05c
$$

### Step 6 — Final Balanced Equation (with $c = 3$ chosen as basis)

Setting $c = 3$ (a convenient choice; it corresponds to **half a mole of glucose** since glucose has 6 C):

$$
b = 0.2 \times 3 = 0.6
$$

$$
d = 6 - 3 = 3
$$

$$
e = 6 - 0.6(3) = 4.2
$$

$$
a = 6 - 1.05(3) = 2.85
$$

**The fully balanced stoichiometric equation is therefore:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2.85\,\text{O}_2 + 0.6\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2} + 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4.2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Calculate Maximum Theoretical Yield $Y_{X/S}$

The molecular weight of biomass $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$:

$$
M_X = 12(1) + 1(1.8) + 16(0.5) + 14(0.2) = 12 + 1.8 + 8.0 + 2.8 = 24.6 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

The molecular weight of glucose:

$$
M_S = 12(6) + 1(12) + 16(6) = 180 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

From the balanced equation, **3 moles of biomass** (mass = $3 \times 24.6 = 73.8$ g) are produced per **1 mole of glucose** (mass = 180 g):

$$
Y_{X/S} = \frac{3 \times 24.6}{1 \times 180} = \frac{73.8}{180} = 0.41 \;\text{g biomass / g glucose}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Y_{X/S}^{\max} = 0.41 \;\text{g/g}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using O₂ uptake:** $Y_{X/O_2} = 73.8 / (2.85 \times 32) = 73.8 / 91.2 = 0.81$ g/g. This value is in the **expected aerobic range of 0.8 – 1.2 g/g**, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Degree of Reduction of Key Substrates

**General formula** for a compound $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{n}
$$

### Case A — Methane ($\text{CH}_4$)

$$
\gamma_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{4(1) + 4 - 2(0) - 3(0)}{1} = \frac{8}{1} = 8
$$

### Case B — Methanol ($\text{CH}_4\text{O}$)

$$
\gamma_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \frac{4(1) + 4 - 2(1) - 3(0)}{1} = \frac{6}{1} = 6
$$

### Case C — Ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$)

$$
\gamma_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}} = \frac{4(2) + 6 - 2(1) - 3(0)}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6
$$

### Case D — Acetic Acid ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$)

$$
\gamma_{\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}} = \frac{4(2) + 4 - 2(2) - 3(0)}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4
$$

### Case E — Glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$)

$$
\gamma_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 3(0)}{6} = \frac{24 + 12 - 12}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4
$$

### Case F — Biomass ($\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$)

$$
\gamma_X = \frac{4(1) + 1.8 - 2(0.5) - 3(0.2)}{1} = \frac{4 + 1.8 - 1.0 - 0.6}{1} = 4.2
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Respiratory Quotient for Various Substrates

### General RQ Formula for $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b$ Combustion

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n + \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow n\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{a}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{RQ} = \frac{n}{n + (a/4) - (b/2)}
$$

### Substrate A — Glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$)

$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 6 + \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 + 3 - 3 = 6
$$

$$
\text{RQ}_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.00
$$

### Substrate B — Ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$)

$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 2 + \frac{6}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 2 + 1.5 - 0.5 = 3
$$

$$
\text{RQ}_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.67
$$

### Substrate C — Methane ($\text{CH}_4$)

$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - 0 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

$$
\text{RQ}_{\text{methane}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50
$$

### Substrate D — Oxalic Acid ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}_4$)

$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 2 + \frac{2}{4} - \frac{4}{2} = 2 + 0.5 - 2 = 0.5
$$

$$
\text{RQ}_{\text{oxalic acid}} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4.00
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation #4: Electron (γ) Balance Cross-Check

For the aerobic growth equation derived in §3.1, verify the **γ balance**:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2.85\,\text{O}_2 + 0.6\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2} + 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4.2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Degrees of reduction:**
- $\gamma_{\text{glucose}} = 4$
- $\gamma_{\text{O}_2} = -4$ (oxygen is the electron acceptor)
- $\gamma_{\text{NH}_3} = 0$ (nitrogen is already in its reduced form as –3)
- $\gamma_{\text{biomass}} = 4.2$
- $\gamma_{\text{CO}_2} = 0$ (fully oxidized)
- $\gamma_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0$

**Electron balance (input electrons = output electrons):**

$$
\gamma_{\text{glucose}} \cdot 6 + \gamma_{\text{O}_2} \cdot 2 \cdot 2.85 = \gamma_X \cdot 1 \cdot 3 + \gamma_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot 3
$$

$$
(4)(6) + (-4)(5.7) = (4.2)(3) + (0)(3)
$$

$$
24 - 22.8 = 12.6
$$

$$
1.2 \approx 12.6
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The above appears inconsistent because we have **6 C per mole of glucose, but only 3 C in biomass on the right** — the γ balance should be done per **C-atom** or as **total electrons transferred to O₂**. Recomputing per mole of oxygen (with sign convention that O₂ accepts 4 e⁻ per mole):
>
> **Total electrons in substrate:** $\gamma_{\text{glucose}} \times n_C = 4 \times 6 = 24$ electron equivalents
>
> **Total electrons accepted by O₂:** $4 \times 2.85 = 11.4$ electron equivalents
>
> **Total electrons in biomass:** $\gamma_X \times 3 = 4.2 \times 3 = 12.6$ electron equivalents
>
> **Total electrons in CO₂:** $0 \times 3 = 0$
>
> **Check:** $11.4 + 12.6 = 24.0$ ✓ **(Balanced!)** The electron balance is verified.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Master Summary Table

| Substrate | Formula | $\gamma$ | RQ | $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$ (g/g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | **4** | **1.00** | **0.41** |
| Ethanol | C₂H₆O | **6** | **0.67** | **0.61** |
| Methanol | CH₄O | **6** | **0.67** | **0.41** |
| Acetic acid | C₂H₄O₂ | **4** | **1.00** | **0.34** |
| Methane | CH₄ | **8** | **0.50** | **0.62** |
| Glycerol | C₃H₈O₃ | **4.67** | **0.86** | **0.45** |
| Biomass | CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂ | **4.2** | — | — |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Elemental Flow in Aerobic Cell Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Glucose Substrate C6H12O6] --> B[Microbial Cell Reactor]
    C[Oxygen Supply O2] --> B
    D[Ammonia Supply NH3] --> B
    B --> E[Biomass CH1.8O0.5N0.2]
    B --> F[Carbon Dioxide CO2]
    B --> G[Water H2O]
    E --> H[Carbon Element C]
    E --> I[Hydrogen Element H]
    E --> J[Oxygen Element O]
    E --> K[Nitrogen Element N]
    F --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    H --> L[Elemental Balance C equation]
    I --> M[Elemental Balance H equation]
    J --> N[Elemental Balance O equation]
    K --> O[Elemental Balance N equation]
    L --> P[Yield Coefficient YXS Calculation]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Maximum Theoretical Biomass Yield]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Degree of Reduction Logic Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Substrate CnHaObNc] --> B[Compute Per Carbon]
    B --> C[Apply Formula 4n plus a minus 2b minus 3c divided by n]
    C --> D[gamma value 0 to 8]
    D --> E{gamma range}
    E -->|gamma equals 0| F[Fully Oxidized like CO2]
    E -->|gamma between 1 and 3| G[Organic Acids like Acetic]
    E -->|gamma equals 4| H[Carbohydrates like Glucose]
    E -->|gamma between 4 and 6| I[Reduced Substrates like Ethanol]
    E -->|gamma equals 8| J[Highly Reduced like Methane]
    F --> K[Biomass Synthesis Limitation]
    G --> L[Moderate Reducing Power]
    H --> M[Standard Fermentation Substrate]
    I --> N[High Electron Donor Capacity]
    J --> O[Maximum Theoretical Yield Possible]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Respiratory Quotient Diagnostic Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure CO2 Production Rate] --> C[RQ Calculator]
    B[Measure O2 Consumption Rate] --> C
    C --> D[RQ equals rCO2 divided by rO2]
    D --> E{RQ Value Interpretation}
    E -->|RQ less than 0.7| F[Lipid or Alkane Substrate]
    E -->|RQ equals 0.5| G[Methane Oxidation]
    E -->|RQ between 0.7 and 0.9| H[Fatty Acid or Glycerol]
    E -->|RQ equals 1.0| I[Carbohydrate like Glucose]
    E -->|RQ greater than 1.0| J[Organic Acid Metabolism]
    E -->|RQ equals 4.0| K[Oxalic Acid or Reduced Acid]
    F --> L[Adjust Process Control Strategy]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Optimize Aeration and Feed]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow: Stoichiometric Problem-Solving Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Substrate and Biomass Formula] --> S2[Step 2 Write Generalized Equation with Unknown Coefficients]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Write Four Elemental Balances C H O N]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Choose Basis Usually One Mole of Substrate]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Solve System of Linear Equations]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Substitute Coefficients into Equation]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Calculate Molecular Weights and Yields]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Verify with Electron Gamma Balance]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Cross Check with RQ Consistency]
    S9 --> S10[Final Validated Stoichiometric Equation and Yield]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Degree of Reduction." Calculate the degree of reduction of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) and methane (CH₄).**

**Model Answer:**

> **Degree of Reduction (γ)** is defined as the number of electrons released per mole of carbon atom when a compound is completely oxidized to its reference oxidation products (CO₂, H₂O, and NH₃ for biogenic elements). It is a measure of the **reducing power** of a substrate.

**General formula** for $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{n}
$$

**For Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆):**

$$
\gamma = \frac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 0}{6} = \frac{24 + 12 - 12}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4
$$

**For Methane (CH₄):**

$$
\gamma = \frac{4(1) + 4 - 0 - 0}{1} = 8
$$

**Interpretation:** Glucose has γ = 4, while methane has γ = 8, indicating that methane is **twice as reduced** as glucose per C-atom and thus has a higher energy content and higher potential biomass yield.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by Respiratory Quotient (RQ)? Calculate the RQ for the complete oxidation of glucose and ethanol.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** is defined as the ratio of the moles of **CO₂ produced** to the moles of **O₂ consumed** during the oxidation of a substrate.

$$
\text{RQ} = \frac{\text{moles of CO}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{moles of O}_2 \text{ consumed}}
$$

**For Glucose:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{RQ}_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.00
$$

**For Ethanol:**

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{RQ}_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.67
$$

**Diagnostic Value:** RQ = 1 indicates carbohydrate metabolism; RQ < 1 (like 0.67 for ethanol) indicates a more reduced substrate that requires more O₂ per CO₂ evolved.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the concept of elemental balance in microbial growth. Write the generalized stoichiometric equation for aerobic growth on an organic substrate with ammonia as the nitrogen source. State the four elemental balance equations clearly. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A bacterium with biomass formula CH₁.₆₄O₀.₅₂N₀.₁₆ grows aerobically on glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) with NH₃ as the nitrogen source. Derive the complete stoichiometric equation and calculate the maximum theoretical biomass yield coefficient $Y_{X/S}$ in g/g. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Concept of Elemental Balance:** In any bioprocess, the **Law of Conservation of Mass** demands that the total mass of each chemical element entering the system must equal the total mass of that element leaving the system. This is formalized through **elemental balances** for C, H, O, N (and occasionally P, S), applied to the entire bioreactor as a control volume.

**Generalized Stoichiometric Equation:**

$$
\text{C}_w\text{H}_x\text{O}_y\text{N}_z + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow c\,\text{CH}_\alpha\text{O}_\beta\text{N}_\delta + d\,\text{CO}_2 + e\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Four Elemental Balance Equations:**

| Element | Equation |
|---|---|
| **C balance** | $w = c + d$ |
| **H balance** | $x + 3b = \alpha c + 2e$ |
| **N balance** | $z + b = \delta c$ |
| **O balance** | $y + 2a = \beta c + 2d + e$ |

**Engineering Utility:** These balances form the foundation of all yield coefficient calculations, oxygen demand estimations, and heat evolution predictions in bioreactor design.

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Write the balanced equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow c\,\text{CH}_{1.64}\text{O}_{0.52}\text{N}_{0.16} + d\,\text{CO}_2 + e\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Carbon balance:**

$$
6 = c + d \quad\Rightarrow\quad d = 6 - c
$$

**Step 3 — Nitrogen balance:**

$$
b = 0.16\,c
$$

**Step 4 — Hydrogen balance:**

$$
12 + 3b = 1.64c + 2e
$$

Substituting $b = 0.16c$:

$$
12 + 0.48c = 1.64c + 2e \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2e = 12 - 1.16c \quad\Rightarrow\quad e = 6 - 0.58c
$$

**Step 5 — Oxygen balance:**

$$
6 + 2a = 0.52c + 2d + e
$$

Substituting $d$ and $e$:

$$
6 + 2a = 0.52c + 2(6 - c) + (6 - 0.58c)
$$

$$
6 + 2a = 0.52c + 12 - 2c + 6 - 0.58c = 18 - 2.06c
$$

$$
2a = 12 - 2.06c \quad\Rightarrow\quad a = 6 - 1.03c
$$

**Step 6 — Set basis $c = 3$:**

- $b = 0.16 \times 3 = 0.48$
- $d = 6 - 3 = 3$
- $e = 6 - 0.58(3) = 4.26$
- $a = 6 - 1.03(3) = 2.91$

**Final balanced equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2.91\,\text{O}_2 + 0.48\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_{1.64}\text{O}_{0.52}\text{N}_{0.16} + 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4.26\,\text{H}_2\text{O}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate $Y_{X/S}$:**

Molecular weight of biomass:

$$
M_X = 12 + 1.64 + 16(0.52) + 14(0.16) = 12 + 1.64 + 8.32 + 2.24 = 24.2 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

Molecular weight of glucose = 180 g/mol.

$$
Y_{X/S} = \frac{3 \times 24.2}{1 \times 180} = \frac{72.6}{180} = 0.403 \;\text{g/g}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Y_{X/S}^{\max} = 0.40 \;\text{g biomass / g glucose}\;}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Define the "Degree of Reduction" and explain its significance in bioprocess calculations. Compute the degree of reduction for: (i) Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), (ii) Ethanol (C₂H₆O), (iii) Methane (CH₄), and (iv) Biomass (CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂). Comment on the relationship between γ and the maximum theoretical biomass yield. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A yeast grows on glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) under aerobic conditions with ammonia as the N-source. The biomass formula is CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂. Calculate the Respiratory Quotient (RQ) for this process. If the volumetric O₂ uptake rate is 10 mmol/L·h, what is the expected CO₂ evolution rate? (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Definition:** The **Degree of Reduction (γ)** is the number of electrons released per mole of carbon atom when a compound is oxidized to the reference state (CO₂, H₂O, NH₃). It quantifies the **reducing power per C-atom** and is essential for **electron balance** in bioprocesses.

**Formula** for $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{n}
$$

**Computations:**

| Compound | Calculation | $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) | $[4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 0] / 6$ | **4** |
| Ethanol (C₂H₆O) | $[4(2) + 6 - 2(1) - 0] / 2$ | **6** |
| Methane (CH₄) | $[4(1) + 4 - 0 - 0] / 1$ | **8** |
| Biomass (CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂) | $[4 + 1.8 - 1.0 - 0.6] / 1$ | **4.2** |

**Significance & Yield Relationship:** The maximum theoretical biomass yield is given by:

$$
Y_{X/S}^{\max} = \frac{\gamma_S \cdot M_X}{\gamma_X \cdot M_S}
$$

where $M_X$ and $M_S$ are molecular weights of biomass and substrate, respectively. **Substrates with higher γ (more reduced) generally yield more biomass** per unit mass because they contain more electrons that can be channelled into the more reduced biomass. This is why methane (γ = 8) has a much higher $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$ (~0.62 g/g) than glucose (γ = 4, $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$ = 0.41 g/g).

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Use the previously derived stoichiometric equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2.85\,\text{O}_2 + 0.6\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2} + 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4.2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate RQ:**

For every **2.85 moles of O₂ consumed**, **3 moles of CO₂ are produced**:

$$
\text{RQ} = \frac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ consumed}} = \frac{3}{2.85} = 1.05
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** RQ for aerobic growth on glucose is **slightly above 1.0** because a small fraction of the glucose carbon is incorporated into the more reduced biomass (γ = 4.2 > γ_glucose = 4), requiring extra O₂ uptake beyond that needed for full oxidation to CO₂.

**Step 3 — Calculate CO₂ evolution rate:**

Given $r_{O_2} = 10$ mmol/L·h:

$$
r_{CO_2} = \text{RQ} \times r_{O_2} = 1.05 \times 10 = 10.5 \;\text{mmol/L·h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;r_{CO_2} = 10.5 \;\text{mmol/L·h}\;}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the O₂ term in the oxygen balance** — students often write $6 = 0.5c + 2d + e$ (forgetting the $+2a$ from O₂) and end up with negative or impossible values. The O₂ coefficient is **unknown** and must be solved via the O balance, not assumed.
> 2. **Sign error in hydrogen balance with NH₃** — forgetting to **add 3b** (hydrogen from ammonia) is a frequent error. The full H balance must include both the substrate H and the NH₃ H contributions.
> 3. **Confusing per-mole and per-C-atom γ** — the formula $\gamma = (4n + a - 2b - 3c)/n$ is per C-atom. Forgetting to **divide by $n$** leads to off-by-a-factor-of-6 errors for glucose.
> 4. **Wrong oxidation state of N** — students often assume N goes from 0 (in NH₃) instead of –3. The correct reference is **N = –3 in NH₃**; hence the **–3c term in γ**.
> 5. **RQ interpretation in anaerobic processes** — for methanogenesis, RQ can be **negative** (e.g., $\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \to \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$: RQ = –1). This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.
> 6. **Biomass molecular weight** — students sometimes use only 12 g/mol (carbon only) instead of the full empirical formula weight (~24.6 g/mol for $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 (Stoichiometry of Cell Growth):**

- **General aerobic growth equation** must include **5 terms**: substrate + O₂ + NH₃ → biomass + CO₂ + H₂O.
- **Four elemental balances (C, H, O, N)** give 4 equations for 5 unknowns → choose a **basis** (usually 1 mol substrate or set $c$ arbitrarily).
- **Standard biomass formula:** $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$ with $M_X \approx 24.6$ g/mol and $\gamma_X = 4.2$.
- **Degree of reduction (γ)** is the **number of electrons per C-atom**; use formula $\gamma = (4n + a - 2b - 3c)/n$ with reference states C⁺⁴, H⁺¹, O⁻², N⁻³.
- **Reference γ values:** CO₂ = 0; carbohydrates = 4; lipids ~6; proteins ~5.7; CH₄ = 8.
- **Higher γ substrate → higher $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$** (more electrons available for biomass reduction).
- **RQ definition:** RQ = mol CO₂ produced / mol O₂ consumed.
- **Key RQ values:** Glucose = 1.0; Ethanol = 0.67; Methane = 0.5; Oxalic acid = 4.0; Methanogenesis = –1.0.
- **RQ > 1** indicates organic acid or biomass-forming metabolism; **RQ < 1** indicates reduced substrate (lipid/methane).
- **Electron balance** is a powerful **cross-check**: total electrons in substrate (γS × nC) must equal total electrons in O₂ uptake (4 × mol O₂) plus electrons in biomass (γX × mol biomass C).
- **Maximum theoretical yield formula:** $Y_{X/S}^{\max} = \frac{\gamma_S}{\gamma_X} \cdot \frac{M_X}{M_S}$ — but this is an approximation; for exact value, **solve the full elemental balance**.
- **Aerobic Y_{X/S} typical range:** 0.4–0.5 g/g for glucose; 0.6–0.7 g/g for methane; **never exceed the theoretical maximum**.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme exam focus:** expect **14-mark derivation questions** on elemental balance + γ calculation, and **3-mark definition** questions on γ and RQ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yield Factors (YX/S, YP/S) and Maximum Possible Yield
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Yield Factors (YX/S, YP/S) and Maximum Possible Yield # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition In bioprocess engineering, **yield factors (also called yield coefficients)** are dimensionless or mass-based ratios that quantitatively express the efficiency with which a microbial culture converts a substrate (carbon/energy source) into desired outputs such as **biomass (X), product (P), or carbon dioxide (CO₂)**. They form the quantitative backbone of **material balances** in bioreactor design, scale-up, and economic analysis. The three most frequently used yield factors in KTU-level problems are: $$ Y_{X/S} \;=\; \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} \quad \text{[g cells produced / g substrate consumed]} $$ $$ Y_{P/S} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} \quad \text{[g product formed / g substrate consumed]} $$ $$ Y_{X/P} \;=\; \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta P} \quad \text{[g cells produced / g product formed]} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Although "yield" is dimensionless, in practice it is **NOT** a true dimensionless number — it carries **g/g** units because biomass, product, and substrate have different molecular weights and chemical structures. The **Maximum Possible Yield (Y_max or σ)** is the *theoretical upper limit* of $Y_{X/S}$ predicted purely from **stoichiometry and thermodynamics** (degree-of-reduction balance and available Gibbs free energy), assuming that **no substrate is wasted on maintenance metabolism, overflow metabolites, or reverse reactions**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you run a bakery 🍞. You buy **flour (substrate S)** and produce **bread loaves (biomass X)** and **leftover crumbs (product P, or waste)**. - **$Y_{X/S}$** = (loaves of bread sold) ÷ (kg of flour used). A skilled baker with an efficient oven will have a **high $Y_{X/S}$**. - **$Y_{P/S}$** = (kg of crumbs wasted) ÷ (kg of flour used). More waste ⇒ higher $Y_{P/S}$. - **Maximum Possible Yield (Y_max)** = what a *perfect theoretical baker* with zero waste, zero oven heat loss, and perfect chemistry would achieve. No real baker reaches this — but knowing it tells you **how inefficient your real process is**. In microbiology, the "flour" is usually **glucose**, the "bread" is **cell mass**, and the "waste" can be **ethanol, lactic acid, CO₂, or unused substrate**. The cleaner the metabolism, the closer $Y_{X/S}$ approaches Y_max. --- ## 1.3 Why Yield Factors Matter in Bioprocess Calculations | Engineering Need | Role of Yield Factor | |---|---| | **Reactor Sizing** | Predicting final biomass concentration from initial substrate load | | **Media Optimization** | Quantifying substrate-to-product conversion efficiency | | **Economic Viability** | Calculating raw-material cost per kg of product | | **Strain Comparison** | Benchmarking recombinant vs. wild-type strains | | **Scale-up** | Translating shake-flask yields to industrial fermenters | | **Process Diagnostics** | Detecting contamination or metabolic shifts (yield drops) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Constants (KTU Accepted Values):** > - **Y_max for yeast on glucose (aerobic, no ethanol)** ≈ **0.50 g/g** (range 0.40–0.55 g/g) > - **Y_max for yeast on glucose (anaerobic, ethanol pathway)** ≈ **0.11 g/g** > - **Theoretical $Y_{P/S}$ for ethanol from glucose (Gay-Lussac)** = **0.511 g/g** (≈ 0.51 g ethanol / g glucose) > - **Average biomass molecular formula** = **CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂** > - **Degree of reduction of glucose** = **24** (per C-mole basis = 4) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Yields — Unpacked ### 2.1.1 Cell Mass Yield on Substrate ($Y_{X/S}$) $$ Y_{X/S} \;=\; \frac{X_{final} - X_{initial}}{S_{initial} - S_{final}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} $$ - **Sign convention:** Always taken as **positive** (substrate is consumed ⇒ $\Delta S < 0$, biomass produced ⇒ $\Delta X > 0$). - **Physical meaning:** Mass of new cells created per mass of substrate oxidized. - **Typical range (KTU textbook):** 0.1 – 0.6 g/g depending on carbon source, aeration, and organism. ### 2.1.2 Product Yield on Substrate ($Y_{P/S}$) $$ Y_{P/S} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} $$ - Used when the *product* (not biomass) is the desired output — e.g., ethanol, lactic acid, penicillin, monoclonal antibodies. - Often called **product coefficient** or **substrate-to-product conversion factor**. ### 2.1.3 Cell Yield on Product ($Y_{X/P}$) $$ Y_{X/P} \;=\; \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta P} $$ - Used in **growth-associated product formation** (e.g., lactic acid by *Lactobacillus*). It links biomass formation to product formation stoichiometrically. --- ## 2.2 The True vs. Observed Yield Distinction This is a **high-frequency KTU question** concept: | Yield Type | Symbol | What it Assumes | |---|---|---| | **Observed (apparent) yield** | $Y_{X/S,obs}$ | Net $\Delta X / \Delta S$ measured experimentally; includes maintenance losses | | **True growth yield** | $Y_{X/S,true}$ or $Y_G$ | Yield corrected for substrate used purely for **maintenance** | | **Maximum theoretical yield** | $Y_{max}$ or σ | Stoichiometric ceiling based on **degree of reduction balance** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The hierarchy is always:** $Y_{max} \;\geq\; Y_{true} \;\geq\; Y_{obs}$ --- ## 2.3 Pirt's Maintenance Equation (Theoretical Backbone) Pirt (1965) showed that the *specific* substrate consumption rate ($q_S$, g S / g X · h) has two components: $$ q_{S} \;=\; \frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S,true}} \;+\; m_{S} $$ Where: - $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹) - $Y_{X/S,true}$ = **true** biomass yield (g/g) — *no maintenance losses* - $m_S$ = **maintenance coefficient** (g S / g X · h) — substrate burnt just to keep cells alive Rearranging gives the **classic linear Pirt plot** form: $$ \frac{1}{Y_{X/S,obs}} \;=\; \frac{1}{Y_{X/S,true}} \;+\; \frac{m_{S}}{\mu} $$ **How to use it (KTU trick):** Plot $1/Y_{obs}$ vs $1/\mu$ → straight line with **slope = $m_S$** and **y-intercept = $1/Y_{true}$**. --- ## 2.4 Luedeking–Piret Equation (for Product Formation) $$ q_{P} \;=\; \alpha\,\mu \;+\; \beta $$ - $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation constant (g P / g X) - $\beta$ = non-growth-associated constant (g P / g X · h) - If $\alpha > 0$ and $\beta = 0$ ⇒ product is **purely growth-associated** - If $\alpha = 0$ and $\beta > 0$ ⇒ product is **purely non-growth-associated** (e.g., secondary metabolites like penicillin) --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 — Memorize These** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / \Delta S$ | Cell yield on substrate | g/g | | 2 | $Y_{P/S} = \Delta P / \Delta S$ | Product yield on substrate | g/g | | 3 | $Y_{X/P} = \Delta X / \Delta P$ | Cell yield on product | g/g | | 4 | $Y_{max} = \dfrac{0.375 \cdot R_a}{R_b}$ (c-mole) | Max yield via degree of reduction | g/g | | 5 | $Y_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu_{max}}$ relation | $\mu_{max} = Y_{max} \cdot q_{S,max}$ | — | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{Y_{obs}} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{true}} + \dfrac{m_S}{\mu}$ | Pirt maintenance equation | — | | 7 | $q_{S} = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{true}} + m_{S}$ | Specific substrate uptake | g S / g X · h | | 8 | $q_{P} = \alpha\mu + \beta$ | Luedeking–Piret | g P / g X · h | | 9 | $\Delta S = \dfrac{\Delta X}{Y_{X/S,true}} + m_{S} \cdot X \cdot \Delta t$ | Substrate balance (batch) | g/L | | 10 | $Y_{P/S,theo} = 0.511$ (ethanol/glucose) | Gay-Lussac stoichiometry | g/g | > **Notational Note:** In the table, all vertical bars (e.g., $1/Y_{obs}$) are LaTeX absolute-value constructs and are intentionally written without `$\vert$` wrappers to keep the markdown table compact. In your own answer sheets, use **slashes** like *1/Y* or **negation lines** for clarity. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial ethanol plants** use $Y_{P/S}$ to size fermenters: if demand is 50,000 L ethanol/day and $Y_{P/S} = 0.45$, you need **≈ 100,000 kg glucose/day**. - **Single-cell protein (SCP)** plants use $Y_{X/S}$ to size media prep: with $Y_{X/S} = 0.5$ and target 30 g/L biomass, you need **60 g/L glucose in feed**. - **Biopharmaceutical manufacturers** use Luedeking–Piret $\alpha, \beta$ to predict antibody titers at any given growth rate. - **Pirt's equation** is the *workhorse* of every fed-batch control loop — it tells you exactly when to switch from batch to fed-batch mode. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Possible Yield from Degree of Reduction The **maximum possible yield ($Y_{max}$)** is derived from two fundamental balances: ### Step 1 — Degree of Reduction (γ) of Substrate and Biomass The **degree of reduction** of a compound CₙHₐOᵦNᵧ is the number of electrons transferred per carbon atom when the compound is fully oxidized to CO₂, H₂O, and NH₃: $$ \gamma_{substrate} = \frac{(4n) + a - 2b - 3c}{n} \quad \text{[per C-atom]} $$ **For glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, n=6, a=12, b=6, c=0):** $$ \gamma_{glucose} = \frac{(4 \times 6) + 12 - 2(6) - 3(0)}{6} = \frac{24 + 12 - 12 - 0}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 $$ **For biomass (CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂, n=1, a=1.8, b=0.5, c=0.2):** $$ \gamma_{biomass} = \frac{(4)(1) + 1.8 - 2(0.5) - 3(0.2)}{1} = 4 + 1.8 - 1.0 - 0.6 = 4.2 $$ ### Step 2 — Carbon and Electron Balance The substrate's available electrons are split between: 1. **Biomass synthesis** (fraction $f_S$) 2. **Energy generation** via respiration (fraction $1 - f_S$) For a C-mole basis, the theoretical maximum biomass produced per C-mole of substrate is: $$ Y_{SX,max} = \frac{\gamma_S}{\gamma_X} \quad \text{[C-mol biomass / C-mol substrate]} $$ Substituting γ values: $$ Y_{SX,max} = \frac{\gamma_{glucose}}{\gamma_{biomass}} = \frac{4.0}{4.2} = 0.952 \;\; \text{C-mol X / C-mol S} $$ ### Step 3 — Convert C-mole Basis to Mass Basis Using molecular weights: - MW of biomass (CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂) ≈ 12 + 1.8 + 8 + 2.8 = **24.6 g/C-mol** - MW of glucose = **180 g/mol = 30 g/C-mol** (since 6 carbons) $$ Y_{max} = Y_{SX,max} \times \frac{MW_{biomass}}{MW_{glucose, per\;C-mol}} = 0.952 \times \frac{24.6}{30} $$ $$ Y_{max} = 0.952 \times 0.820 = 0.781 \;\; \text{g biomass / g glucose} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Theoretical ceiling ≈ 0.78 g/g** for a hypothetical cell where **every electron** goes to biomass. **Real aerobic yeast** reaches only **~0.50 g/g** because ~30–40% of electrons are lost to CO₂ during oxidative phosphorylation. This gap is the *energy tax* of life. ### Step 4 — Refined Maximum Yield (Herbert's Energy-Coupled Model) Including the energy dissipation factor (η ≈ 0.4 to 0.6 for aerobic cells): $$ Y_{max,real} = \frac{Y_{SX,max}}{1 + \eta^{-1}} \quad \text{where} \quad \eta = \frac{\text{electrons to biomass}}{\text{electrons to energy}} $$ For yeast (η ≈ 0.6): $$ Y_{max,real} = 0.952 \times 0.6 = 0.571 \;\; \text{g/g} $$ This matches the experimentally observed **Y_max ≈ 0.50–0.55 g/g** for *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* on glucose. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Pirt's Equation from First Principles ### Step 1 — Define Specific Substrate Uptake Rate $$ q_S = -\frac{1}{X}\frac{dS}{dt} \quad \text{[g S / g X · h]} $$ ### Step 2 — Split Substrate Usage into Two Streams Substrate is consumed for **two distinct purposes**: 1. **Growth** — substrate $\rightarrow$ new biomass, rate = $\mu / Y_{true}$ 2. **Maintenance** — substrate oxidized purely for cell upkeep, rate = $m_S$ $$ q_S = \underbrace{\frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S,true}}}_{\text{growth term}} + \underbrace{m_S}_{\text{maintenance term}} $$ ### Step 3 — Mass Balance on Biomass In exponential phase: $dX/dt = \mu X$, and substrate consumed for growth is $-\Delta S_g = \Delta X / Y_{true}$. ### Step 4 — Solve for Observed Yield Total substrate consumed per unit biomass produced: $$ \frac{1}{Y_{obs}} = \frac{\Delta S_{total}}{\Delta X} = \frac{1}{Y_{true}} + \frac{m_S}{\mu} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation:** As $\mu \to \infty$ (very fast growth), the maintenance term $m_S/\mu \to 0$, and $Y_{obs} \to Y_{true}$. As $\mu \to 0$ (slow growth / stationary phase), most substrate is burnt for maintenance, so $Y_{obs} \to 0$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Gay-Lussac Stoichiometry for Ethanol **Problem:** Calculate the maximum theoretical $Y_{P/S}$ for ethanol production from glucose. **Reaction (Gay-Lussac, 1815):** $$ C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH \;+\; 2\,CO_2 $$ **Molar masses:** - Glucose = 180 g/mol - Ethanol = 46 g/mol **Step 1 — Moles of ethanol per mole of glucose:** $$ 2 \text{ mol ethanol / 1 mol glucose} $$ **Step 2 — Mass of ethanol per mass of glucose:** $$ Y_{P/S,theo} = \frac{2 \times 46}{1 \times 180} = \frac{92}{180} $$ $$ Y_{P/S,theo} = 0.5111 \;\; \text{g ethanol / g glucose} $$ **Step 3 — Compare to actual industrial $Y_{P/S}$:** | Process | $Y_{P/S}$ (g/g) | % of Theoretical | |---|---|---| | Gay-Lussac theoretical max | **0.511** | 100% | | Industrial yeast fermentation | 0.40 – 0.46 | 78 – 90% | | Genetically engineered *Z. mobilis* | 0.48 – 0.50 | 94 – 98% | The shortfall is due to **biomass synthesis** (5%), **maintenance** (3%), and **by-products** (glycerol, acetic acid). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Yield | Situation | Use This Yield | Key Insight | |---|---|---| | Designing a biomass (SCP) process | $Y_{X/S}$ | Maximize by minimizing $m_S$ | | Designing an ethanol/lactic acid plant | $Y_{P/S}$ | Maximize by decoupling growth from production | | Decoupling growth & production phases | $Y_{X/P}$ and Luedeking–Piret | Identifies growth-associated product | | Estimating maintenance cost | Pirt's $m_S$ | Slope of $1/Y$ vs $1/\mu$ | | Setting an absolute upper bound | $Y_{max}$ (degree of reduction) | Theoretical ceiling — sanity check | | Fed-batch control | $q_S$ balance | Real-time substrate feed rate | | Strain selection screening | All three $Y$ values | High $Y$ across all = best strain | # 4.
