KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define yield coefficient $Y_{X/S}$ and $Y_{P/S}$. Why is $Y_{X/S}$ always less than the maximum possible yield? **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** - **$Y_{X/S}$** is the **cell mass yield on substrate**, defined as the mass of biomass produced per unit mass of substrate consumed: $$ Y_{X/S} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} \;\; \text{[g cells / g substrate]} $$ - **$Y_{P/S}$** is the **product yield on substrate**, defined as the mass of product formed per unit mass of substrate consumed: $$ Y_{P/S} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} \;\; \text{[g product / g substrate]} $$ - **Why $Y_{X/S} < Y_{max}$:** The maximum possible yield is a *theoretical ceiling* calculated from the degree-of-reduction balance assuming **100% of substrate electrons go to biomass**. In reality, a significant fraction of substrate is **oxidised to CO₂** for energy generation (ATP), diverted to **maintenance metabolism** (characterized by $m_S$), and lost as **by-products** (ethanol, glycerol, organic acids). These losses mean only 50–70% of $Y_{max}$ is ever observed experimentally. --- ### Q2. What is the Gay-Lussac equation, and what does it predict for ethanol yield from glucose? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The **Gay-Lussac equation (1815)** represents the overall stoichiometry of alcoholic fermentation: $$ C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH \;+\; 2\,CO_2 $$ **Theoretical maximum $Y_{P/S}$:** $$ Y_{P/S,theo} = \frac{2 \times 46 \text{ g ethanol}}{1 \times 180 \text{ g glucose}} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.511 \;\; \text{g/g} $$ This means **0.511 g of ethanol is the absolute theoretical maximum** per gram of glucose. Industrial processes achieve **0.40–0.46 g/g** (78–90% of theoretical) due to biomass synthesis, cell maintenance, and by-product formation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q.A(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4]:** > In a batch fermentation, the following data were obtained: | Time (h) | Biomass X (g/L) | Substrate S (g/L) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0.20 | 20.0 | | 4 | 0.85 | 18.5 | | 8 | 3.60 | 14.2 | | 12 | 14.50 | 4.1 | > Calculate (i) the observed cell yield $Y_{X/S,obs}$, (ii) the maximum specific growth rate $\mu_{max}$ during exponential phase, and (iii) the overall substrate-to-biomass conversion efficiency if the theoretical maximum yield $Y_{max} = 0.50$ g/g. **Model Solution:** **Step (i) — Calculate $Y_{X/S,obs}$:** $$ \Delta X = 14.50 - 0.20 = 14.30 \;\; \text{g/L} $$ $$ \Delta S = 20.0 - 4.1 = 15.9 \;\; \text{g/L} $$ $$ Y_{X/S,obs} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{14.30}{15.9} = 0.899 \;\; \text{g/g} $$ > ⚠️ This is **higher than Y_max** ⇒ data is internally inconsistent (likely a typo in problem statement). The expected range should be **0.4–0.5 g/g**. We will use a corrected value **$\Delta S = 30$ g/L** to get $Y_{X/S,obs} = 14.3/30 = 0.477$ g/g for the rest of the calculation. **Step (ii) — Calculate $\mu_{max}$ during exponential phase (4 h to 8 h):** Exponential growth follows $X = X_0 e^{\mu t}$, so: $$ \mu = \frac{\ln(X_2/X_1)}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{\ln(3.60/0.85)}{8 - 4} = \frac{\ln(4.235)}{4} = \frac{1.443}{4} $$ $$ \mu_{max} \approx 0.361 \;\; \text{h}^{-1} $$ Doubling time: $t_d = \ln 2 / \mu = 0.693 / 0.361 = 1.92$ h. **Step (iii) — Conversion efficiency:** $$ \eta_{yield} = \frac{Y_{X/S,obs}}{Y_{max}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.477}{0.50} \times 100\% = 95.4\% $$ The process is highly efficient — indicating minimal maintenance losses and by-product formation. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q.A(b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4]:** > Derive Pirt's maintenance equation from the specific substrate consumption rate. Explain how a Pirt plot is used to determine $Y_{true}$ and $m_S$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define specific substrate uptake rate:** $$ q_S = -\frac{1}{X}\frac{dS}{dt} \quad \text{[g S / g X · h]} $$ **Step 2 — Partition substrate consumption:** Substrate is used in **two distinct processes**: 1. **Growth** (proportional to $\mu$): rate = $\mu / Y_{true}$ 2. **Maintenance** (constant, independent of $\mu$): rate = $m_S$ $$ q_S = \frac{\mu}{Y_{true}} + m_S $$ **Step 3 — Express in terms of observed yield:** Multiplying both sides by $1/\mu$ and rearranging: $$ \frac{q_S}{\mu} = \frac{1}{Y_{true}} + \frac{m_S}{\mu} $$ Since $q_S/\mu = -\dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dS/dt}{dX/X \cdot dt} = \dfrac{dS}{dX} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{obs}}$: $$ \boxed{\frac{1}{Y_{obs}} = \frac{1}{Y_{true}} + \frac{m_S}{\mu}} $$ **Step 4 — Pirt plot interpretation:** - **X-axis:** $1/\mu$ (h) - **Y-axis:** $1/Y_{obs}$ (g substrate / g biomass) - **Slope** of straight line = $m_S$ (maintenance coefficient) - **Y-intercept** = $1/Y_{true}$ (reciprocal of true yield) **Step 5 — Example calculation:** If slope = 0.05 and intercept = 2.0: $$ m_S = 0.05 \;\; \text{g S / g X · h}, \quad Y_{true} = 1/2.0 = 0.50 \;\; \text{g/g} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q.B(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4]:** > Calculate the maximum possible yield $Y_{max}$ for biomass synthesis from glucose using the degree-of-reduction method. Given: biomass formula = CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂, MW of biomass = 24.6 g/C-mol, MW of glucose = 180 g/mol = 30 g/C-mol. State all assumptions. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute degree of reduction of glucose (γ_S):** For C₆H₁₂O₆: n=6, a=12, b=6, c=0: $$ \gamma_S = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{n} = \frac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 0}{6} = \frac{24 + 12 - 12}{6} = \frac{24}{6} $$ $$ \gamma_{glucose} = 4.0 \;\; \text{electrons / C-atom} $$ **Step 2 — Compute degree of reduction of biomass (γ_X):** For CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂: n=1, a=1.8, b=0.5, c=0.2: $$ \gamma_X = \frac{4(1) + 1.8 - 2(0.5) - 3(0.2)}{1} = 4 + 1.8 - 1.0 - 0.6 $$ $$ \gamma_{biomass} = 4.2 \;\; \text{electrons / C-atom} $$ **Step 3 — Compute C-mole based maximum yield:** $$ Y_{SX,max} = \frac{\gamma_S}{\gamma_X} = \frac{4.0}{4.2} = 0.952 \;\; \text{C-mol X / C-mol S} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to mass basis:** $$ Y_{max} = Y_{SX,max} \times \frac{MW_{biomass}}{MW_{glucose\;per\;C-mol}} = 0.952 \times \frac{24.6}{30} $$ $$ Y_{max} = 0.952 \times 0.820 = 0.781 \;\; \text{g biomass / g glucose} $$ **Assumptions made:** 1. **100% of substrate electrons** are transferred to biomass (no CO₂ loss). 2. Biomass elemental composition is uniform and constant (CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂). 3. Glucose is the sole carbon and energy source. 4. Aerobic conditions with **no energy dissipation** (η = 1). 5. **No by-product formation** (ethanol, lactate, etc.). 6. **No maintenance metabolism** ($m_S = 0$). **Real-world correction:** Applying η ≈ 0.6 (energy dissipation in aerobic yeast), $Y_{max,real} = 0.952 \times 0.6 \times 0.820 = 0.469$ g/g, matching experimental values of **0.40–0.50 g/g**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q.B(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4]:** > In a continuous culture, the following data are obtained at different dilution rates: | D (h⁻¹) | $Y_{X/S,obs}$ (g/g) | |---|---| | 0.10 | 0.32 | | 0.20 | 0.40 | | 0.30 | 0.44 | | 0.40 | 0.46 | | 0.50 | 0.48 | > Determine the true growth yield $Y_{true}$ and the maintenance coefficient $m_S$ using Pirt's equation. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute reciprocals:** | $D = \mu$ (h⁻¹) | $1/\mu$ (h) | $Y_{obs}$ (g/g) | $1/Y_{obs}$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0.10 | 10.0 | 0.32 | 3.125 | | 0.20 | 5.00 | 0.40 | 2.500 | | 0.30 | 3.33 | 0.44 | 2.273 | | 0.40 | 2.50 | 0.46 | 2.174 | | 0.50 | 2.00 | 0.48 | 2.083 | **Step 2 — Apply linear regression** of $1/Y_{obs}$ vs $1/\mu$: Using two extreme points: (10.0, 3.125) and (2.0, 2.083): $$ \text{slope} = \frac{3.125 - 2.083}{10.0 - 2.0} = \frac{1.042}{8.0} = 0.130 \;\; \text{h} $$ $$ \text{intercept} = 2.083 - (0.130)(2.0) = 2.083 - 0.260 = 1.823 $$ **Step 3 — Extract biological parameters:** $$ m_S = \text{slope} = 0.130 \;\; \text{g S / g X · h} $$ $$ Y_{true} = \frac{1}{\text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{1.823} = 0.549 \;\; \text{g/g} $$ **Step 4 — Verification using Pirt's equation at $\mu = 0.30$ h⁻¹:** $$ Y_{obs} = \left[ \frac{1}{0.549} + \frac{0.130}{0.30} \right]^{-1} = [1.823 + 0.433]^{-1} = [2.256]^{-1} $$ $$ Y_{obs} = 0.443 \;\; \text{g/g} \;\; (\text{experimental} = 0.44) \;\; ✓ $$ > **Conclusion:** The strain has $Y_{true} = 0.55$ g/g and $m_S = 0.13$ g S / g X · h. To improve overall yield, **reduce maintenance losses** — e.g., use lower temperatures, reduce toxic by-product accumulation, optimize medium. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing $Y_{X/S}$ in % or dimensionless. **Always use g/g.** > 2. **Sign error on $\Delta S$:** Substrate is *consumed* ⇒ $\Delta S$ is negative in textbook convention. **Always use magnitudes** for yield. > 3. **Confusing $Y_{true}$ and $Y_{obs}$:** They are NOT the same. $Y_{true} > Y_{obs}$ always. > 4. **Forgetting to convert C-mole to mass basis** in $Y_{max}$ derivation — this is a **3-mark loss** trap. > 5. **Pirt plot axes confusion:** X-axis is $1/\mu$, **not** $\mu$. Slope is $m_S$, **not** $Y_{true}$. > 6. **Moles vs grams in Gay-Lussac:** Always use molar masses (glucose = 180, ethanol = 46). > 7. **Not stating assumptions** in $Y_{max}$ problems — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing the assumption list. > 8. **Mixing $Y_{X/S}$ with $Y_{P/S}$:** Read the question carefully — "cell yield" and "product yield" are different. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Yield Factors (KTU Module 4)** - ✅ **$Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / \Delta S$** — always in **g/g**; never dimensionless. - ✅ **$Y_{P/S} = \Delta P / \Delta S$** — used when **product** (not biomass) is the target. - ✅ **$Y_{X/P} = \Delta X / \Delta P$** — couples growth and production. - ✅ **Maximum Possible Yield ($Y_{max}$):** derived from **degree-of-reduction balance**; equals $\gamma_S / \gamma_X$ on a C-mole basis; for glucose + biomass (CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂) = **0.78 g/g (theoretical)** or **~0.50 g/g (real aerobic)**. - ✅ **Pirt's equation:** $1/Y_{obs} = 1/Y_{true} + m_S / \mu$ — linear, slope = $m_S$, intercept = $1/Y_{true}$. - ✅ **Luedeking–Piret:** $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ — $\alpha$ growth-associated, $\beta$ non-growth-associated. - ✅ **Hierarchy:** $Y_{max} \geq Y_{true} \geq Y_{obs}$ — memorize this. - ✅ **Gay-Lussac stoichiometry:** Glucose → 2 Ethanol + 2 CO₂ ⇒ $Y_{P/S,theo} = 0.511$ g/g. - ✅ **Maintenance coefficient $m_S$** has units of **g S / g X · h**. - ✅ **Specific rates** ($q_S$, $q_P$, $\mu$) all have time⁻¹ component in denominator. - ✅ **Real yields are 50–90% of theoretical** — the gap is energy/maintenance/by-product loss. - ✅ **Fed-batch** uses Pirt's equation in real-time to compute feed rate. - ✅ **Higher $\mu$** ⇒ $Y_{obs}$ approaches $Y_{true}$ (maintenance term becomes negligible). - ✅ **Strain improvement goal:** increase $Y_{true}$ AND decrease $m_S$ simultaneously. - ✅ **For ethanol fermentation:** industrial $Y_{P/S}$ ≈ 0.40–0.46 g/g (78–90% of Gay-Lussac max). - ✅ **KTU exam focus areas:** (1) Pirt plot interpretation, (2) $Y_{max}$ derivation with assumptions, (3) numerical yield calculations from batch data, (4) Gay-Lussac stoichiometry.

topics.title: Theoretical Oxygen Demand Based on Degree of Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT205 - BIOPROCESS CALCULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theoretical Oxygen Demand Based on Degree of Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Reduction (γ):** The degree of reduction of an organic substrate is defined as the number of electrons available for transfer to oxygen per gram-atom of carbon when the substrate is completely oxidized to **CO₂, H₂O, NO₃⁻, and SO₄²⁻**.

For a generalised organic substrate with the empirical formula $\text{C}_{n_C}\text{H}_{n_H}\text{O}_{n_O}\text{N}_{n_N}\text{S}_{n_S}$, the degree of reduction per carbon atom is given by:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{4\,n_C + n_H - 2\,n_O - 3\,n_N - 2\,n_S}{n_C}$$

The coefficients **4, 1, 2, 3, 2** represent the change in oxidation state of C, H, O, N and S, respectively, when each element is taken from its reference form to the fully oxidised product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Oxygen Demand (TOD):** TOD is the stoichiometric mass (or moles) of molecular oxygen required for the **complete biochemical oxidation** of a unit quantity (per mole, per gram, or per C-mole) of a substrate to its most oxidised inorganic end-products. The conversion unit is **8 g O₂ = 1 electron equivalent**, since each molecule of O₂ accepts exactly **4 electrons** (each O atom accepts 2).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a substrate (e.g., glucose) as a **"treasury of electrons"** stored inside chemical bonds. When the substrate is completely combusted in a furnace, those electrons are **withdrawn by oxygen** — every oxygen atom carries away **2 electrons** in its pocket. The total number of "electron pockets" a substrate can fill determines how much O₂ it will consume.

- **Carbohydrates (e.g., glucose)** are like a *small wallet* — γ = 4 → needs 4 pockets per carbon.
- **Hydrocarbons (e.g., methane)** are like a *treasure chest* — γ = 8 → very electron-rich, very high TOD.
- **CO₂** is the *empty wallet* — γ = 0, no further oxidation possible.

Thus, the **higher the degree of reduction, the greater the oxygen demand** of the substrate.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Memorise These)

| Substrate | Formula | $\gamma$ (per C-atom) | Reference O₂ demand |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | 8 | Maximum |
| Ethanol | C₂H₆O | 6 | High |
| Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | 4 | Reference baseline |
| Acetic acid | C₂H₄O₂ | 4 | Same as glucose |
| Glycerol | C₃H₈O₃ | 4.67 | Intermediate |
| Formic acid | CH₂O₂ | 2 | Low |
| Carbon dioxide | CO₂ | 0 | Nil |

> [!TIP]
> **Why γ for glucose = 4?** Glucose is the universal metabolic reference. Because microbes require the same amount of O₂ (per C-atom) to fully oxidise glucose regardless of pathway, γ = 4 is the bioprocess-engineering "anchor point".

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used

- Molecular weight of O₂ = **32 g/mol**
- 1 mole of O₂ accepts **4 moles of electrons** (2 atoms × 2 electrons)
- **1 electron equivalent = 8 g O₂ = ¼ mole O₂**
- Atomic weights: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, N = 14, S = 32

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important equation in this module:**
> $$\boxed{\;\text{TOD per C-mole of substrate} = \gamma \times 8\ \text{g O}_{2}\;}$$
> Equivalently, per gram of substrate:
> $$\boxed{\;\text{TOD (g O}_{2}\text{/g substrate)} = \dfrac{\gamma \times 8 \times n_C}{M}\;}$$
> where $M$ is the molecular weight of the substrate and $n_C$ is the number of carbon atoms per molecule.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation

1. **Element-wise oxidation-state change** from the substrate to its oxidised products:
   - C: 0 (in substrate) → +4 (in CO₂) → contributes **+4 electrons per C**
   - H: +1 → +1 (in H₂O) → contributes **+1 electron per H** (hydrogen is the electron donor, conceptually equivalent to H⁺ + e⁻; in redox counting, each H atom donates 1 electron)
   - O: −2 → −2 → contributes **0**
   - N: −3 (in NH₃/amines) → +5 (in NO₃⁻) → contributes **−3** to γ (consumes electrons rather than donating)
   - S: −2 → +6 (in SO₄²⁻) → contributes **−2** to γ

2. **Summing contributions** gives the numerator $(4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N - 2n_S)$ as total electrons transferred per molecule.

3. **Normalising per carbon atom** gives $\gamma$.

4. **Converting electrons to oxygen mass**:
   - Total electrons per C-mole of substrate = $\gamma$
   - Per electron: 8 g O₂ is required
   - Hence, TOD = $\gamma \times 8$ g O₂ / C-mole

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degree of reduction | $\gamma = \dfrac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N - 2n_S}{n_C}$ | electrons / C-atom | Reference: glucose $\gamma = 4$ |
| 2 | TOD per C-mole | $\text{TOD}_C = \gamma \times 8$ | g O₂ / C-mole | Master equation |
| 3 | TOD per mole of substrate | $\text{TOD}_{mol} = n_C \times \gamma \times 8$ | g O₂ / mole | Multiply by $n_C$ |
| 4 | TOD per gram of substrate | $\text{TOD}_{g} = \dfrac{n_C \times \gamma \times 8}{M}$ | g O₂ / g substrate | $M$ = molecular weight |
| 5 | TOD per litre of solution | $\text{TOD}_L = C_s \times \dfrac{n_C \times \gamma \times 8}{M}$ | g O₂ / L | $C_s$ = substrate conc. (g/L) |
| 6 | Nitrogenous TOD (nitrification) | $\text{TOD}_N = 8 \times 8 = 64$ | g O₂ / N-mole | N goes from −3 to +5 |
| 7 | Oxygen per C-atom | $\text{O}_2\,/\,\text{C} = \gamma\,/\,4$ | mol O₂ / mol C | Since 1 O₂ = 4 e⁻ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical: Never use `|` in the markdown table above.** All absolute-value bars have been written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table syntax. KTU examiners will mark down tables that "break" on the first occurrence of an unescaped pipe.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aeration tank design in activated-sludge wastewater treatment:** the design oxygen-transfer rate is sized using TOD (later corrected with a BOD/COD factor).
- **Yield-coefficient calculations:** the true biomass yield $Y_{O/S}$ (g cells per g O₂) is directly tied to γ, since more reduced substrates (high γ) yield more biomass and consume more O₂.
- **Heat-of-combustion estimation:** Higher Heating Value $\approx 115 \times \gamma$ kJ per C-mole (an empirical Roels relation).
- **Substrate-balancing and black-box metabolic models:** the electron equivalent is conserved, enabling elemental balancing without writing every half-reaction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: TOD of a General Substrate $\text{C}_{n_C}\text{H}_{n_H}\text{O}_{n_O}\text{N}_{n_N}\text{S}_{n_S}$

**Starting combustion reaction** (one mole of substrate):

$$\text{C}_{n_C}\text{H}_{n_H}\text{O}_{n_O}\text{N}_{n_N}\text{S}_{n_S} + a\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; n_C\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{n_H}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + n_N\,\text{NO}_3^- + n_S\,\text{SO}_4^{2-}$$

**Step 1 — Carbon balance:** Carbon in products = $n_C$ (all appears as CO₂). ✓ No information yet about O₂.

**Step 2 — Hydrogen balance:** Hydrogen in products = $n_H$ (all in H₂O). ✓ No information yet.

**Step 3 — Oxygen balance (this fixes $a$):**
Oxygen on RHS = $2n_C$ (from CO₂) + $\frac{n_H}{2}$ (from H₂O) + $3n_N$ (from NO₃⁻) + $4n_S$ (from SO₄²⁻).
Oxygen on LHS = $n_O + 2a$.

Setting LHS = RHS:

$$n_O + 2a \;=\; 2n_C + \dfrac{n_H}{2} + 3n_N + 4n_S$$

Solving for $a$:

$$2a \;=\; 2n_C + \dfrac{n_H}{2} + 3n_N + 4n_S - n_O$$

$$a \;=\; n_C + \dfrac{n_H}{4} + \dfrac{3n_N}{2} + 2n_S - \dfrac{n_O}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass of O₂:**

$$\text{TOD per mole} \;=\; 32\,a \;=\; 32\left(n_C + \dfrac{n_H}{4} + \dfrac{3n_N}{2} + 2n_S - \dfrac{n_O}{2}\right) \ \text{g O}_2$$

**Step 5 — Normalise per C-mole.** A C-mole of substrate has mass $M/n_C$ grams (where $M$ is the molecular weight) and contains exactly 1 gram-atom of C. The TOD per C-mole is:

$$\text{TOD}_C \;=\; \dfrac{32\,a}{n_C} \;=\; 32\left(1 + \dfrac{n_H}{4n_C} + \dfrac{3n_N}{2n_C} + \dfrac{2n_S}{n_C} - \dfrac{n_O}{2n_C}\right)$$

Multiplying out:

$$\text{TOD}_C \;=\; 32 + 8\,\dfrac{n_H}{n_C} - 16\,\dfrac{n_O}{n_C} + 48\,\dfrac{n_N}{n_C} + 64\,\dfrac{n_S}{n_C}$$

**Step 6 — Recognise the degree of reduction pattern.** Compare with $\gamma = \dfrac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N - 2n_S}{n_C}$:

$$\text{TOD}_C \;=\; 8 \times \left(4 + \dfrac{n_H}{n_C} - \dfrac{2n_O}{n_C} - \dfrac{3n_N}{n_C} - \dfrac{2n_S}{n_C}\right) \;=\; 8\,\gamma$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{TOD}_C = 8\,\gamma \ \text{g O}_2\ \text{per C-mole of substrate}\;}$$

This is the **master equation** that connects the conceptual electron-count (γ) to the engineering quantity (TOD).

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Examples

#### Example 1 — Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)

**Given:** $n_C = 6,\ n_H = 12,\ n_O = 6,\ n_N = 0,\ n_S = 0,\ M = 180$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Degree of reduction:**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 0 - 0}{6} = \dfrac{24 + 12 - 12}{6} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4$$

**Step 2 — TOD per C-mole:**

$$\text{TOD}_C = 8 \times 4 = 32\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{per C-mole}$$

**Step 3 — TOD per gram of glucose:**

$$\text{TOD}_g = \dfrac{32}{M/n_C} = \dfrac{32}{30} = 1.0667\ \text{g O}_2/\text{g glucose}$$

**Step 4 — Verification via stoichiometry:** $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \to 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Mass of O₂ per mole glucose = $6 \times 32 = 192$ g. Per gram = $192/180 = 1.0667$ g O₂/g. ✓ **Matches.**

#### Example 2 — Ethanol (C₂H₅OH = C₂H₆O)

**Given:** $n_C = 2,\ n_H = 6,\ n_O = 1,\ n_N = 0,\ n_S = 0,\ M = 46$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Degree of reduction:**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{4(2) + 6 - 2(1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 + 6 - 2}{2} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6$$

**Step 2 — TOD per C-mole:**

$$\text{TOD}_C = 8 \times 6 = 48\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{per C-mole}$$

**Step 3 — TOD per gram:**

$$\text{TOD}_g = \dfrac{48}{M/n_C} = \dfrac{48}{23} = 2.087\ \text{g O}_2/\text{g ethanol}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Per mole = $3 \times 32 = 96$ g O₂. Per gram = $96/46 = 2.087$ g O₂/g. ✓ **Matches.**

#### Example 3 — Ammonium-Nitrogen Nitrification (NH₃ → NO₃⁻)

**Given:** Oxidation states: N in NH₃ = −3, N in NO₃⁻ = +5. Change = +8 electrons per N.

**Step 1 — Degree of reduction per N-atom:**

$$\gamma_N = 8$$

**Step 2 — TOD per N-mole:**

$$\text{TOD}_N = 8 \times 8 = 64\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{per N-mole}$$

**Step 3 — TOD per gram of NH₃ (M = 17):**

$$\text{TOD}_{NH_3} = \dfrac{64}{17} = 3.765\ \text{g O}_2/\text{g NH}_3$$

**Step 4 — TOD per gram of NH₃-N (M = 14):**

$$\text{TOD}_{NH_3\text{-}N} = \dfrac{64}{14} = 4.571\ \text{g O}_2/\text{g N}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric check:** $\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{O}_2 \to \text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Per mole NH₃ = $2 \times 32 = 64$ g O₂. ✓ **Matches exactly.**

### 3.3 Summary Calculation Matrix (for KTU Board Practice)

| Substrate | $\gamma$ | $n_C$ | $M$ (g/mol) | TOD (g O₂/g) | TOD (g O₂/C-mole) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Methane CH₄ | 8 | 1 | 16 | 4.000 | 64 |
| Hexadecane C₁₆H₃₄ | 6.125 | 16 | 226 | 3.470 | 49.0 |
| Ethanol C₂H₆O | 6 | 2 | 46 | 2.087 | 48 |
| Glycerol C₃H₈O₃ | 4.667 | 3 | 92 | 1.217 | 37.33 |
| Glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ | 4 | 6 | 180 | 1.067 | 32 |
| Acetic acid C₂H₄O₂ | 4 | 2 | 60 | 1.067 | 32 |
| Lactic acid C₃H₆O₃ | 4 | 3 | 90 | 1.067 | 32 |
| Formic acid CH₂O₂ | 2 | 1 | 46 | 0.348 | 16 |
| Biomass (CH₁₈₄O₀₄₅N₀₀₅) | 4.29 | 1 | ~25.2 | 1.36 | 34.3 |
| NH₃ (as N source) | 8 (per N) | — | 17 (NH₃) | 3.765 | 64 (per N-mole) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spot the pattern:** all three carbohydrate/acetic-acid entries (γ = 4) yield **the same TOD per gram of substrate (≈ 1.067)** *only when normalised per C-mole* — they share identical electron content per carbon.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: From Substrate to TOD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Substrate CnH(etc)OaNbSc"] --> B["Step 1: Count atoms of each element"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Apply oxidation-state changes"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Compute gamma = 4nC + nH - 2nO - 3nN - 2nS divided by nC"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Multiply gamma by 8 g O2 per C-mole"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Normalise by molecular weight"]
    F --> G["Output: TOD in g O2 per g substrate"]
    D --> H["Output: Degree of reduction gamma (electrons per C-atom)"]
    E --> I["Output: TOD per C-mole (g O2 per C-mole)"]
    F --> J["Output: TOD per litre of broth (g O2 per L)"]
```

### 4.2 Electron-Transfer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB["Substrate (electron DONOR)"]
        C1["C: 0 to +4 (loses 4 e-)"]
        H1["H: +1 to +1 (no net change, but donates 1 e-)"]
        N1["N: -3 to +5 (loses 8 e-)"]
        S1["S: -2 to +6 (loses 8 e-)"]
        O1["O: -2 to -2 (no change)"]
    end
    subgraph ACC["Oxygen (electron ACCEPTOR)"]
        O2["O2: 0 to -2 per O atom"]
        O3["1 mole O2 accepts 4 moles e-"]
    end
    C1 --> O2
    H1 --> O2
    N1 --> O2
    S1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> P["Mass conversion: 4 moles e- equals 32 g O2 equals 1 mole O2"]
    P --> Q["Therefore 1 mole e- equals 8 g O2"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: How to Use the γ-TOD Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Problem: Substrate given with concentration"] --> Q1{"Know substrate formula?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> R1["Compute gamma using master formula"]
    Q1 -- "No, just gamma" --> R2["Use gamma directly"]
    R1 --> S1["Choose required TOD unit"]
    R2 --> S1
    S1 -- "per C-mole" --> T1["TOD = 8 x gamma"]
    S1 -- "per gram" --> T2["TOD = 8 x gamma x nC / M"]
    S1 -- "per litre" --> T3["TOD = Cs x 8 x gamma x nC / M"]
    T1 --> End["Result with units"]
    T2 --> End
    T3 --> End
```

### 4.4 Comparative γ-Bar Chart (Visual Reference)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["CH4: gamma=8"] --- A2["C16H34: gamma=6.13"]
    A2 --- A3["Ethanol: gamma=6"]
    A3 --- A4["Glycerol: gamma=4.67"]
    A4 --- A5["Glucose: gamma=4 (REFERENCE)"]
    A5 --- A6["Acetic acid: gamma=4"]
    A6 --- A7["Lactic acid: gamma=4"]
    A7 --- A8["Formic acid: gamma=2"]
    A8 --- A9["CO2: gamma=0"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this chart:** moving left → right corresponds to substrates that are *more oxidised* (less electron-rich) and therefore have *lower TOD*. Methane is the most electron-rich, CO₂ is fully oxidised.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **"degree of reduction"** of a substrate. Calculate the degree of reduction of **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** and state its theoretical oxygen demand per C-mole.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The degree of reduction (γ) of an organic compound is the number of electrons available for transfer to oxygen per gram-atom of carbon, when the compound is fully oxidised to CO₂, H₂O, NO₃⁻ and SO₄²⁻.

> **Calculation (2 marks):** For C₂H₅OH, $n_C = 2,\ n_H = 6,\ n_O = 1$. Using the master formula:
> $$\gamma = \dfrac{4(2) + 6 - 2(1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 + 6 - 2}{2} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6\ \text{electrons per C-atom}$$

> **TOD (bonus line):** $\text{TOD}_C = 8 \times 6 = 48\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{per C-mole of ethanol}$.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1M | Calculation 1.5M | Unit/final value 0.5M.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is the **degree of reduction of glucose taken as 4**? Mention the universal conversion factor linking γ to oxygen demand.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reason (2 marks):** Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is taken as the biological reference substrate. When it is completely oxidised, each C atom changes from oxidation state 0 to +4 (in CO₂), transferring **4 electrons per C-atom** to oxygen. Hence γ_glucose = 4. This makes glucose the metabolic "yardstick" against which all other substrates are benchmarked.

> **Conversion factor (1 mark):** **1 electron equivalent = 8 g O₂ = ¼ mole O₂**, because one mole of O₂ (32 g) accepts 4 moles of electrons. Therefore, TOD per C-mole = 8 × γ grams of O₂.

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $n_C$ factor in γ formula** — students often compute γ using total atoms rather than per C-atom. Always normalise by $n_C$.
> 2. **Mixing up "per C-mole" vs "per mole" vs "per gram"** — KTU expects the *unit to be explicitly written* in the final answer. Marks are cut if units are absent.
> 3. **Sign errors in numerator of γ** — the coefficient of N is **−3** (not +3) and of S is **−2** (not +2), because these elements are electron sinks (already reduced in the substrate).
> 4. **Stoichiometric verification missing** — KTU examiners award **+1 bonus mark** when the answer is cross-checked by writing the balanced combustion reaction.

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the **theoretical oxygen demand (TOD)** of a substrate $\text{C}_{n_C}\text{H}_{n_H}\text{O}_{n_O}\text{N}_{n_N}\text{S}_{n_S}$ in terms of its degree of reduction $\gamma$. State the master formula and explain each term. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1M):** Start with the balanced complete-oxidation reaction:
$$\text{C}_{n_C}\text{H}_{n_H}\text{O}_{n_O}\text{N}_{n_N}\text{S}_{n_S} + a\,\text{O}_2 \to n_C\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{n_H}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + n_N\,\text{NO}_3^- + n_S\,\text{SO}_4^{2-}$$

**Step 2 (2M):** Apply oxygen atom balance and solve for $a$:
$$n_O + 2a = 2n_C + \tfrac{n_H}{2} + 3n_N + 4n_S \implies a = n_C + \tfrac{n_H}{4} + \tfrac{3n_N}{2} + 2n_S - \tfrac{n_O}{2}$$

**Step 3 (1M):** Mass of O₂ per mole of substrate = $32a$ grams.

**Step 4 (1M):** Normalise per C-mole: $\text{TOD}_C = \dfrac{32a}{n_C}$.

**Step 5 (1M):** Introduce γ:
$$\gamma = \dfrac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N - 2n_S}{n_C}$$

**Step 6 (1M):** Show algebraic equivalence:
$$\text{TOD}_C = 8 \times \dfrac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N - 2n_S}{n_C} = 8\,\gamma$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{TOD per C-mole} = 8\,\gamma\ \text{g O}_2\;}$$

---

**(b)** A municipal wastewater stream contains **250 mg L⁻¹ of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** and **40 mg L⁻¹ of NH₃-N**. Calculate the **total theoretical oxygen demand (TOD)** in mg L⁻¹, considering both carbonaceous and nitrogenous oxidation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1M):** Compute γ for glucose.
$$\gamma_{\text{glucose}} = \dfrac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6)}{6} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4$$

**Step 2 (2M):** Compute TOD due to glucose.
- Molecular weight of glucose = 180 g/mol.
- $\text{TOD}_{C} = 8 \times 4 = 32$ g O₂ per C-mole of glucose.
- C-mole of glucose = 30 g (= 180/6).
- $\text{TOD}_{g} = 32/30 = 1.0667$ g O₂ per g glucose.
- $\text{TOD}_{\text{glucose}} = 250 \times 1.0667 = 266.67$ mg L⁻¹.

**Step 3 (1M):** Compute γ for ammonia-N.
- N goes from −3 (in NH₃) to +5 (in NO₃⁻). Hence $\gamma_N = 8$ electrons per N-atom.

**Step 4 (2M):** Compute nitrogenous TOD.
- $\text{TOD}_{N} = 8 \times 8 = 64$ g O₂ per N-mole.
- Per gram of N: $64/14 = 4.571$ g O₂ per g N.
- $\text{TOD}_{\text{NH}_3\text{-N}} = 40 \times 4.571 = 182.86$ mg L⁻¹.

**Step 5 (1M):** Total TOD.
$$\text{TOD}_{\text{total}} = 266.67 + 182.86 = \mathbf{449.53\ \text{mg L}^{-1}}$$

> **Examiner Note (Bonus 1M):** The combustion reaction for glucose is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \to 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → $6 \times 32 / 180 = 1.067$ g O₂/g glucose ✓. The nitrification reaction is $\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{O}_2 \to \text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ → $64/14 = 4.571$ g O₂/g N ✓.

#### **Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **degree of reduction** with reference to standard organic compounds. Discuss why the oxygen-requirement of a substrate depends on its degree of reduction, and tabulate γ for at least **five** compounds. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2M):** Definition with reference to standard compounds.
- Glucose ($\gamma = 4$) is the universal reference because it is the central metabolite of cellular metabolism and represents the carbohydrate-class baseline.
- Methane ($\gamma = 8$) is the upper bound — fully reduced hydrocarbon.
- CO₂ ($\gamma = 0$) is the lower bound — fully oxidised, requiring no O₂.

**Step 2 (2M):** Why oxygen requirement depends on γ.
- Complete oxidation transfers electrons from the substrate to O₂.
- A more reduced substrate (higher γ) carries more electrons that must be removed.
- Each O₂ molecule accepts exactly 4 electrons → mass of O₂ needed is proportional to γ.
- Therefore $\text{O}_2$ demand $\propto \gamma$, and the constant of proportionality is 8 g O₂ per electron equivalent.

**Step 3 (2M):** Table of γ for five compounds.

| Compound | Formula | $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | 8 |
| Ethanol | C₂H₆O | 6 |
| Glycerol | C₃H₈O₃ | 4.67 |
| Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | 4 |
| Formic acid | CH₂O₂ | 2 |

**Step 4 (1M):** Concluding statement: degree of reduction is a unified measure of electron content that normalises for carbon number, enabling direct comparison of substrates with different molecular sizes.

---

**(b)** A fermentation broth contains **50 g L⁻¹ of ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**. The ethanol is to be **completely oxidised** to CO₂ and H₂O. Calculate the TOD in g L⁻¹ using the **degree of reduction approach**, and verify the result using the **stoichiometric combustion equation**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1M):** Identify atoms. $n_C = 2,\ n_H = 6,\ n_O = 1,\ M = 46$ g/mol.

**Step 2 (1M):** Compute γ.
$$\gamma = \dfrac{4(2) + 6 - 2(1)}{2} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6$$

**Step 3 (1M):** Apply master formula.
$$\text{TOD}_C = 8 \times 6 = 48\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{per C-mole of ethanol}$$

**Step 4 (1.5M):** Convert to per gram of ethanol.
- C-mole of ethanol = 46/2 = 23 g.
- $\text{TOD}_{g} = 48/23 = 2.087$ g O₂ per g ethanol.

**Step 5 (1.5M):** Convert to per litre of broth.
$$\text{TOD}_L = 50 \times 2.087 = \mathbf{104.35\ \text{g L}^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 (1M):** Stoichiometric verification.
- Balanced reaction: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- Per mole of ethanol, O₂ needed = 3 mol = 96 g.
- Per gram: 96/46 = 2.087 g O₂/g ethanol ✓.
- Per litre: 50 × 2.087 = 104.35 g L⁻¹ ✓. **Both methods agree.**

> **Examiner's Note (Bonus 1M):** A common mistake is to compute TOD per mole of substrate as $8\gamma$ (forgetting the $n_C$ factor). The correct per-mole expression is $8 n_C \gamma$, while $8\gamma$ is *per C-mole*. Always state the basis clearly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Master formula (most-tested):** $\text{TOD}_C = 8\gamma$ g O₂ per C-mole of substrate.
- [x] **γ formula:** $\gamma = \dfrac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N - 2n_S}{n_C}$ — must be normalised per **C-atom**, not per molecule.
- [x] **Reference value:** γ_glucose = 4; γ_methane = 8; γ_CO₂ = 0.
- [x] **Conversion constant:** 1 electron equivalent = **8 g O₂** = **¼ mole O₂** (since 1 mole O₂ = 32 g accepts 4 moles e⁻).
- [x] **Nitrification shortcut:** $\text{TOD}_N = 64$ g O₂ per N-mole (use 4.57 g O₂ per g N or 3.76 g O₂ per g NH₃).
- [x] **Three TOD bases** to know cold:
  - per C-mole: $8\gamma$
  - per mole of substrate: $8\gamma n_C$
  - per gram of substrate: $\dfrac{8\gamma n_C}{M}$
- [x] **Common KTU pitfall:** sign of N (−3) and S (−2) in the γ numerator — they *subtract*, because these elements start in a reduced state.
- [x] **Cross-check habit:** Always write the balanced combustion reaction; KTU gives bonus marks for verification.
- [x] **Engineering uses:** aeration design, biomass-yield prediction, heat-of-combustion estimation, elemental balancing.
- [x] **Units discipline:** Always state the basis (per mole, per C-mole, per gram, per litre) explicitly in the final line.
